## Detailed Analysis

### 1. Non-Pressure Ulcer Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Non-Pressure Ulcer Coding Guidelines with identical behavioral logic and specific code assignment rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `195_198_199_201_202`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assignment of the code for unstageable pressure ulcer (L89.--0) should be based on the clinical documentation. These codes are used for pressure ulcers whose stage cannot be clinically determined (e.g., the ulcer is covered by eschar or has been treated with a skin or muscle graft). This code should not be confused with the codes for unspecified stage (L89.--9). When there is no documentation regarding the stage of the pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified stage (L89.-- 9).
>
> If during an encounter, the stage of an unstageable pressure ulcer is revealed after debridement, assign only the code for the stage revealed following debridement.
>
> Pressure ulcers described as healing should be assigned the appropriate pressure ulcer stage code based on the documentation in the medical record. If the documentation does not provide information about the stage of the healing pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified stage.
>
> If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a current (new) pressure ulcer or if the patient is being treated for a healing pressure ulcer, query the provider.
>
> For ulcers that were present on admission but healed at the time of discharge, assign the code for the site and stage of the pressure ulcer at the time of admission.
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient hospital with a pressure ulcer at one stage and it progresses to a higher stage, two separate codes should be assigned: one code for the site and stage of the ulcer on admission and a second code for the same ulcer site and the highest stage reported during the stay.
>
> No code is assigned if the documentation states that the non-pressure ulcer is completely healed at the time of admission.
>
> Non-pressure ulcers described as healing should be assigned the appropriate non-pressure ulcer code based on the documentation in the medical record. If the documentation does not provide information about the severity of the healing non-pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified severity.
>
> If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a current (new) non-pressure ulcer or if the patient is being treated for a healing non-pressure ulcer, query the provider.
>
> For ulcers that were present on admission but healed at the time of discharge, assign the code for the site and severity of the non-pressure ulcer at the time of admission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Non-Pressure Ulcer Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign unstageable pressure ulcer code (L89.--0) only when stage cannot be clinically determined due to eschar or grafts; use unspecified stage (L89.--9) if stage is not documented.
- If the stage of an unstageable pressure ulcer is revealed after debridement, assign only the code for the stage revealed following debridement.
- For ulcers present on admission but healed at discharge, assign the code for the site and stage/severity at the time of admission.
- If documentation is unclear regarding whether a non-pressure ulcer is current or healing, query the provider.
- No code is assigned if documentation states a non-pressure ulcer is completely healed at the time of admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the stage or severity of the pressure ulcer to assign a specific code.
- Documentation must clarify whether the ulcer is current (new) or healing to avoid misclassification.
- For unstageable ulcers, documentation must confirm the presence of eschar or grafts preventing stage determination.
- If a pressure ulcer progresses to a higher stage during an inpatient stay, separate codes must be assigned for admission stage and highest reported stage.
- If documentation lacks stage or severity information for healing ulcers, assign the unspecified stage or severity code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning unspecified stage codes (L89.--9) when the ulcer is clinically unstageable due to eschar or grafts.
- Assigning codes for non-pressure ulcers that are completely healed at the time of admission.
- Failing to query the provider when documentation is unclear about whether a non-pressure ulcer is current or healing.
- Assigning a single code for a pressure ulcer that progresses to a higher stage during an inpatient stay without coding both stages.
- Using unspecified stage codes when the ulcer is documented as unstageable rather than using the specific unstageable code.

**New in this version:**
- Specific instruction to assign only the code for the stage revealed after debridement for previously unstageable ulcers.
- Mandatory assignment of admission stage/severity codes for ulcers that heal during the encounter.
- Requirement to assign unspecified severity codes for healing non-pressure ulcers when severity is not documented.
- Explicit rule prohibiting code assignment for non-pressure ulcers completely healed at admission.
- Clarification that unstageable codes (L89.--0) are distinct from unspecified stage codes (L89.--9).

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `625_626_627_629_630`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assignment of the code for unstageable pressure ulcer (L89.--0) should be based on the clinical documentation. These codes are used for pressure ulcers whose stage cannot be clinically determined (e.g., the ulcer is covered by eschar or has been treated with a skin or muscle graft). This code should not be confused with the codes for unspecified stage (L89.--9). When there is no documentation regarding the stage of the pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified stage (L89.-- 9).
>
> If during an encounter, the stage of an unstageable pressure ulcer is revealed after debridement, assign only the code for the stage revealed following debridement.
>
> Assignment of the pressure ulcer stage code should be guided by clinical documentation of the stage or documentation of the terms found in the Alphabetic Index. For clinical terms describing the stage that are not found in the Alphabetic Index, and there is no documentation of the stage, the provider should be queried.
>
> - 4) Patients admitted with pressure ulcers documented as healed No code is assigned if the documentation states that the pressure ulcer is completely healed at the time of admission.
>
> Pressure ulcers described as healing should be assigned the appropriate pressure ulcer stage code based on the documentation in the medical record. If the documentation does not provide information about the stage of the healing pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified stage.
>
> If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a current (new) pressure ulcer or if the patient is being treated for a healing pressure ulcer, query the provider.
>
> For ulcers that were present on admission but healed at the time of discharge, assign the code for the site and stage of the pressure ulcer at the time of admission.
>
> - 6) Patient admitted with pressure ulcer evolving into another stage during the admission
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient hospital with a pressure ulcer at one stage and it progresses to a higher stage, two separate codes should be assigned: one code for the site and stage of the ulcer on admission and a second code for the same ulcer site and the highest stage reported during the stay.
>
> No code is assigned if the documentation states that the non-pressure ulcer is completely healed at the time of admission.
>
> Non-pressure ulcers described as healing should be assigned the appropriate non-pressure ulcer code based on the documentation in the medical record. If the documentation does not provide information about the severity of the healing non-pressure ulcer, assign the appropriate code for unspecified severity.
>
> If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a current (new) non-pressure ulcer or if the patient is being treated for a healing non-pressure ulcer, query the provider.
>
> For ulcers that were present on admission but healed at the time of discharge, assign the code for the site and severity of the non-pressure ulcer at the time of admission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Non-Pressure Ulcer Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for unstageable pressure ulcer (L89.--0) only if stage cannot be clinically determined due to eschar or grafts; use unspecified stage (L89.--9) if stage is not documented.
- If stage of an unstageable pressure ulcer is revealed after debridement, assign only the code for the stage revealed following debridement.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding whether a patient has a current (new) non-pressure ulcer or is being treated for a healing non-pressure ulcer.
- For ulcers present on admission but healed at discharge, assign the code for the site and severity at the time of admission.
- If a patient is admitted with a non-pressure ulcer that evolves to a higher stage during admission, assign two separate codes: one for admission stage and one for the highest stage reported.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Assignment of unstageable pressure ulcer codes must be based on clinical documentation indicating the stage cannot be determined.
- No code is assigned if documentation states the non-pressure ulcer is completely healed at the time of admission.
- Healing non-pressure ulcers must be assigned codes based on documented severity; unspecified severity is used if not provided.
- Provider query is mandatory when documentation is unclear about the status (new vs. healing) of a non-pressure ulcer.
- Coding for evolving ulcers requires documentation of both the admission stage and the highest stage reached during the stay.
- Assignment of unstageable pressure ulcer codes must be guided by clinical documentation of the stage or terms found in the Alphabetic Index.
- Provider query is required for clinical terms describing the stage that are not found in the Alphabetic Index when there is no documentation of the stage.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning unspecified stage codes when clinical documentation explicitly indicates the ulcer is unstageable due to eschar or grafts.
- Assigning a single code for a non-pressure ulcer that progresses to a higher stage during an inpatient admission.
- Assigning codes for non-pressure ulcers without querying the provider when the distinction between new and healing ulcers is unclear.
- Assigning codes for non-pressure ulcers that are completely healed at the time of admission.
- Using unspecified severity codes when the medical record provides specific severity documentation for healing non-pressure ulcers.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to assign only the revealed stage code for unstageable ulcers after debridement.
- Mandatory provider query protocol for ambiguous documentation regarding new versus healing non-pressure ulcers.
- Requirement to assign admission-stage codes for non-pressure ulcers that heal before discharge.
- Dual-code assignment rule for non-pressure ulcers evolving to higher stages during inpatient stays.
- Clarification that unspecified stage (L89.--9) applies only when stage is not documented, distinct from unstageable codes.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present identical clinical logic for assigning codes for unstageable ulcers (L89.--0), distinguishing them from unspecified stage codes (L89.--9), and handling cases where ulcers heal during admission. Both versions mandate provider queries for ambiguous documentation and prohibit coding for ulcers completely healed at admission. The differences are purely administrative, such as the newer version explicitly referencing the Alphabetic Index for term lookup and slightly rephrasing the 'healed at admission' rule, without altering the underlying coding behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assignment of the code for unstageable pressure ulcer (L89.--0) should be based on the clinical documentation. -> icd (New Version): Assignment of the code for unstageable pressure ulcer (L89.--0) should be based on the clinical documentation.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a current (new) pressure ulcer or if the patient is being treated for a healing pressure ulcer, query the provider. -> icd (New Version): Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding whether a patient has a current (new) non-pressure ulcer or is being treated for a healing non-pressure ulcer.

*Traceability: Chunk `195_198_199_201_202` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `625_626_627_629_630` (icd (New Version))*

### 2. Uncertain Diagnosis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical 'Uncertain Diagnosis Coding Guidelines' feature with matching behaviors and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `389_397_410`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the diagnosis documented at the time of discharge is qualified as 'probable,' 'suspected,' 'likely,' 'questionable,' 'possible,' or 'still to be ruled out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty, code the condition as if it existed or was established. The bases for these guidelines are the diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup or observation, and initial therapeutic approach that correspond most closely with the established diagnosis.
>
> Note: This guideline is applicable only to inpatient admissions to short-term, acute, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.
>
> If the diagnosis documented at the time of discharge is qualified as 'probable,' 'suspected,' 'likely,' 'questionable,' 'possible,' or 'still to be ruled out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty, code the condition as if it existed or was established. The bases for these guidelines are the diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup or observation, and initial therapeutic approach that correspond most closely with the established diagnosis.
>
> Note: This guideline is applicable only to inpatient admissions to short-term, acute, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.
>
> Do not code diagnoses documented as 'probable', 'suspected,' 'questionable,' 'rule out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or 'working diagnosis' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty. Rather, code the condition(s) to the highest degree of certainty for that encounter/visit, such as symptoms, signs, abnormal test results, or other reason for the visit.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practices used by short-term, acute care, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Uncertain Diagnosis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code uncertain diagnoses (e.g., 'probable', 'suspected') as if they existed or were established for inpatient admissions.
- Basis for coding uncertain diagnoses includes diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup, and initial therapeutic approach.
- Do not code diagnoses documented with uncertainty terms for outpatient encounters; instead, code to the highest degree of certainty (symptoms, signs, abnormal results).
- Apply uncertain diagnosis coding rules only to inpatient admissions in short-term, acute, long-term care, and psychiatric hospitals.
- Differentiate coding practices between inpatient settings (code as established) and outpatient settings (code as symptoms/signs).

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnosis must be qualified by terms like 'probable', 'suspected', 'likely', 'questionable', 'possible', 'still to be ruled out', 'compatible with', or 'consistent with'.
- Setting must be an inpatient admission to a short-term, acute, long-term care, or psychiatric hospital.
- Coding must be supported by diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup, or initial therapeutic approach corresponding to the established diagnosis.
- For non-inpatient encounters, documentation must support coding to the highest degree of certainty (symptoms, signs, abnormal test results).
- Uncertain diagnosis terms must not be used as the primary basis for coding in outpatient encounters.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding uncertain diagnoses as if they were established for outpatient encounters.
- Using uncertain diagnosis terms (e.g., 'probable', 'suspected') as the primary basis for coding in outpatient settings.
- Applying inpatient uncertain diagnosis coding rules to outpatient encounters.
- Coding uncertain diagnoses without supporting diagnostic workup or therapeutic approach in inpatient settings.
- Failing to distinguish between inpatient and outpatient coding practices for uncertain diagnoses.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit clarification that uncertain diagnosis coding applies only to inpatient admissions.
- Mandatory requirement to code uncertain diagnoses as if established based on workup and therapeutic approach.
- Prohibition against coding uncertain diagnoses in outpatient settings; must code symptoms, signs, or abnormal results instead.
- Updated definitions for uncertain diagnosis terms including 'compatible with' and 'consistent with'.
- Emphasis on differentiating coding practices between inpatient and outpatient settings for uncertain diagnoses.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `822_830_843`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the diagnosis documented at the time of discharge is qualified as 'probable,' 'suspected,' 'likely,' 'questionable,' 'possible,' or 'still to be ruled out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty, code the condition as if it existed or was established. The bases for these guidelines are the diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup or observation, and initial therapeutic approach that correspond most closely with the established diagnosis.
>
> Note: This guideline is applicable only to inpatient admissions to short-term, acute, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.
>
> If the diagnosis documented at the time of discharge is qualified as 'probable,' 'suspected,' 'likely,' 'questionable,' 'possible,' or 'still to be ruled out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty, code the condition as if it existed or was established. The bases for these guidelines are the diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup or observation, and initial therapeutic approach that correspond most closely with the established diagnosis.
>
> Note: This guideline is applicable only to inpatient admissions to short-term, acute, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.
>
> Do not code diagnoses documented as 'probable', 'suspected,' 'questionable,' 'rule out,' 'compatible with,' 'consistent with,' or 'working diagnosis' or other similar terms indicating uncertainty. Rather, code the condition(s) to the highest degree of certainty for that encounter/visit, such as symptoms, signs, abnormal test results, or other reason for the visit.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practices used by short-term, acute care, long-term care and psychiatric hospitals.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Uncertain Diagnosis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code uncertain diagnoses (e.g., 'probable', 'suspected') as if they existed or were established for inpatient admissions.
- Basis for coding uncertain diagnoses includes diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup, and initial therapeutic approach.
- Do not code diagnoses documented with uncertainty terms for outpatient encounters; instead, code to the highest degree of certainty (symptoms, signs, abnormal results).
- Apply inpatient coding rules only to short-term, acute, long-term care, and psychiatric hospitals.
- Differentiate coding practices between inpatient settings (code as established) and outpatient settings (code as symptoms/signs).

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnosis must be qualified by terms like 'probable', 'suspected', 'likely', 'questionable', 'possible', 'still to be ruled out', 'compatible with', or 'consistent with'.
- Setting must be an inpatient admission to a short-term, acute, long-term care, or psychiatric hospital.
- Diagnostic workup, arrangements for further workup, or initial therapeutic approach must correspond to the established diagnosis.
- For outpatient visits, code must reflect the highest degree of certainty (symptoms, signs, abnormal test results) rather than the uncertain diagnosis itself.
- Documentation must explicitly indicate uncertainty to trigger the specific coding rule for inpatient settings.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding uncertain diagnoses as if they were established for outpatient encounters.
- Applying inpatient 'code as established' rules to outpatient visits.
- Using uncertain diagnosis codes (e.g., R codes for symptoms) as the primary reason for visit in inpatient settings when the uncertain diagnosis is the focus of care.
- Ignoring the setting (inpatient vs. outpatient) when determining whether to code an uncertain diagnosis as established.
- Coding 'working diagnosis' as a confirmed condition in outpatient settings.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to code uncertain diagnoses as established for inpatient admissions based on therapeutic approach and workup.
- Clarification that outpatient coding for uncertain diagnoses must default to symptoms, signs, or abnormal test results.
- Specific list of uncertainty terms ('probable', 'suspected', 'likely', 'questionable', 'possible', 'still to be ruled out', 'compatible with', 'consistent with', 'working diagnosis').
- Mandatory distinction between inpatient and outpatient coding practices for uncertain diagnoses.
- Requirement to validate the basis of coding (workup, observation, therapy) for uncertain inpatient diagnoses.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical logic: coding uncertain diagnoses as established for inpatient admissions based on workup and therapy, while prohibiting this for outpatient encounters. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating no substantive change in the coding rules themselves. The differences in the provided profiles appear to be administrative clarifications or formatting updates rather than logical shifts in the guidelines.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Applies uncertain diagnosis coding rules only to inpatient admissions in short-term, acute, long-term care, and psychiatric hospitals. -> icd (New Version): Applies uncertain diagnosis coding rules only to inpatient admissions in short-term, acute, long-term care, and psychiatric hospitals.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Prohibits coding diagnoses documented with uncertainty terms for outpatient encounters; code to highest degree of certainty. -> icd (New Version): Prohibits coding diagnoses documented with uncertainty terms for outpatient encounters; code to highest degree of certainty.

*Traceability: Chunk `389_397_410` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `822_830_843` (icd (New Version))*

---

### 3. Principal Diagnosis Sequencing Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Principal Diagnosis Sequencing Rules with identical behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `385_387_388`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In the unusual instance when two or more diagnoses equally meet the criteria for principal diagnosis as determined by the circumstances of admission, diagnostic workup and/or therapy provided, and the Alphabetic Index, Tabular List, or another coding guidelines does not provide sequencing direction, any one of the diagnoses may be sequenced first.
>
> Sequence as the principal diagnosis the condition, which after study occasioned the admission to the hospital, even though treatment may not have been carried out due to unforeseen circumstances.
>
> When the admission is for treatment of a complication resulting from surgery or other medical care, the complication code is sequenced as the principal diagnosis. If the complication is classified to the T80-T88 series and the code lacks the necessary specificity in describing the complication, an additional code for the specific complication should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Principal Diagnosis Sequencing Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- When two or more diagnoses equally meet criteria for principal diagnosis without specific sequencing direction, any one may be sequenced first.
- Sequence the condition that occasioned the admission after study, even if treatment was not carried out due to unforeseen circumstances.
- When admission is for treatment of a complication resulting from surgery or other medical care, the complication code is sequenced as the principal diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnoses must equally meet criteria for principal diagnosis based on circumstances of admission, diagnostic workup, and/or therapy provided.
- Sequencing direction must be absent in the Alphabetic Index, Tabular List, or other coding guidelines for the 'any one' rule to apply.
- The complication code must be classified to the T80-T88 series for it to be sequenced as the principal diagnosis.

**New in this version:**
- Requirement to assign an additional code for the specific complication if the T80-T88 series code lacks necessary specificity in describing the complication.
- If the complication code is classified to the T80-T88 series and lacks necessary specificity, an additional code for the specific complication must be assigned.

>

### 4. Underdosing Code Assignment Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding underdosing code assignment and SDOH integration with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `308_378`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Underdosing refers to taking less of a medication than is prescribed by a provider or a manufacturer's instruction. Discontinuing the use of a prescribed medication on the patient's own initiative (not directed by the patient's provider) is also classified as an underdosing. For underdosing, assign the code from categories T36-T50 (fifth or sixth character '6'). Documentation of a change in the patient's condition is not required in order to assign an underdosing code. Documentation that the patient is taking less of a medication than is prescribed or discontinued the prescribed medication is sufficient for code assignment.
>
> Codes for underdosing should never be assigned as principal or first-listed codes. If a patient has a relapse or exacerbation of the medical condition for which the drug is prescribed because of the reduction in dose, then the medical condition itself should be coded.
>
> Noncompliance (Z91.12-, Z91.13-, Z91.14 -and Z91.A4-) or complication of care (Y63.6-Y63.9) codes are to be used with an underdosing code to indicate intent, if known.
>
> Social determinants of health (SDOH) codes describing social problems, conditions, or risk factors that influence a patient's health should be assigned when this information is documented in the patient's medical record. Assign as many SDOH codes as are necessary to describe all of the social problems, conditions, or risk factors documented during the current episode of care. For example, a patient who lives alone may suffer an acute injury temporarily impacting their ability to perform routine activities of daily living. When documented as such, this would support assignment of code Z60.2, Problems related to living alone. However, merely living alone, without documentation of a risk or unmet need for assistance at home, would not support assignment of code Z60.2. Documentation by a clinician (or patient-reported information that is signed off by a clinician) that the patient expressed concerns with access and availability of food would support assignment of code Z59.41, Food insecurity. Similarly, medical record documentation indicating the patient is homeless would support assignment of a code from subcategory Z59.0-, Homelessness.
>
> For social determinants of health classified to chapter 21, such as information found in categories Z55-Z65, Persons with potential health hazards related to socioeconomic and psychosocial circumstances, code assignment may be based on medical record documentation from clinicians involved in the care of the patient who are not the patient's provider since this information represents social information, rather than medical diagnoses. For example, coding professionals may utilize documentation of social information from social workers, community health workers, case managers, or nurses, if their documentation is included in the official medical record.
>
> Patient self-reported documentation may be used to assign codes for social determinants of health, as long as the patient self-reported information is signed-off by and incorporated into the medical record by either a clinician or provider.
>
> Social determinants of health codes are located primarily in these Z code categories:

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Underdosing Code Assignment and SDOH Integration

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from categories T36-T50 with fifth or sixth character '6' for underdosing or self-discontinuation.
- Never assign underdosing codes as principal or first-listed codes; code the underlying medical condition if present.
- Use Noncompliance (Z91.12-, Z91.13-, Z91.14-, Z91.A4-) or complication of care (Y63.6-Y63.9) codes with underdosing codes to indicate intent.
- Assign Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) codes based on documented social problems, conditions, or risk factors influencing health.
- Allow patient self-reported SDOH information if signed off and incorporated into the medical record by a clinician or provider.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must state the patient is taking less medication than prescribed or discontinued the medication on their own initiative.
- Documentation of a change in the patient's condition is not required to assign an underdosing code.
- SDOH codes require documentation of specific social problems, conditions, or risk factors, not just the presence of a social circumstance.
- SDOH documentation may come from clinicians other than the patient's provider (e.g., social workers, case managers) if included in the official medical record.
- Patient self-reported SDOH data must be verified and signed off by a clinician or provider to be valid for coding.
- Assign as many SDOH codes as are necessary to describe all documented social problems, conditions, or risk factors during the current episode of care.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning underdosing codes as principal or first-listed diagnoses.
- Requiring documentation of a change in patient condition to validate an underdosing code.
- Assigning SDOH codes based solely on the existence of a social circumstance without documented risk or unmet need.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit inclusion of Noncompliance (Z91.12-, Z91.13-, Z91.14-, Z91.A4-) and complication of care (Y63.6-Y63.9) codes to indicate intent alongside underdosing codes.
- Expanded authority to use SDOH documentation from non-provider clinicians (social workers, community health workers, case managers, nurses) for code assignment.
- Validation of patient self-reported SDOH information through clinician or provider sign-off and incorporation into the medical record.
- SDOH codes for categories Z55-Z65 may be assigned based on documentation from clinicians other than the patient's provider (e.g., social workers, community health workers, case managers, nurses) if included in the official medical record.

---

Documentation that the patient is taking less of a medication than is prescribed or discontinued the prescribed medication is sufficient for code assignment.
>

>

Assign as many SDOH codes as are necessary to describe all of the social problems, conditions, or risk factors documented during the current episode of care. When documented as such, this would support assignment of code Z60.2, Problems related to living alone. However, merely living alone, without documentation of a risk or unmet need for assistance at home, would not support assignment of code Z60.2. Similarly, medical record documentation indicating the patient is experiencing homelessness would support assignment of a code from subcategory Z59.0-, Homelessness.
>
> For social determinants of health classified to chapter 21, such as information found in categories Z55-Z65, Persons with potential health hazards related to socioeconomic and psychosocial circumstances, code assignment may be based on medical record documentation from clinicians involved in the care of the patient who are not the patient's provider since this information represents social information, rather than medical diagnoses. For example, coding professionals may utilize documentation of social information from social workers, community health workers, case managers, or nurses, if their documentation is included in the official medical record.
>
> Patient self-reported documentation may be used to assign codes for social determinants of health, as long as the patient self-reported information is signed-off by and incorporated into the medical record by either a clinician or provider.
>
> Social determinants of health codes are located primarily in these Z code categories:

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Underdosing Code Assignment and SDOH Integration

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from categories T36-T50 with fifth or sixth character '6' for underdosing or self-discontinuation.
- Never assign underdosing codes as principal or first-listed codes; code the underlying medical condition if the underdosing caused a relapse.
- Use Noncompliance (Z91.12-, Z91.13-, Z91.14-, Z91.A4-) or complication of care (Y63.6-Y63.9) codes with underdosing codes to indicate intent if known.
- Assign Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) codes based on documented social problems, conditions, or risk factors influencing health.
- Allow patient self-reported SDOH information if signed off and incorporated into the medical record by a clinician or provider.

- Patient self-reported SDOH information must be signed off by a clinician or provider to be valid for code assignment.
- Assign as many SDOH codes as are necessary to describe all documented social problems, conditions, or risk factors during the current episode of care.

- Assigning underdosing codes as principal or first-listed diagnoses.
- Requiring documentation of a change in the patient's condition to validate an underdosing code.
- Assigning SDOH codes without documented evidence of a specific social problem, condition, or risk factor.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit inclusion of self-discontinuation of prescribed medication as a valid trigger for underdosing codes.
- Expanded authority to use SDOH documentation from non-provider clinicians (social workers, community health workers, case managers, nurses) for categories Z55-Z65.
- Mandatory requirement that patient self-reported SDOH information be signed off by a clinician or provider to be coded.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical logic for assigning T36-T50 codes, including the requirement to never use them as principal diagnoses and the specific documentation triggers. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the 'New items' and 'Deprecated' sections, such as explicitly listing 'community health workers' in the newer version and clarifying the 'self-discontinuation' trigger, which do not alter the operational coding rules. These changes represent administrative refinements to the documentation of the guidelines rather than substantive shifts in coding behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Expanded authority to use SDOH documentation from non-provider clinicians (social workers, community health workers, case managers, nurses) for code assignment. -> icd (New Version): Expanded authority to use SDOH documentation from non-provider clinicians (social workers, community health workers, case managers, nurses) for categories Z55-Z65.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit inclusion of Noncompliance (Z91.12-, Z91.13-, Z91.14-, Z91.A4-) and complication of care (Y63.6-Y63.9) codes to indicate intent alongside underdosing codes. -> icd (New Version): Explicit inclusion of self-discontinuation of prescribed medication as a valid trigger for underdosing codes.

### 5. Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding code assignment logic and specific exceptions for non-provider documentation.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `27_41`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The assignment of a diagnosis code is based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition exists. The provider's statement that the patient has a particular condition is sufficient. Code assignment is not based on clinical criteria used by the provider to establish the diagnosis. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, query the provider.
>
> Code assignment is based on the documentation by the patient's provider (i.e., physician or other qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis). There are a few exceptions when code assignment may be based on medical record documentation from clinicians who are not the patient's provider (i.e., physician or other qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis). In this context, 'clinicians' other than the patient's provider refer to healthcare professionals permitted, based on regulatory or accreditation requirements or internal hospital policies, to document in a patient's official medical record.
>
> These exceptions include codes for:
>
> - Body Mass Index (BMI)
> - Depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers
> - Pressure ulcer stage
> - Coma scale
> - NIH stroke scale (NIHSS)
> - Social determinants of health (SDOH) classified to Chapter 21
> - Laterality
> - Blood alcohol level
> - Underimmunization status
>
> This information is typically , or may be, documented by other clinicians involved in the care of the patient (e.g., a dietitian often documents the BMI, a nurse often documents the pressure ulcer stages, and an emergency medical technician often documents the coma scale). However, the associated diagnosis (such as overweight, obesity, acute stroke, pressure ulcer, or a condition classifiable to category F10, Alcohol related disorders) must be documented by the patient's provider. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, either from the same clinician or different clinicians, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification.
>
> The BMI, coma scale, NIHSS, blood alcohol level codes, codes for social determinants of health and underimmunization status should only be reported as secondary diagnoses.
>
> See Section I.C.21.c.17. for additional information regarding coding social determinants of health.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code assignment is based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition exists, not on clinical criteria used to establish the diagnosis.
- If there is conflicting medical record documentation, the provider must be queried for clarification.
- Exceptions allow code assignment based on documentation from non-provider clinicians (e.g., dietitians, nurses) for specific metrics like BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and coma scale.
- The associated diagnosis for these exceptions must still be documented by the patient's provider.
- Codes for BMI, coma scale, NIHSS, blood alcohol level, SDOH, and underimmunization status must only be reported as secondary diagnoses.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The provider's statement that the patient has a particular condition is sufficient for code assignment.
- For exceptions, the associated diagnosis (e.g., obesity, acute stroke) must be documented by the patient's provider.
- Specific metrics (BMI, ulcer depth, etc.) may be documented by permitted non-provider clinicians per regulatory or internal policies.
- Conflicting documentation from any clinician requires a provider query before finalizing codes.
- Exception codes must be assigned as secondary diagnoses, not primary.
- The associated diagnosis for exception codes must be documented by the patient's provider (physician or other qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis).
- Code assignment relies on documentation by the patient's provider unless a specific exception applies based on regulatory or accreditation requirements or internal hospital policies.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit inclusion of Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) classified to Chapter 21 as an exception for non-provider documentation.
- Clarification that underimmunization status is an exception allowing non-provider documentation of the metric.
- Mandatory restriction that exception codes (BMI, NIHSS, etc.) are limited to secondary diagnosis reporting.
- Laterality is included as an exception allowing code assignment based on documentation from non-provider clinicians.
- Codes for depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers are included as an exception allowing code assignment based on documentation from non-provider clinicians.

---

Code assignment is not based on clinical criteria used by the provider to establish the diagnosis. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, query the provider.

However, the associated diagnosis (such as overweight, obesity, acute stroke, pressure ulcer, or a condition classifiable to category F10, Alcohol related disorders) must be documented by the patient's provider. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, either from the same clinician or different clinicians, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification.
>

>

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code assignment is based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition exists, not on clinical criteria used to establish the diagnosis.
- If conflicting medical record documentation exists, the provider must be queried for clarification.
- Exceptions allow code assignment based on documentation from non-provider clinicians (e.g., dietitians, nurses) for specific metrics like BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and coma scale.
- The associated diagnosis for these exceptions must still be documented by the patient's provider.
- Codes for BMI, coma scale, NIHSS, blood alcohol level, SDOH, and underimmunization status must be reported as secondary diagnoses.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The patient's provider must legally be accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.
- The provider's statement that a condition exists is sufficient for code assignment.
- Associated diagnoses for exception metrics must be documented by the patient's provider.
- Specific exception metrics (BMI, ulcer depth, etc.) must be documented by permitted clinicians in the official medical record.
- Exception codes must be reported as secondary diagnoses.
- If there is conflicting medical record documentation, either from the same clinician or different clinicians, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification.

**New in this version:**
- BMI, depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers, pressure ulcer stage, coma scale, NIHSS, SDOH, laterality, blood alcohol level, underimmunization status, and firearm injury intent are now permitted as exceptions for code assignment based on non-provider documentation.
- The requirement that associated diagnoses for these exceptions must be documented by the patient's provider is explicitly defined.
- The rule that these specific exception codes must only be reported as secondary diagnoses is established.
- Depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers is now permitted as an exception for code assignment based on non-provider documentation.
- Laterality is now permitted as an exception for code assignment based on non-provider documentation.
- Firearm injury intent is now permitted as an exception for code assignment based on non-provider documentation.
- Codes for social determinants of health (SDOH) classified to Chapter 21 are now permitted as exceptions for code assignment based on non-provider documentation.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Definition Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the baseline rule that code assignment relies on the provider's statement, with exceptions limited to BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and coma scale. The newer version expands this definition by explicitly adding depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers, laterality, firearm injury intent, SDOH, and underimmunization status to the list of permissible non-provider documented exceptions. Both versions maintain the strict requirement that the associated diagnosis for these exceptions must be documented by the patient's provider and that these exception codes must be reported as secondary diagnoses.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Exceptions allow code assignment based on non-provider documentation for BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and coma scale. -> icd (New Version): Exceptions now explicitly include depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers, laterality, firearm injury intent, SDOH, and underimmunization status in addition to the original metrics.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The list of exception codes is limited to BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and coma scale. -> icd (New Version): The list is expanded to include laterality, firearm injury intent, SDOH, and underimmunization status, broadening the scope of metrics that can be assigned based on non-provider documentation.

*Traceability: Chunk `27_41` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `459_473` (icd (New Version))*

### 6. Sign and Symptom Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same 'Sign and Symptom Coding Guidelines' feature with identical behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `45_274`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sign/symptom and 'unspecified' codes have acceptable, even necessary, uses. While specific diagnosis codes should be reported when they are supported by the available medical record documentation and clinical knowledge of the patient's health condition, there are instances when signs/symptoms or unspecified codes are the best choices for accurately reflecting the healthcare encounter. Each healthcare encounter should be coded to the level of certainty known for that encounter.
>
> As stated in the introductory section of these official coding guidelines, a joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved. The entire record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
>
> If a definitive diagnosis has not been established by the end of the encounter, it is appropriate to report codes for sign(s) and/or symptom(s) in lieu of a definitive diagnosis. When sufficient clinical information isn't known or available about a particular health condition to assign a more specific code, it is acceptable to report the appropriate 'unspecified' code (e.g., a diagnosis of pneumonia has been determined, but not the specific type). Unspecified codes should be reported when they are the codes that most accurately reflect what is known about the patient's condition at the time of that particular encounter. It would be inappropriate to select a specific code that is not supported by the medical record documentation or conduct medically unnecessary diagnostic testing in order to determine a more specific code.
>
> Codes for signs and symptoms may be reported in addition to a related definitive diagnosis when the sign or symptom is not routinely associated with that diagnosis, such as the various signs and symptoms associated with complex syndromes. The definitive diagnosis code should be sequenced before the symptom code.
>
> Signs or symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes, unless otherwise instructed by the classification.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sign and Symptom Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Report codes for signs/symptoms in lieu of a definitive diagnosis if a definitive diagnosis has not been established by the end of the encounter.
- Report appropriate 'unspecified' codes when sufficient clinical information is not known or available to assign a more specific code.
- Report codes for signs and symptoms in addition to a related definitive diagnosis when the sign or symptom is not routinely associated with that diagnosis.
- Sequence the definitive diagnosis code before the symptom code when both are reported.
- Do not assign codes for signs or symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process as additional codes unless otherwise instructed.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Specific diagnosis codes must be supported by available medical record documentation and clinical knowledge of the patient's health condition.
- The entire medical record must be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
- Coding must reflect the level of certainty known for the encounter.
- It is inappropriate to select a specific code that is not supported by the medical record documentation.
- Conducting medically unnecessary diagnostic testing to determine a more specific code is prohibited.

---

- The entire medical record must be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
- Coding must reflect the level of certainty known for the encounter.
- It is inappropriate to select a specific code that is not supported by the medical record documentation.
- Conducting medically unnecessary diagnostic testing to determine a more specific code is prohibited.

### 7. Diagnostic Statement Coding Guidelines

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the provider has included a diagnosis in the final diagnostic statement, such as the discharge summary or the face sheet, it should ordinarily be coded. Some providers include in the diagnostic statement resolved conditions or diagnoses and status-post procedures from previous admissions that have no bearing on the current stay. Such conditions are not to be reported and are coded only if required by hospital policy.
>
> However, history codes (categories Z80-Z87) may be used as secondary codes if the historical condition or family history has an impact on current care or influences treatment.

**Extracted Profile:**

- Do not report resolved conditions or status-post procedures from previous admissions that have no impact on the current hospital stay.
- Use history codes (categories Z80-Z87) as secondary codes only if the historical condition or family history has an impact on current care or influences treatment.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnosis must be included in the final diagnostic statement to be ordinarily coded.
- Resolved conditions or status-post procedures must have a bearing on the current stay to be reported.
- History codes require documented impact on current care or treatment influence to be valid as secondary codes.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding resolved conditions or status-post procedures from previous admissions that have no bearing on the current stay.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit permission to use history codes (Z80-Z87) as secondary codes when the historical condition impacts current care.

> **Actual Text**

- Diagnoses are sequenced according to the circumstances of the admission.

### 11. Impending and Threatened Condition Coding
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding logic for impending and threatened conditions with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `38`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code any condition described at the time of discharge as 'impending' or 'threatened' as follows:
>
> If it did occur, code as confirmed diagnosis.
>
> If it did not occur, reference the Alphabetic Index to determine if the condition has a subentry term for 'impending' or 'threatened' and also reference main term entries for 'Impending' and for 'Threatened.'
>
> If the subterms are listed, assign the given code.
>
> If the subterms are not listed, code the existing underlying condition(s) and not the condition described as impending or threatened.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Impending and Threatened Condition Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code any condition described at discharge as 'impending' or 'threatened' as a confirmed diagnosis if it did occur.
- If the condition did not occur, reference the Alphabetic Index for subentry terms under 'impending' or 'threatened' and main term entries.
- Assign the given code if subterms for 'impending' or 'threatened' are listed in the index.
- If subterms are not listed, code the existing underlying condition(s) and do not code the condition described as impending or threatened.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Verification of whether the condition actually occurred at the time of discharge.
- Consultation of the Alphabetic Index for specific subentry terms related to 'impending' or 'threatened'.
- Verification of main term entries for 'Impending' and 'Threatened' in the index.
- Documentation of the underlying condition if no specific impending/threatened subterm exists.

---

### 12. Combination Code Assignment Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Combination Code Assignment Logic with identical behavioral definitions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `36`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A combination code is a single code used to classify: Two diagnoses, or A diagnosis with an associated secondary process (manifestation) A diagnosis with an associated complication
>
> Combination codes are identified by referring to subterm entries in the Alphabetic Index and by reading the inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
>
> Assign only the combination code when that code fully identifies the diagnostic conditions involved or when the Alphabetic Index so directs. Multiple coding should not be used when the classification provides a combination code that clearly identifies all of the elements documented in the diagnosis. When the combination code lacks necessary specificity in describing the manifestation or complication, an additional code should be used as a secondary code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Combination Code Assignment Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- A combination code is a single code used to classify two diagnoses, or a diagnosis with an associated secondary process (manifestation), or a diagnosis with an associated complication.
- Combination codes are identified by referring to subterm entries in the Alphabetic Index and by reading the inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
- Assign only the combination code when that code fully identifies the diagnostic conditions involved or when the Alphabetic Index so directs.
- Multiple coding should not be used when the classification provides a combination code that clearly identifies all of the elements documented in the diagnosis.
- When the combination code lacks necessary specificity in describing the manifestation or complication, an additional code should be used as a secondary code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The combination code must fully identify the diagnostic conditions involved.
- The Alphabetic Index must direct the use of the combination code.
- Inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List must be consulted to identify the code.
- All elements documented in the diagnosis must be clearly identified by the combination code to avoid multiple coding.
- An additional secondary code is required only when the combination code lacks necessary specificity for the manifestation or complication.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using multiple codes when a single combination code clearly identifies all documented elements.
- Assigning a combination code without verifying inclusion/exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
- Failing to consult the Alphabetic Index subterm entries for code identification.
- Omitting a secondary code when the combination code lacks specificity for a manifestation or complication.
- Classifying two diagnoses or a diagnosis with a secondary process using separate codes instead of a combination code when available.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to use subterm entries in the Alphabetic Index for identifying combination codes.
- Mandatory review of inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List for code validation.
- Clarification that a combination code is a single code for two diagnoses, a diagnosis with a manifestation, or a diagnosis with a complication.
- Rule that multiple coding is prohibited if the combination code fully identifies all documented elements.
- Directive to add a secondary code only when the combination code lacks specificity for the manifestation or complication.

---

**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `203`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient hospital with a non-pressure ulcer at one severity level and it progresses to a higher severity level, two separate codes should be assigned: one code for the site and severity level of the ulcer on admission and a second code for the same ulcer site and the highest severity level reported during the stay.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for pressure ulcer stage documentation by clinicians other than patient's provider

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Inpatient Ulcer Severity Coding Protocol

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign two separate codes for non-pressure ulcers that progress to a higher severity level during an inpatient stay.
- The first code represents the site and severity level of the ulcer on admission.
- The second code represents the same ulcer site and the highest severity level reported during the stay.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient was admitted with a non-pressure ulcer at a specific severity level.
- Documentation must confirm the ulcer progressed to a higher severity level during the inpatient stay.
- Clinicians other than the patient's provider must follow Section I.B.14. for pressure ulcer stage documentation.

### 17. Observation Diagnosis Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe identical observation diagnosis coding rules with no behavioral differences between the versions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `401`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted for observation for a medical condition, assign a code for the medical condition as the first-listed diagnosis.
>
> When a patient presents for outpatient surgery and develops complications requiring admission to observation, code the reason for the surgery as the first reported diagnosis (reason for the encounter), followed by codes for the complications as secondary diagnoses.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Observation Diagnosis Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a code for the medical condition as the first-listed diagnosis when a patient is admitted for observation.
- Code the reason for the surgery as the first reported diagnosis when a patient presents for outpatient surgery and develops complications requiring admission to observation.
- Code complications as secondary diagnoses when they occur during outpatient surgery requiring observation admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The patient must be admitted specifically for observation status.
- The medical condition must be the primary reason for the observation admission.
- Complications must require admission to observation status to be coded as secondary diagnoses.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `834`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted for observation for a medical condition, assign a code for the medical condition as the first-listed diagnosis.
>
> When a patient presents for outpatient surgery and develops complications requiring admission to observation, code the reason for the surgery as the first reported diagnosis (reason for the encounter), followed by codes for the complications as secondary diagnoses.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Observation Diagnosis Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a code for the medical condition as the first-listed diagnosis when a patient is admitted for observation.
- Code the reason for the surgery as the first reported diagnosis when a patient presents for outpatient surgery and develops complications requiring admission to observation.
- Code complications as secondary diagnoses when they occur during outpatient surgery requiring observation admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The patient must be admitted specifically for observation status.
- The medical condition must be clearly documented as the reason for the observation admission.
- If complications arise from outpatient surgery, the surgical reason must be documented as the primary encounter reason.

### 19. Unspecified Medical Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the use of unspecified and other specified codes with matching behavioral definitions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `15`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code. For those categories for which an unspecified code is not provided, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified.
>
> See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unspecified Medical Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code.
- For categories without a specific unspecified code, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified conditions.
- Coders must verify medical record sufficiency before assigning unspecified codes.
- Coders should utilize 'other specified' codes as a fallback when unspecified codes are unavailable for a condition category.
- Guidelines reference Section I.B.18 regarding the use of signs, symptoms, and unspecified codes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Medical record information must be insufficient to assign a more specific code for an unspecified code to be valid.
- The absence of a specific unspecified code in a category permits the use of the 'other specified' code to cover unspecified cases.
- Documentation must support the insufficiency of detail required to justify an unspecified code assignment.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning unspecified codes when sufficient information exists to determine a more specific diagnosis.
- Using unspecified codes as a default without verifying medical record insufficiency.
- Failing to consider 'other specified' codes when an unspecified code is not available for a condition.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit permission to use 'other specified' codes to represent both other and unspecified conditions in categories lacking an unspecified code.
- Mandatory reference to Section I.B.18 for guidance on signs, symptoms, and unspecified code usage.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `447`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code. For those categories for which an unspecified code is not provided, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified.
>
> See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unspecified Medical Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code.
- For categories without a specific unspecified code, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified conditions.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Information in the medical record must be insufficient to assign a more specific code before using an unspecified code.
- The 'other specified' code may be used to represent unspecified conditions only when no specific unspecified code exists for that category.

### 22. Abuse and Neglect Coding Protocols
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the sequencing and coding requirements for confirmed and suspected abuse and neglect cases.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `310`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sequence first the appropriate code from categories T74, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, confirmed, or T76, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, suspected, for abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, followed by any accompanying mental health or injury code(s).
>
> If the documentation in the medical record states abuse or neglect, it is coded as confirmed (T74.-). It is coded as suspected if it is documented as suspected (T76.-).
>
> For cases of confirmed abuse or neglect an external cause code from the assault section (X92-Y09 ) should be added to identify the cause of any physical injuries. A perpetrator code (Y07) should be added when the perpetrator of the abuse is known. For suspected cases of abuse or neglect, do not report external cause or perpetrator code.
>
> If a suspected case of abuse, neglect or mistreatment is ruled out during an encounter code Z04.71, Encounter for examination and observation following alleged physical adult abuse, ruled out, or code Z04.72, Encounter for examination and observation following alleged child physical abuse, ruled out, should be used, not a code from T76.
>
> If a suspected case of alleged rape or sexual abuse is ruled out during an encounter code Z04.41, Encounter for examination and observation following alleged adult rape or code Z04.42, Encounter for examination and observation following alleged child rape, should be used, not a code from T76.
>
> If a suspected case of forced sexual exploitation or forced labor exploitation is ruled out during an encounter, code Z04.81, Encounter for examination and observation of victim following forced sexual exploitation, or code Z04.82, Encounter for examination and observation of victim following forced labor exploitation, should be used, not a code from T76.
>
> See Section I.C.15. Abuse in a pregnant patient.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Abuse and Neglect Coding Protocols

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence confirmed abuse/neglect codes (T74.-) followed by mental health or injury codes, then external cause codes (X92-Y09) and perpetrator codes (Y07) if known.
- Code suspected abuse/neglect as T76.- only if not ruled out; if ruled out, use specific Z04.71, Z04.72, Z04.41, Z04.42, Z04.81, or Z04.82 codes instead.
- Do not report external cause or perpetrator codes for suspected cases of abuse or neglect.
- Assign T74.- for confirmed abuse/neglect documented in the medical record.
- Apply specific Z04 encounter codes for ruled-out allegations of physical abuse, rape, sexual abuse, forced sexual exploitation, or forced labor exploitation.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state 'abuse' or 'neglect' to assign confirmed codes (T74.-).
- Documentation must explicitly state 'suspected' to assign suspected codes (T76.-).
- External cause codes (X92-Y09) and perpetrator codes (Y07) are mandatory for confirmed cases to identify injury causes and perpetrators.
- If a suspected case is ruled out during an encounter, a specific Z04.- code must be used rather than a T76.- code.
- Perpetrator codes (Y07) are required only when the perpetrator of the abuse is known.
- Documentation must explicitly reference 'abuse in a pregnant patient' to trigger specific coding considerations per Section I.C.15.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using T76.- codes for cases of suspected abuse or neglect that have been ruled out during the encounter.
- Reporting external cause or perpetrator codes for suspected cases of abuse or neglect.
- Failing to sequence confirmed abuse/neglect codes before accompanying mental health or injury codes.
- Using T76.- codes for ruled-out allegations of rape, sexual abuse, forced sexual exploitation, or forced labor exploitation instead of Z04.- codes.
- Omitting external cause codes for confirmed abuse cases where physical injuries are present.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of Z04.71 and Z04.72 for ruled-out physical adult and child abuse encounters.
- Mandatory use of Z04.41 and Z04.42 for ruled-out adult and child rape encounters.
- Mandatory use of Z04.81 and Z04.82 for ruled-out forced sexual exploitation and forced labor exploitation encounters.
- Requirement to add perpetrator code (Y07) specifically when the perpetrator is known in confirmed cases.
- Explicit instruction to sequence T74.- codes before any accompanying mental health or injury codes.

---

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sequence first the appropriate code from categories T74, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, confirmed, or T76, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, suspected, for abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, followed by any accompanying mental health or injury code(s).

>

> If a suspected case of forced sexual exploitation or forced labor exploitation is ruled out during an encounter, code Z04.81, Encounter for examination and observation of victim following forced sexual exploitation, or code Z04.82, Encounter for examination and observation of victim following forced labor exploitation, should be used, not a code from T76.
>
> See Section I.C.15. Abuse in a pregnant patient.

**Extracted Profile:**

- Use specific ruled-out encounter codes (Z04.71, Z04.72, Z04.41, Z04.42, Z04.81, Z04.82) instead of T76 codes when abuse is ruled out.

- Documentation must explicitly state 'suspected' to assign suspected codes (T76.-).

- Failing to sequence confirmed codes (T74.-) before suspected codes (T76.-) or accompanying condition codes.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of specific encounter codes Z04.71, Z04.72, Z04.41, Z04.42, Z04.81, and Z04.82 for ruled-out cases of physical, sexual, and exploitation abuse.
- Requirement to add perpetrator code Y07 specifically when the perpetrator is known in confirmed cases.

This represents an administrative clarification rather than a change in clinical behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions mandatory use of Z04 codes for ruled-out cases generally -> icd (New Version): Explicitly lists mandatory use of specific encounter codes Z04.71, Z04.72, Z04.41, Z04.42, Z04.81, and Z04.82 for ruled-out cases.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): States external cause codes are mandatory for confirmed cases -> icd (New Version): States external cause codes are mandatory only when the abuse is confirmed and injuries are present.

*Traceability: Chunk `310` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `738` (icd (New Version))*

### 23. Abnormal Findings Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Clinical Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding guideline regarding the requirement for provider indication of clinical significance for abnormal findings.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `396`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Abnormal findings (laboratory, x-ray, pathologic, and other diagnostic results) are not coded and reported unless the provider indicates their clinical significance. If the findings are outside the normal range and the provider has ordered other tests to evaluate the condition or prescribed treatment, it is appropriate to ask the provider whether the abnormal finding should be added.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practices in the outpatient setting for coding encounters for diagnostic tests that have been interpreted by a provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Abnormal Findings Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Abnormal findings are not coded unless the provider indicates their clinical significance.
- Findings outside the normal range require provider confirmation of clinical significance before coding.
- Coder must ask the provider if an abnormal finding should be added if other tests were ordered or treatment prescribed.
- Coding practices differ from outpatient diagnostic test interpretation coding.
- Provider indication of clinical significance is the primary trigger for coding abnormal findings.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must explicitly indicate the clinical significance of the abnormal finding.
- Documentation must show the finding is outside the normal range.
- Evidence of provider ordering additional tests or prescribing treatment supports coding.
- Coder must query the provider if clinical significance is not documented.
- Coding is prohibited without explicit provider indication of significance.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding abnormal findings without provider indication of clinical significance.
- Assuming clinical significance based solely on the finding being outside normal range.
- Applying outpatient diagnostic test interpretation rules to inpatient abnormal findings.
- Coding abnormal findings as integral parts of the disease process without provider confirmation.
- Assigning codes for abnormal findings without verifying if they require treatment or further evaluation.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory provider query process for abnormal findings lacking clinical significance documentation.
- Explicit distinction between inpatient abnormal findings coding and outpatient diagnostic test interpretation.
- Requirement to document clinical significance before assigning any abnormal finding code.
- Guideline update to prevent coding of incidental findings without provider validation.
- Enhanced documentation standards for laboratory, x-ray, and pathologic results in claims.

---

>

If the findings are outside the normal range and the provider has ordered other tests to evaluate the condition or prescribed treatment, it is appropriate to ask the provider whether the abnormal finding should be added.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practices in the outpatient setting for coding encounters for diagnostic tests that have been interpreted by a provider.

- Abnormal findings must be linked to a specific clinical condition or treatment plan to be reportable.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must explicitly indicate the clinical significance of the abnormal finding.
- Documentation must show the finding is outside the normal range.
- Evidence of provider-ordered tests or prescribed treatment related to the finding is required.
- Clinical context must distinguish the finding from routine diagnostic test interpretations.
- The abnormal finding must be integral to the patient's care plan or diagnosis.

- Assigning codes to abnormal findings solely based on laboratory or x-ray results.

- Coding abnormal findings in outpatient settings using inpatient guidelines.

**New in this version:**

- New guidelines distinguishing inpatient vs. outpatient coding for diagnostic interpretations.
- Additional data elements required to support the coding of abnormal findings.
