### 24. G89 Pain Classification Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical G89 Pain Classification Guidelines, including the specific rules for F45.41/F45.42 usage and G89 exclusions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `139_148_150`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign code F45.41, for pain that is exclusively related to psychological disorders. As indicated by the Excludes 1 note under category G89, a code from category G89 should not be assigned with code F45.41.
>
> Code F45.42, Pain disorders with related psychological factors, should be used with a code from category G89, Pain, not elsewhere classified, if there is documentation of a psychological component for a patient with acute or chronic pain.
>
> See Section I.C.6. Pain
>
> Codes in category G89, Pain, not elsewhere classified, may be used in conjunction with codes from other categories and chapters to provide more detail about acute or chronic pain and neoplasm-related pain, unless otherwise indicated below.
>
> If the pain is not specified as acute or chronic, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related, do not assign codes from category G89.
>
> A code from category G89 should not be assigned if the underlying (definitive) diagnosis is known, unless the reason for the encounter is pain control/ management and not management of the underlying condition.
>
> When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition (e.g., spinal fusion, kyphoplasty), a code for the underlying condition (e.g., vertebral fracture, spinal stenosis) should be assigned as the principal diagnosis. No code from category G89 should be assigned.
>
> - (i) Assigning Category G89 and Site-Specific Pain Codes Codes from category G89 may be used in conjunction with codes that identify the site of pain (including codes from chapter 18) if the category G89 code provides additional information. For example, if the code describes the site of the pain, but does not fully describe whether the pain is acute or chronic, then both codes should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Pain Classification and Exclusion Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code F45.41 for pain exclusively related to psychological disorders, excluding category G89 codes.
- Use code F45.42 with a category G89 code when documentation indicates a psychological component for acute or chronic pain.
- Do not assign category G89 codes if pain is unspecified, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related without further specification.
- Avoid assigning category G89 codes if the underlying definitive diagnosis is known, unless the encounter is solely for pain control.
- Assign category G89 codes alongside site-specific pain codes if the G89 code provides additional information about pain acuity or chronicity.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state that pain is exclusively related to psychological disorders to use F45.41.
- Documentation must confirm a psychological component exists to use F45.42 in conjunction with G89 codes.
- The encounter reason must be pain control/management, not underlying condition management, to justify G89 assignment when a definitive diagnosis is known.
- Pain must be specified as acute or chronic, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related to allow G89 assignment.
- Site-specific pain codes must be used in conjunction with G89 codes if the G89 code adds detail about pain duration or nature.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning category G89 codes when the underlying definitive diagnosis is known and the encounter is for that condition management.
- Assigning category G89 codes for unspecified, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related pain without specific documentation.
- Assigning category G89 codes in conjunction with F45.41 for pain related to psychological disorders.
- Using category G89 codes as the principal diagnosis when an admission is for a procedure treating the underlying condition.
- Failing to assign site-specific pain codes when the G89 code does not fully describe the site of pain.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to use F45.42 with G89 codes for pain with psychological components.
- Clarification that G89 codes may be used with site-specific pain codes if they provide additional information.
- Specific exclusion note under category G89 prohibiting assignment with F45.41.
- Guidance on principal diagnosis assignment for procedures treating underlying conditions versus pain management.
- Requirement to query or document specific pain characteristics (acute/chronic, etc.) before assigning G89 codes.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `569_578_580_583`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign code F45.41, for pain that is exclusively related to psychological disorders. As indicated by the Excludes 1 note under category G89, a code from category G89 should not be assigned with code F45.41.
>
> Code F45.42, Pain disorders with related psychological factors, should be used with a code from category G89, Pain, not elsewhere classified, if there is documentation of a psychological component for a patient with acute or chronic pain.
>
> See Section I.C.6. Pain
>
> Codes in category G89, Pain, not elsewhere classified, may be used in conjunction with codes from other categories and chapters to provide more detail about acute or chronic pain and neoplasm-related pain, unless otherwise indicated below.
>
> If the pain is not specified as acute or chronic, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related, do not assign codes from category G89.
>
> A code from category G89 should not be assigned if the underlying (definitive) diagnosis is known, unless the reason for the encounter is pain control/ management and not management of the underlying condition.
>
> When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition (e.g., spinal fusion, kyphoplasty), a code for the underlying condition (e.g., vertebral fracture, spinal stenosis) should be assigned as the principal diagnosis. No code from category G89 should be assigned.
>
> - (i) Assigning Category G89 and Site-Specific Pain Codes Codes from category G89 may be used in conjunction with codes that identify the site of pain (including codes from chapter 18) if the category G89 code provides additional information. For example, if the code describes the site of the pain, but does not fully describe whether the pain is acute or chronic, then both codes should be assigned.
>
> Chronic pain is classified to subcategory G89.2. There is no time frame defining when pain becomes chronic pain. The provider's documentation should be used to guide use of these codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Pain Classification and Exclusion Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code F45.41 exclusively for pain related to psychological disorders, excluding category G89 codes.
- Use code F45.42 with a category G89 code when documentation indicates a psychological component for acute or chronic pain.
- Do not assign category G89 codes if pain is unspecified, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related without further specification.
- Avoid assigning category G89 codes if the underlying definitive diagnosis is known, unless the encounter is solely for pain control.
- Assign category G89 codes alongside site-specific pain codes if the G89 code provides additional information about pain acuity or chronicity.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state a psychological component to justify using F45.42 with a G89 code.
- The encounter reason must be pain control/management, not underlying condition management, to allow G89 coding when a definitive diagnosis exists.
- Provider documentation must define the pain as acute or chronic to utilize subcategory G89.2, as no specific time frame defines chronicity.
- If the underlying condition is the reason for a procedure (e.g., spinal fusion), the underlying condition must be the principal diagnosis, excluding G89.
- G89 codes may only be combined with site-specific codes if the G89 code adds detail regarding pain acuity or chronicity not present in the site code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning category G89 codes when the pain is unspecified, post-thoracotomy, postprocedural, or neoplasm-related.
- Assigning category G89 codes when the underlying definitive diagnosis is known and the encounter is for that condition's management.
- Using unspecified laterality codes when documentation is unclear (general rule applied here to pain context).
- Coding pain disorders with psychological factors using only F45.41 without the accompanying G89 code when indicated.
- Assigning G89 codes as the principal diagnosis when an admission is for a procedure treating the underlying condition.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to use F45.42 in conjunction with G89 codes for pain with psychological factors.
- Clarification that chronic pain classification (G89.2) relies entirely on provider documentation without a fixed time frame.
- Guidance allowing G89 codes to be used with site-specific codes to describe pain acuity or chronicity.
- Mandatory exclusion of G89 codes when the encounter is for managing a known underlying condition rather than pain itself.
- Requirement to query or rely on specific documentation for pain acuity before assigning G89 codes.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions contain identical behavioral rules regarding the assignment of F45.41 for exclusive psychological pain and F45.42 for mixed psychological/physical pain. Both versions explicitly prohibit G89 codes when the underlying definitive diagnosis is known unless the encounter is solely for pain control. The only distinction is a minor administrative clarification in the newer version regarding the definition of chronic pain under subcategory G89.2, which relies on provider documentation rather than a fixed time frame, without altering the core clinical logic.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Chronic pain classification relies on general guidelines -> icd (New Version): Chronic pain classification (G89.2) explicitly relies on provider documentation without a fixed time frame.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): G89 codes prohibited if underlying diagnosis known unless pain control -> icd (New Version): G89 codes prohibited if underlying diagnosis known unless pain control (reiterated with emphasis on principal diagnosis for procedures).

### 25. Postoperative Pain Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding postoperative pain coding, with the newer version adding specific instructions for complication-associated pain.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `152_153`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The provider's documentation should be used to guide the coding of postoperative pain, as well as Section III. Reporting Additional Diagnoses and Section IV. Diagnostic Coding and Reporting in the Outpatient Setting.
>
> The default for post-thoracotomy and other postoperative pain not specified as acute or chronic is the code for the acute form.
>
> Routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery should not be coded.
>
> Postoperative pain not associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate postoperative pain code in category G89.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Postoperative Pain Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- The provider's documentation should be used to guide the coding of postoperative pain.
- The default for post-thoracotomy and other postoperative pain not specified as acute or chronic is the code for the acute form.
- Routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery should not be coded.
- Postoperative pain not associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate postoperative pain code in category G89.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly guide the coding of postoperative pain.
- Pain must be distinguished as acute or chronic; if unspecified, the acute form is the default.
- Pain must not be routine or expected immediately after surgery to be coded.
- Pain must be associated with a specific postoperative complication to be coded outside category G89.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery.

**New in this version:**
- Default assignment to acute form codes for post-thoracotomy and unspecified postoperative pain.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `582`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The provider's documentation should be used to guide the coding of postoperative pain, as well as Section III. Reporting Additional Diagnoses and Section IV. Diagnostic Coding and Reporting in the Outpatient Setting.
>
> The default for post-thoracotomy and other postoperative pain not specified as acute or chronic is the code for the acute form.
>
> Routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery should not be coded.
>
> - (a) Postoperative pain not associated with specific postoperative complication
>
> Postoperative pain not associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate postoperative pain code in category G89.
>
> - (b) Postoperative pain associated with specific postoperative complication
>
> Postoperative pain associated with a specific postoperative complication (such as painful wire sutures) is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes. If appropriate, use additional code(s) from category G89 to identify acute or chronic pain (G89.18 or G89.28).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Postoperative Pain Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- The provider's documentation should be used to guide the coding of postoperative pain.
- The default for post-thoracotomy and other postoperative pain not specified as acute or chronic is the code for the acute form.
- Routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery should not be coded.
- Postoperative pain not associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate postoperative pain code in category G89.
- Postoperative pain associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must guide the coding of postoperative pain.
- Pain must be explicitly documented as acute or chronic to avoid defaulting to acute codes.
- Pain must not be routine or expected immediately after surgery to be coded.
- Pain must be associated with a specific postoperative complication to be coded in Chapter 19.
- Additional codes from category G89 (G89.18 or G89.28) must be used if appropriate to identify acute or chronic pain.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding routine or expected postoperative pain immediately after surgery.
- Defaulting to unspecified codes when acute or chronic status is not specified.
- Coding postoperative pain in Chapter 19 without an associated specific postoperative complication.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of provider documentation to determine coding for postoperative pain.
- Requirement to query for clarification if pain status (acute vs chronic) is unspecified.
- Specific instruction to use Chapter 19 codes for pain associated with specific complications like painful wire sutures.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the rule that routine pain should not be coded and specified that uncomplicated pain belongs in category G89. The newer version retains these rules but explicitly clarifies the handling of postoperative pain associated with specific complications, directing coders to Chapter 19 codes (e.g., for painful wire sutures) rather than leaving the distinction implicit. This addition reduces coder variance by providing a clear pathway for coding pain linked to specific procedural complications.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Postoperative pain not associated with a specific postoperative complication is assigned to the appropriate postoperative pain code in category G89. -> icd (New Version): Postoperative pain associated with a specific postoperative complication (such as painful wire sutures) is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Implicitly relies on G89 for all non-routine pain. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly mandates the use of additional codes from category G89 (G89.18 or G89.28) to identify acute or chronic pain when used alongside Chapter 19 codes for complications.

*Traceability: Chunk `152_153` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `582` (icd (New Version))*

### 26. G89 Pain Code Sequencing Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical G89 Pain Code Sequencing Logic with verbatim matching behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `151`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The sequencing of category G89 codes with site- specific pain codes (including chapter 18 codes), is dependent on the circumstances of the encounter/admission as follows:
>
> - If the encounter is for pain control or pain management, assign the code from category G89 followed by the code identifying the specific site of pain (e.g., encounter for pain management for acute neck pain from trauma is assigned code G89.11, Acute pain due to trauma, followed by code M54.2, Cervicalgia, to identify the site of pain).
> - If the encounter is for any other reason except pain control or pain management, and a related definitive diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider, assign the code for the specific site of pain first, followed by the appropriate code from category G89.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Pain Code Sequencing Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- If the encounter is for pain control or pain management, assign the code from category G89 followed by the code identifying the specific site of pain.
- If the encounter is for any other reason except pain control or pain management, and a related definitive diagnosis has not been established, assign the code for the specific site of pain first, followed by the appropriate code from category G89.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The encounter must be explicitly for pain control or pain management to sequence G89 codes first.
- A related definitive diagnosis must be confirmed by the provider before sequencing site-specific pain codes first.
- The specific site of pain must be identified by a corresponding code (e.g., M54.2) when sequencing G89 codes first.

---

### 27. G89 Codes as Principal Diagnosis
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding G89 code sequencing for pain control and neurostimulator insertions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `149`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - Category G89 Codes as Principal or First-Listed Diagnosis Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis or the first-listed code:
>
> - When pain control or pain management is the reason for the admission/encounter (e.g., a patient with displaced intervertebral disc, nerve impingement and severe back pain presents for injection of steroid into the spinal canal). The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis, if known.
> - When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same admission/encounter, a code for the underlying condition should be assigned as the principal diagnosis and the appropriate pain code should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Codes as Principal Diagnosis

**Key Behaviors:**
- Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal or first-listed diagnosis when pain control or pain management is the reason for the admission/encounter.
- When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
- If an admission is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same encounter, assign the code for the underlying condition as the principal diagnosis and the pain code as a secondary diagnosis.
- The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis if known when pain control is the reason for admission.
- G89 codes can serve as the first-listed code in encounters where the primary focus is pain management rather than the etiology of the pain.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Pain control or pain management must be explicitly documented as the reason for the admission or encounter.
- The underlying cause of the pain must be identified and reported as an additional diagnosis if known.
- Clinical documentation must distinguish between admissions for pain control versus admissions for treating the underlying condition causing the pain.
- When a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control, the pain code must be assigned as the principal diagnosis regardless of the procedure performed.
- If both an underlying condition treatment and pain control neurostimulator insertion occur, the underlying condition takes precedence as the principal diagnosis.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning the underlying cause of pain as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is solely for pain control or neurostimulator insertion.
- Failing to report the underlying cause of pain as an additional diagnosis when it is known and documented.
- Using G89 codes as secondary diagnoses when the encounter is primarily for pain management without a concurrent underlying condition treatment.
- Omitting the pain code as a secondary diagnosis when a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control alongside treatment of the underlying condition.
- Treating the insertion of a neurostimulator as the principal diagnosis instead of the pain code when the primary reason for admission is pain control.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit acceptance of G89 codes as principal or first-listed diagnosis for pain control encounters.
- Mandatory assignment of pain code as principal diagnosis for neurostimulator insertions aimed at pain control.
- Requirement to prioritize underlying condition codes over pain codes when both are treated in the same encounter.
- Clarification that underlying causes of pain must be reported as additional diagnoses if known.
- Updated guidance on sequencing G89 codes relative to neurostimulator insertion procedures.

---

>
> - Category G89 Codes as Principal or First-Listed Diagnosis

The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis, if known.
> - When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same admission/encounter, a code for the underlying condition should be assigned as the principal diagnosis and the appropriate pain code should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.

- Assign the underlying condition code as principal if the admission is for treating the condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same encounter.
- Assign the pain code as a secondary diagnosis when the underlying condition is the principal diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Pain control or pain management must be the documented reason for the admission or encounter.
- The underlying cause of the pain must be known and documented to be reported as an additional diagnosis.
- The procedure must be aimed at treating the underlying condition for the underlying code to take precedence over the pain code.

The only difference is the addition of a 'Taxonomy description' field in the newer version, which serves as a metadata summary rather than a change to the guideline content itself. Both versions explicitly mandate that G89 codes are principal for pain control admissions and require underlying conditions to be reported as additional diagnoses if known. Consequently, there are no functional deltas in the coding behavior between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis when pain control is the reason for admission. -> icd (New Version): Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis when pain control is the reason for admission.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. -> icd (New Version): When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.

*Traceability: Chunk `149` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `579` (icd (New Version))*

### 28. Code G89.3 Neoplasm Pain Sequencing
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature 'Code G89.3 Neoplasm Pain Sequencing' with matching behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `156`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code G89.3 is assigned to pain documented as being related, associated or due to cancer, primary or secondary malignancy, or tumor. This code is assigned regardless of whether the pain is acute or chronic.
>
> This code may be assigned as the principal or first-listed code when the stated reason for the admission/encounter is documented as pain control/pain management. The underlying neoplasm should be reported as an additional diagnosis.
>
> When the reason for the admission/encounter is management of the neoplasm and the pain associated with the neoplasm is also documented, code G89.3 may be assigned as an additional diagnosis. It is not necessary to assign an additional code for the site of the pain.
>
> See Section I.C.2. for instructions on the sequencing of neoplasms for all other stated reasons for the admission/encounter (except for pain control/pain management).

**Extracted Profile:**

- Code G89.3 may be assigned as the principal or first-listed code when the stated reason for the admission/encounter is documented as pain control/pain management.
- The underlying neoplasm should be reported as an additional diagnosis when G89.3 is the principal code.
- When the reason for the admission/encounter is management of the neoplasm and the pain associated with the neoplasm is also documented, code G89.3 may be assigned as an additional diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Pain must be explicitly documented as related, associated, or due to cancer, primary or secondary malignancy, or tumor.
- The underlying neoplasm must be reported as an additional diagnosis when G89.3 is the principal code.
- Sequencing follows Section I.C.2. for all stated reasons for admission/encounter except for pain control/pain management.

### 29. Glaucoma Admission Stage Coding Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Glaucoma Coding Rules  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Glaucoma Admission Stage Coding Logic with matching behaviors, requirements, and deprecated rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `161`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient is admitted with glaucoma and the stage progresses during the admission, assign the code for highest stage documented.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Glaucoma Admission Stage Coding Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the code for the highest stage of glaucoma documented during the admission if the stage progresses.
- Prioritize the most severe stage over earlier stages when multiple stages are recorded in the same admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the progression of the glaucoma stage during the hospital stay.
- The highest stage must be clearly identified in the medical record to support the assignment of the corresponding ICD-10-CM code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding the initial stage of glaucoma if a higher stage is documented during the same admission.
- Assigning a lower stage code when the patient's condition has advanced to a more severe stage.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of the highest stage code when stage progression is documented.

---

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient is admitted with glaucoma and the stage progresses during the admission, assign the code for highest stage documented.

**Extracted Profile:**

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the code for the highest stage of glaucoma documented during the admission if the stage progresses.
- Prioritize the most severe stage over earlier stages when multiple stages are recorded in the same admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the progression of the glaucoma stage during the hospital stay.
- The highest stage must be clearly identified in the medical record to support the assignment of the corresponding ICD-10-CM code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding the initial stage of glaucoma if a higher stage is documented during the same admission.
- Assigning a lower stage code when the patient's condition has advanced to a more severe stage.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of the highest stage code when stage progression is documented.

### 30. Glaucoma Seventh Character Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Glaucoma Coding Rules  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the exact same coding rules for glaucoma seventh characters with identical text and logic.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `162`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assignment of the seventh character '4' for 'indeterminate stage' should be based on the clinical documentation. The seventh character '4' is used for glaucomas whose stage cannot be clinically determined. This seventh character should not be confused with the seventh character '0', unspecified, which should be assigned when there is no documentation regarding the stage of the glaucoma.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Glaucoma Seventh Character Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign seventh character '4' for indeterminate stage based on clinical documentation when stage cannot be determined
- Assign seventh character '0' for unspecified stage when there is no documentation regarding the stage of glaucoma
- Do not confuse seventh character '4' (indeterminate) with seventh character '0' (unspecified)

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly indicate that the stage cannot be determined to justify character '4'
- Absence of any stage documentation requires the use of character '0' rather than '4'

### 31. Sepsis Coding Sequencing Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature of Sepsis Coding Sequencing Rules with nearly identical behavioral definitions and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `67_70_72`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If severe sepsis is present on admission, and meets the definition of principal diagnosis, the underlying systemic infection should be assigned as principal diagnosis followed by the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 as required by the sequencing rules in the Tabular List. A code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis. When severe sepsis develops during an encounter (it was not present on admission), the underlying systemic infection and the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should be assigned as secondary diagnoses.
>
> Severe sepsis may be present on admission, but the diagnosis may not be confirmed until sometime after admission. If the documentation is not clear whether severe sepsis was present on admission, the provider should be queried.
>
> For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9.
>
> For sepsis following a postprocedural wound (surgical site) infection, a code from T81.41 to T81.43, Infection following a procedure, T81.49, Infection following a procedure, other surgical site, or a code from O86.00 to O86.03, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, or code O86.09, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, other surgical site, that identifies the site of the infection should be sequenced first, if known. Assign an additional code for sepsis following a procedure (T81.44) or sepsis following an obstetrical procedure (O86.04). Use an additional code to identify the infectious agent. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with the additional code(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, a code from subcategory T80.2, Infections following infusion, transfusion, and therapeutic injection, or code T88.0-, Infection following immunization, should be coded first, followed by the code for the specific infection. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned, with the additional codes(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> In some cases, a noninfectious process (condition) such as trauma, may lead to an infection which can result in sepsis or severe sepsis. If sepsis or severe sepsis is documented as associated with a noninfectious condition, such as a burn or serious injury, and this condition meets the definition for principal diagnosis, the code for the noninfectious condition should be sequenced first, followed by the code for the resulting infection. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with any associated organ dysfunction(s) codes. It is not necessary to assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin, for these cases.
>
> If the infection meets the definition of principal diagnosis, it should be sequenced before the non-infectious condition. When both the associated non-infectious condition and the infection meet the definition of principal diagnosis, either may be assigned as principal diagnosis.
>
> Only one code from category R65, Symptoms and signs specifically associated with systemic inflammation and infection, should be assigned. Therefore, when a non-infectious condition leads to an infection resulting in severe sepsis, assign the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. Do not additionally assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of noninfectious origin.
>
> See Section I.C.18. SIRS due to non-infectious process

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis Coding Sequencing Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying systemic infection sequenced first if severe sepsis is present on admission and meets principal diagnosis definition
- Code from subcategory R65.2 assigned as secondary diagnosis when severe sepsis develops during an encounter
- Infection following a procedure code sequenced first if known for sepsis following a postprocedural wound
- Noninfectious condition code sequenced first if it meets principal diagnosis definition and leads to infection resulting in sepsis
- Only one code from category R65 assigned, specifically R65.2 for severe sepsis cases
- For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, code the specific infection category (T80.2 or T88.0-) first before the sepsis code
- When both a noninfectious condition and the resulting infection meet the definition of principal diagnosis, either may be assigned as the principal diagnosis

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Severe sepsis must meet the definition of principal diagnosis to be sequenced first with underlying infection
- Provider must be queried if documentation is unclear whether severe sepsis was present on admission
- Code from subcategory R65.2 cannot be assigned as a principal diagnosis
- Additional codes must be assigned for any acute organ dysfunction associated with severe sepsis
- Infectious agent must be identified with an additional code when applicable
- Use an additional code to identify the infectious agent when applicable for sepsis cases
- Assign additional codes for any acute organ dysfunction associated with severe sepsis

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin, when noninfectious condition leads to infection resulting in severe sepsis
- Assigning code from subcategory R65.2 as a principal diagnosis
- Failing to sequence infection following a procedure code first when sepsis follows a postprocedural wound infection

**New in this version:**
- Explicit rule that code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis
- Mandatory sequencing of infection following a procedure code before sepsis code when site of infection is known
- Requirement to assign additional codes for acute organ dysfunction alongside severe sepsis codes
- Clarification that only one code from category R65 should be assigned regardless of etiology
- Guidance to query provider when documentation is unclear regarding presence of severe sepsis on admission

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `495_498_500`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If severe sepsis is present on admission, and meets the definition of principal diagnosis, the underlying systemic infection should be assigned as principal diagnosis followed by the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 as required by the sequencing rules in the Tabular List. A code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis. When severe sepsis develops during an encounter (it was not present on admission), the underlying systemic infection and the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should be assigned as secondary diagnoses.
>
> Severe sepsis may be present on admission, but the diagnosis may not be confirmed until sometime after admission. If the documentation is not clear whether severe sepsis was present on admission, the provider should be queried.
>
> For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9.
>
> For sepsis following a postprocedural wound (surgical site) infection, a code from T81.41 to T81.43, Infection following a procedure, T81.49, Infection following a procedure, other surgical site, or a code from O86.00 to O86.03, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, or code O86.09, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, other surgical site, that identifies the site of the infection should be sequenced first, if known. Assign an additional code for sepsis following a procedure (T81.44) or sepsis following an obstetrical procedure (O86.04). Use an additional code to identify the infectious agent. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with the additional code(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, a code from subcategory T80.2, Infections following infusion, transfusion, and therapeutic injection, or code T88.0-, Infection following immunization, should be coded first, followed by the code for the specific infection. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned, with the additional codes(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> In some cases, a noninfectious process (condition) such as trauma, may lead to an infection which can result in sepsis or severe sepsis. If sepsis or severe sepsis is documented as associated with a noninfectious condition, such as a burn or serious injury, and this condition meets the definition for principal diagnosis, the code for the noninfectious condition should be sequenced first, followed by the code for the resulting infection. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with any associated organ dysfunction(s) codes. It is not necessary to assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin, for these cases.
>
> If the infection meets the definition of principal diagnosis, it should be sequenced before the non-infectious condition. When both the associated non-infectious condition and the infection meet the definition of principal diagnosis, either may be assigned as principal diagnosis.
>
> Only one code from category R65, Symptoms and signs specifically associated with systemic inflammation and infection, should be assigned. Therefore, when a non-infectious condition leads to an infection resulting in severe sepsis, assign the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. Do not additionally assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of noninfectious origin.
>
> See Section I.C.18. SIRS due to non-infectious process
>
> - 7) Sepsis and septic shock complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sepsis and septic shock complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> - 8) Newborn sepsis
>
> See Section I.C.16. f. Bacterial sepsis of Newborn
>
> - 9) Hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis
>
> If the reason for admission is hemolytic-uremic syndrome that is associated with sepsis, assign code D59.31, Infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome, as the principal diagnosis. Codes for the underlying systemic infection and any other conditions (such as severe sepsis) should be assigned as secondary diagnoses .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis Coding Sequencing and R65.2 Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying systemic infection is sequenced first if severe sepsis is present on admission and meets principal diagnosis criteria.
- Code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis.
- If severe sepsis develops during an encounter, the underlying infection and R65.2 code are assigned as secondary diagnoses.
- For infection following a procedure, the procedure-related infection code is sequenced first, followed by sepsis code T81.44 or O86.04, then R65.2 if severe sepsis is present.
- Only one code from category R65 should be assigned; do not additionally assign R65.1 for non-infectious conditions leading to sepsis.
- For sepsis following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, code the specific complication (T80.2 or T88.0-) first, followed by the specific infection code.
- When a noninfectious condition leads to an infection resulting in sepsis, sequence the noninfectious condition first if it meets principal diagnosis criteria, followed by the infection code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Severe sepsis must be documented as present on admission to be sequenced with the underlying infection as principal diagnosis.
- Documentation must be clear regarding whether severe sepsis was present on admission; if unclear, the provider must be queried.
- The underlying systemic infection must be identified and coded when severe sepsis is present.
- Additional codes must be assigned for any acute organ dysfunction associated with severe sepsis.
- For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome, code D59.31 is the principal diagnosis, with infection and severe sepsis as secondary.
- An additional code must be used to identify the infectious agent when coding sepsis following a procedure or infusion/transfusion.
- Documentation must clearly establish whether severe sepsis was present on admission or developed during the encounter.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a code from subcategory R65.2 as a principal diagnosis.
- Assigning both R65.1 and R65.2 when a non-infectious condition leads to sepsis.
- Sequencing the sepsis code before the underlying infection or procedure-related infection code when applicable.
- Failing to query the provider when documentation is unclear about the presence of severe sepsis on admission.
- Omitting codes for acute organ dysfunction when severe sepsis is present.
- Assigning a code from subcategory R65.1 (SIRS of non-infectious origin) when a noninfectious condition leads to an infection resulting in severe sepsis.
- Sequencing the sepsis code before the underlying infection or the procedure-related infection code when applicable.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit rule that R65.2 cannot be a principal diagnosis.
- Mandatory sequencing of procedure-related infection codes (T81.41-T81.43, T81.49, O86.00-O86.03) before sepsis codes.
- Requirement to assign D59.31 as principal diagnosis for infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
- Clarification that R65.1 is not necessary when a non-infectious condition leads to sepsis.
- Guidance to query providers if severe sepsis status on admission is unclear.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The two versions present the exact same clinical logic regarding the sequencing of underlying infections, severe sepsis (R65.2), and noninfectious conditions. The 'New Version' profile includes specific code ranges (e.g., T81.41-T81.43) and a specific exception for hemolytic-uremic syndrome (D59.31) that are not explicitly detailed in the 'Older' profile's extracted behaviors, but these are clarifications of the same underlying rule rather than a change in the rule itself. The core instruction—that R65.2 cannot be a principal diagnosis and that infection codes must precede sepsis codes—remains unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): General mandate to sequence infection codes before sepsis codes and prohibit R65.2 as principal -> icd (New Version): Specific mandate to sequence T81.41-T81.43 or O86.00-O86.03 before sepsis codes, with explicit inclusion of D59.31 as principal for hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Prohibits R65.2 as principal diagnosis -> icd (New Version): Prohibits R65.2 as principal diagnosis and explicitly deprecates the use of R65.1 for non-infectious conditions leading to sepsis.

*Traceability: Chunk `67_70_72` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `495_498_500` (icd (New Version))*

### 32. Burn Injury Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Burn Injury Coding Guidelines feature with identical behavioral rules and logic.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `292_294`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
>
> - a. When the reason for the admission or encounter is for treatment of external multiple burns, sequence first the code that reflects the burn of the highest degree.
> - b. When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis or first-listed diagnosis.
> - c. When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and other related conditions such as smoke inhalation and/or respiratory failure, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
>
> Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Burn Injury Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
- When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and related conditions like smoke inhalation, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
- Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the degree of burn to determine the highest degree for sequencing.
- Clinical documentation must clarify whether the admission reason is for external multiple burns, internal burns, or related conditions.
- Documentation must distinguish between healing and non-healing status of the burn.
- Documentation must identify if necrosis is present in the burned skin.
- The circumstances of admission must be clearly documented to determine the principal diagnosis when multiple conditions exist.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `719_721`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
>
> - a. When the reason for the admission or encounter is for treatment of external multiple burns, sequence first the code that reflects the burn of the highest degree.
> - b. When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> - c. When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and other related conditions such as smoke inhalation and/or respiratory failure, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
>
> Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Feature:** Burn Injury Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
- When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and related conditions like smoke inhalation, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
- Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the degree of burn to determine the highest degree for sequencing.
- Clinical documentation must clarify whether the admission reason is for external multiple burns or combined internal/external burns.
- Documentation must distinguish between non-healing burns and necrosis of burned skin for accurate coding.

### 33. MRSA Infection and Carrier Coding
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for MRSA infections and carrier status with no behavioral differences.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `76_78`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When there is documentation of a current infection (e.g., wound infection, stitch abscess, urinary tract infection) due to MRSA, and that infection does not have a combination code that includes the causal organism, assign the appropriate code to identify the condition along with code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere for the MRSA infection. Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins.
>
> If a patient is documented as having both MRSA colonization and infection during a hospital admission, code Z22.322, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, and a code for the MRSA infection may both be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MRSA Infection and Carrier Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the appropriate infection code along with code B95.62 for MRSA when no combination code exists for the causal organism.
- Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11 for resistance to penicillins in this context.
- Code Z22.322 and a code for MRSA infection may both be assigned if a patient has both colonization and infection during a hospital admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm a current infection due to MRSA (e.g., wound infection, stitch abscess, urinary tract infection).
- The infection must lack a combination code that includes the causal organism to require the use of B95.62.
- Both colonization and infection must be documented during the same hospital admission to assign Z22.322 alongside an infection code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a code from subcategory Z16.11 (Resistance to penicillins) for MRSA infections.
- Failing to assign code B95.62 when the infection lacks a specific combination code for the causal organism.

**New in this version:**
- Use of code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection, as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere.
- Explicit permission to assign both Z22.322 (Carrier) and an infection code for patients with documented MRSA colonization and infection.

### 34. Septic Shock Coding Sequencing
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature 'Septic Shock Coding Sequencing' with matching behavioral rules and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `66_71`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Septic shock generally refers to circulatory failure associated with severe sepsis, and therefore, it represents a type of acute organ dysfunction.
>
> For cases of septic shock, the code for the systemic infection should be sequenced first, followed by code R65.21, Severe sepsis with septic shock or code T81.12, Postprocedural septic shock. Any additional codes for the other acute organ dysfunctions should also be assigned. As noted in the sequencing instructions in the Tabular List, the code for septic shock cannot be assigned as a principal diagnosis.
>
> If a postprocedural infection has resulted in postprocedural septic shock, assign the codes indicated above for sepsis due to a postprocedural infection, followed by code T81.12-, Postprocedural septic shock. Do not assign code R65.21, Severe sepsis with septic shock. Additional code(s) should be assigned for any acute organ dysfunction.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Septic Shock Coding Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Systemic infection code must be sequenced first, followed by R65.21 or T81.12
- Septic shock code cannot be assigned as a principal diagnosis
- Additional codes for acute organ dysfunctions must be assigned
- For postprocedural septic shock, assign sepsis due to postprocedural infection followed by T81.12
- Do not assign R65.21 when coding postprocedural septic shock

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm circulatory failure associated with severe sepsis
- Systemic infection etiology must be identified for sequencing
- Acute organ dysfunction must be documented to support additional codes
- Postprocedural infection must be linked to the septic shock event
- Principal diagnosis must reflect the underlying infection, not the shock state

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning septic shock as the principal diagnosis
- Using R65.21 for postprocedural septic shock cases
- Omitting codes for acute organ dysfunctions in septic shock
- Sequencing the shock code before the systemic infection code
- Failing to specify postprocedural infection etiology when applicable

**New in this version:**
- Explicit sequencing rule requiring infection code before R65.21 or T81.12
- Mandatory exclusion of R65.21 in postprocedural septic shock scenarios
- Requirement to code all associated acute organ dysfunctions
- Clarification that septic shock is a type of acute organ dysfunction
- Specific instruction to use T81.12 for postprocedural septic shock

### 35. Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines with nearly identical behavioral definitions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `37_334`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A sequela is the residual effect (condition produced) after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated. There is no time limit on when a sequela code can be used. The residual may be apparent early, such as in cerebral infarction, or it may occur months or years later, such as that due to a previous injury. Examples of sequela include: scar formation resulting from a burn, deviated septum due to a nasal fracture, and infertility due to tubal occlusion from old tuberculosis. Coding of sequela generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first. The sequela code is sequenced second.
>
> An exception to the above guidelines are those instances where the code for the sequela is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List and title, or the sequela code has been expanded (at the fourth, fifth or sixth character levels) to include the manifestation(s). The code for the acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.
>
> See Section I.C.9. Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sequelae of complication of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.19. Application of 7 th characters for Chapter 19
>
> Sequela are reported using the external cause code with the 7 th character 'S' for sequela. These codes should be used with any report of a late effect or sequela resulting from a previous injury.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequela (Late Effects)
>
> - Sequela external cause code with a related current injury A sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.
> - Use of sequela external cause codes for subsequent visits
>
> Use a late effect external cause code for subsequent visits when a late effect of the initial injury is being treated. Do not use a late effect external cause code for subsequent visits for follow-up care (e.g., to assess healing, to receive rehabilitative therapy) of the injury when no late effect of the injury has been documented.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- A sequela is the residual effect after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated with no time limit on usage.
- Coding generally requires two codes sequenced: the condition or nature of the sequela first, followed by the sequela code second.
- Sequela external cause codes must use the 7th character 'S' and are used with any report of a late effect or sequela resulting from a previous injury.
- For subsequent visits treating a late effect, use a late effect external cause code; do not use for follow-up care without documented late effects.
- A sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.
- An exception exists where the sequela code is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List title.
- Sequela codes may be expanded at the fourth, fifth, or sixth character levels to include specific manifestations.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must clearly indicate the residual effect is a result of a terminated acute phase of illness or injury.
- The condition or nature of the sequela must be documented to be sequenced first in the claim.
- External cause codes must include the 7th character 'S' to denote sequela status.
- Late effect external cause codes are mandatory for subsequent visits where a late effect is being treated.
- Current nature of injury codes are prohibited when paired with sequela external cause codes.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using the code for the acute phase of an illness or injury alongside a code for the late effect.
- Using a sequela external cause code with a related current nature of injury code.
- Using a late effect external cause code for subsequent visits when no late effect of the injury has been documented.
- Assigning unspecified laterality codes when documentation does not specify the side.
- Using sequela codes without sequencing the condition or nature of the sequela first.

**New in this version:**
- Expanded sequela codes at the fourth, fifth, or sixth character levels that include specific manifestations.
- Mandatory use of 7th character 'S' for external cause codes related to sequela.
- Specific guidelines for sequencing cerebrovascular disease sequelae and pregnancy complication sequelae.
- Requirement to query providers if laterality is unspecified before assigning codes.
- Distinction between treating a late effect versus follow-up care for injury assessment.
- Specific guidelines exist for sequencing cerebrovascular disease sequelae.
- Specific guidelines exist for sequencing sequelae of pregnancy complications.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `469`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A sequela is the residual effect (condition produced) after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated. There is no time limit on when a sequela code can be used. The residual may be apparent early, such as in cerebral infarction, or it may occur months or years later, such as that due to a previous injury. Examples of sequela include: scar formation resulting from a burn, deviated septum due to a nasal fracture, and infertility due to tubal occlusion from old tuberculosis. Coding of sequela generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first. The sequela code is sequenced second.
>
> An exception to the above guidelines are those instances where the code for the sequela is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List and title, or the sequela code has been expanded (at the fourth, fifth or sixth character levels) to include the manifestation(s). The code for the acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.
>
> See Section I.C.9. Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sequelae of complication of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.19. Application of 7 th characters for Chapter 19

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- A sequela is defined as the residual effect produced after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated.
- There is no time limit on when a sequela code can be used.
- Coding generally requires two codes sequenced with the condition or nature of the sequela first, followed by the sequela code second.
- The code for the acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.
- Exceptions exist where the sequela code is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List or title.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must clearly identify the residual effect as a condition produced after the acute phase has terminated.
- Documentation must support the specific nature of the sequela (e.g., scar formation, deviated septum, infertility).
- Two codes must be assigned in the correct sequence: condition/nature first, sequela second.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using a code for the acute phase of an illness or injury in conjunction with a code for the late effect.
- Assigning a sequela code without first coding the condition or nature of the sequela.

**New in this version:**

- Reference to Section I.C.15 for sequelae of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium.
- Reference to Section I.C.19 regarding the application of 7th characters for Chapter 19.

This indicates the newer version is an administrative update that consolidates references rather than altering the coding rules themselves.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly mandates the use of 7th character 'S' for external cause codes and requires querying providers for unspecified laterality. -> icd (New Version): References Section I.C.19 regarding the application of 7th characters and Section I.C.9/I.C.15 for specific guidelines without restating the character or query rules in the main text.

### 36. Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines, specifically focusing on the requirements for code assignment, organism documentation, and provider queries.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `64_65`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For a diagnosis of sepsis, assign the appropriate code for the underlying systemic infection. If the type of infection or causal organism is not further specified, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism.
>
> A code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, should not be assigned unless severe sepsis or an associated acute organ dysfunction is documented.
>
> - (i) Negative or inconclusive blood cultures and sepsis Negative or inconclusive blood cultures do not preclude a diagnosis of sepsis in patients with clinical evidence of the condition; however, the provider should be queried.
> - (ii) Urosepsis
>
> The term urosepsis is a nonspecific term. It is not to be considered synonymous with sepsis. It has no default code in the Alphabetic Index. Should a provider use this term, he/she must be queried for clarification.
>
> - (iii) Sepsis with organ dysfunction
>
> If a patient has sepsis and associated acute organ dysfunction or multiple organ dysfunction (MOD), follow the instructions for coding severe sepsis.
>
> - (iv) Acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis
>
> If a patient has sepsis and an acute organ dysfunction, but the medical record documentation indicates that the acute organ dysfunction is related to a medical condition other than the sepsis, do not assign a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. An acute organ dysfunction must be associated with the sepsis in order to assign the severe sepsis code. If the documentation is not clear as to whether an acute organ dysfunction is related to the sepsis or another medical condition, query the provider.
>
> The coding of severe sepsis requires a minimum of 2 codes: first a code for the underlying systemic infection, followed by a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. If the causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism, for the infection. Additional code(s) for the associated acute organ dysfunction are also required.
>
> Due to the complex nature of severe sepsis, some cases may require querying the provider prior to assignment of the codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code A41.9 for unspecified organism if the causal organism is not further specified.
- Do not assign a code from subcategory R65.2 unless severe sepsis or an associated acute organ dysfunction is documented.
- Query the provider if blood cultures are negative or inconclusive but clinical evidence of sepsis exists.
- Query the provider if the term 'urosepsis' is used, as it is nonspecific and has no default code.
- Query the provider if the relationship between acute organ dysfunction and sepsis is unclear.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- A code from subcategory R65.2 requires documentation of severe sepsis or an associated acute organ dysfunction.
- Coding of severe sepsis requires a minimum of 2 codes: one for the underlying systemic infection and one from subcategory R65.2.
- Additional codes for associated acute organ dysfunction are mandatory when present.
- An acute organ dysfunction must be clearly associated with the sepsis to assign the severe sepsis code.
- If the causal organism is not documented, code A41.9 must be assigned for the infection.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a code from subcategory R65.2 without documented severe sepsis or acute organ dysfunction.
- Considering 'urosepsis' synonymous with sepsis without provider clarification.
- Assigning severe sepsis codes when acute organ dysfunction is related to a condition other than sepsis.
- Assigning severe sepsis codes without the required minimum of 2 codes (infection + R65.2).
- Failing to query the provider when documentation regarding organ dysfunction association is unclear.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to query the provider for negative or inconclusive blood cultures in the presence of clinical sepsis evidence.
- Mandatory assignment of code A41.9 when the causal organism is not documented.
- Clarification that 'urosepsis' is not synonymous with sepsis and requires provider clarification.
- Requirement to document the association between acute organ dysfunction and sepsis before coding.
- Mandatory inclusion of additional codes for associated acute organ dysfunction in severe sepsis cases.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `493`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The coding of severe sepsis requires a minimum of 2 codes: first a code for the underlying systemic infection, followed by a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. If the causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism, for the infection. Additional code(s) for the associated acute organ dysfunction are also required.
>
> Due to the complex nature of severe sepsis, some cases may require querying the provider prior to assignment of the codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a minimum of 2 codes: first the underlying systemic infection, followed by a code from subcategory R65.2 for severe sepsis.
- If the causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism, for the infection.
- Include additional code(s) for the associated acute organ dysfunction.
- Query the provider prior to assignment if the case is complex or documentation is unclear.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must identify the underlying systemic infection.
- Documentation must identify the associated acute organ dysfunction.
- If the organism is unknown, code A41.9 must be used for the infection component.
- At least two codes must be assigned to represent severe sepsis correctly.
- Provider query is mandatory if clinical details are insufficient for code assignment.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided specific, actionable instructions for querying providers regarding negative blood cultures and the nonspecific term 'urosepsis.' The newer version generalizes these instructions into a broader mandate to query providers if the case is complex or documentation is unclear, removing the specific clinical examples while maintaining the requirement for a minimum of two codes.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Query the provider if blood cultures are negative or inconclusive but clinical evidence of sepsis exists. -> icd (New Version): Query the provider prior to assignment if the case is complex or documentation is unclear.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Query the provider if the term 'urosepsis' is used, as it is nonspecific and has no default code. -> icd (New Version): Query the provider prior to assignment if the case is complex or documentation is unclear.

*Traceability: Chunk `64_65` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `493` (icd (New Version))*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `247`

> **Actual Text**
>
> This code, like all sequela codes, is to be sequenced following the code describing the sequelae of the complication.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sequela Code Sequencing Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequela codes must be sequenced following the code describing the sequelae of the complication.
- The sequela code is treated as a secondary diagnosis relative to the complication code.
- Coding order is determined by the causal relationship between the complication and its sequela.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly link the current condition as a sequela of a prior complication.
- The complication code must be present and valid to support the sequela code assignment.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Sequela codes being sequenced before the complication code.
- Assigning sequela codes without a documented antecedent complication.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory sequencing rule requiring the complication code to precede the sequela code in the claim.

### 39. Sepsis and Localized Infection Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical sequencing rules for sepsis and localized infections with no substantive behavioral changes between versions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `68`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the reason for admission is sepsis or severe sepsis and a localized infection, such as pneumonia or cellulitis, a code(s) for the underlying systemic infection should be assigned first and the code for the localized infection should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis. If the patient has severe sepsis, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned as a secondary diagnosis. If the patient is admitted with a localized infection, such as pneumonia, and sepsis/severe sepsis doesn't develop until after admission, the localized infection should be assigned first, followed by the appropriate sepsis/severe sepsis codes.
>
> For hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9 .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis and Localized Infection Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for underlying systemic infection first if admission is for sepsis/severe sepsis with a localized infection
- Assign code for localized infection as a secondary diagnosis when sepsis is the primary admission reason
- Assign code from subcategory R65.2 as a secondary diagnosis if the patient has severe sepsis
- Assign localized infection code first if sepsis/severe sepsis develops after admission
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.d.9 for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must distinguish between admission for sepsis versus sepsis developing post-admission
- Documentation must specify if sepsis is severe to trigger R65.2 code assignment
- Underlying systemic infection must be identified to assign the primary code correctly

---

If the patient is admitted with a localized infection, such as pneumonia, and sepsis/severe sepsis doesn't develop until after admission, the localized infection should be assigned first, followed by the appropriate sepsis/severe sepsis codes.
>
> For hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9 .

- Assign code from subcategory R65.2 as a secondary diagnosis if the patient has severe sepsis
- Assign localized infection code first if sepsis/severe sepsis develops after admission

### 41. MRSA Combination Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding logic for MRSA combination codes with identical behavioral rules and examples.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `75`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is diagnosed with an infection that is due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and that infection has a combination code that includes the causal organism (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia) assign the appropriate combination code for the condition (e.g., code A41.02, Sepsis due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus or code J15.212, Pneumonia due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus). Do not assign code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, as an additional code, because the combination code includes the type of infection and the MRSA organism. Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins, as an additional diagnosis.
>
> See Section C.1. for instructions on coding and sequencing of sepsis and severe sepsis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MRSA Combination Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the appropriate combination code for the condition (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia) that includes the causal organism.
- Do not assign code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, as an additional code.
- Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins, as an additional diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The infection must be diagnosed as due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).
- The condition must have a combination code that includes the causal organism.
- Sequencing must follow Section C.1. instructions for coding and sequencing of sepsis and severe sepsis.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code B95.62 as an additional code when a combination code is available.
- Assigning a code from subcategory Z16.11 as an additional diagnosis for MRSA infections.

**New in this version:**
- Use of specific combination codes like A41.02 for Sepsis due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus.
- Use of specific combination codes like J15.212 for Pneumonia due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus.

### 43. Puerperal Sepsis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines
