*Both profiles describe the identical coding rules for Puerperal Sepsis (Code O85), organism specification, and exclusions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `235`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code O85, Puerperal sepsis, should be assigned with a secondary code to identify the causal organism (e.g., for a bacterial infection, assign a code from category B95-B96, Bacterial infections in conditions classified elsewhere). A code from category A40, Streptococcal sepsis, or A41, Other sepsis, should not be used for puerperal sepsis. If applicable, use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and any associated acute organ dysfunction.
>
> Code O85 should not be assigned for sepsis following an obstetrical procedure (See Section I.C.1.d.5.b., Sepsis due to a postprocedural infection).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Puerperal Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code O85 with a secondary code to identify the causal organism from categories B95-B96
- Do not use category A40 or A41 codes for puerperal sepsis
- Use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and associated acute organ dysfunction if applicable
- Do not assign code O85 for sepsis following an obstetrical procedure

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must identify the causal organism to support the secondary B95-B96 code assignment
- Clinical documentation must confirm the sepsis is puerperal in nature and not post-procedural
- Documentation of severe sepsis or acute organ dysfunction is required to justify additional R65.2- codes

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using category A40 (Streptococcal sepsis) or A41 (Other sepsis) for puerperal sepsis cases
- Assigning code O85 for sepsis resulting from an obstetrical procedure

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of secondary codes from B95-B96 to specify the bacterial organism for O85
- Requirement to code severe sepsis (R65.2-) and acute organ dysfunction alongside O85 when clinically present

### 44. COVID-19 Principal Diagnosis Sequencing Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical sequencing logic for U07.1 with the same specific exceptions for obstetrics, sepsis, and transplant complications.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `81`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When COVID-19 meets the definition of principal diagnosis, code U07.1, COVID-19, should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate codes for associated manifestations, except when another guideline requires that certain codes be sequenced first, such as obstetrics, sepsis, or transplant complications.
>
> For a COVID-19 infection that progresses to sepsis, see Section I.C.1.d. Sepsis, Severe Sepsis, and Septic Shock
>
> See Section I.C.15.s. for COVID-19 infection in pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.16.h. for COVID-19 infection in newborn
>
> For a COVID-19 infection in a lung transplant patient, see Section I.C.19.g.3.a. Transplant complications other than kidney.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Principal Diagnosis Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code U07.1 (COVID-19) should be sequenced first when it meets the definition of principal diagnosis.
- Associated manifestation codes follow the principal COVID-19 code unless other guidelines dictate otherwise.
- Obstetrics, sepsis, and transplant complications take precedence over COVID-19 sequencing.
- Refer to specific sections for COVID-19 in pregnancy, newborns, and lung transplant patients.
- Sepsis progression requires adherence to specific sepsis sequencing guidelines.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- COVID-19 must meet the clinical definition of principal diagnosis to be sequenced first.
- Other specific guidelines (e.g., obstetrics, sepsis) must be evaluated before finalizing sequence.
- Documentation must support the progression to sepsis if applicable.
- Pregnancy and newborn status must be documented to trigger specific section references.
- Transplant status must be confirmed to apply transplant complication sequencing rules.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit sequencing rule placing U07.1 first for principal diagnoses.
- Mandatory reference to obstetrics, sepsis, and transplant exception guidelines.
- Specific cross-references for pregnancy, childbirth, puerperium, and newborn infections.
- Dedicated reference for lung transplant patient complications.
- Integration of sepsis progression logic into the sequencing workflow.

>
> Certain infections are classified in chapters other than Chapter 1 and no organism is identified as part of the infection code. In these instances, it is necessary to use an additional code from Chapter 1 to identify the organism. A code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, or B97, Viral agents as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, is to be used as an additional code to identify the organism. An instructional note will be found at the infection code advising that an additional organism code is required.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Additional Organism Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use an additional code from Chapter 1 to identify the organism when the infection code in other chapters does not include the organism.
- Select codes from category B95 (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus), B96 (Other bacterial agents), or B97 (Viral agents) as additional codes.
- Follow the instructional note found at the infection code advising that an additional organism code is required.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The infection must be classified in a chapter other than Chapter 1.
- The primary infection code must not identify the organism as part of the code definition.
- An instructional note at the infection code must explicitly advise that an additional organism code is required.

### 46. Hypertensive Disease Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles address Hypertensive Disease Coding Guidelines, with the new version expanding the scope to include specific rules for coding hypertension combined with heart conditions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `172_173`

> **Actual Text**
>
> This diagnostic statement usually refers to an existing state of hypertension under control by therapy. Assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.
>
> Uncontrolled hypertension may refer to untreated hypertension or hypertension not responding to current therapeutic regimen. In either case, assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertensive Disease Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for existing hypertension under control by therapy.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for uncontrolled hypertension, whether untreated or not responding to therapy.
- Differentiate between controlled and uncontrolled states based on therapeutic response documentation.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must specify the status of hypertension (controlled vs. uncontrolled).
- Documentation must indicate if the condition is untreated or not responding to the current therapeutic regimen.
- Codes must be selected strictly from categories I10 through I15.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `593_600_601`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Hypertension with heart conditions classified to I50.-, Heart failure, I51.4, Myocarditis, unspecified, I51.89, Other ill-defined heart diseases, and I51.9, Heart disease, unspecified, is assigned to a code from category I11, Hypertensive heart disease. Use additional code(s) from category I50, Heart failure, or I51, Complications and ill-defined descriptions of heart disease, to identify the heart condition.
>
> Hypertension with heart conditions classified to I51.5, Myocardial degeneration, or I51.7, Cardiomegaly, is assigned to a code from category I11, Hypertensive heart disease. No additional code is assigned to identify the specific heart condition.
>
> The same heart conditions (I50.-, I51.4-I51.7, I51.89, I51.9) with hypertension are coded separately if the provider has documented they are unrelated to the hypertension. The applicable hypertension code I10, Essential (primary) hypertension, or a code from category I15, Secondary hypertension, should be assigned. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
>
> This diagnostic statement usually refers to an existing state of hypertension under control by therapy. Assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.
>
> Uncontrolled hypertension may refer to untreated hypertension or hypertension not responding to current therapeutic regimen. In either case, assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertensive Disease Coding with Heart Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code from category I11 for hypertension with heart conditions classified to I50.-, I51.4, I51.89, or I51.9, and use additional codes from I50 or I51 to identify the heart condition.
- Assign code from category I11 for hypertension with heart conditions classified to I51.5 or I51.7 without assigning an additional code for the specific heart condition.
- Code hypertension and heart conditions separately if documented as unrelated, sequencing based on admission circumstances.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for existing states of hypertension under control by therapy.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for uncontrolled hypertension, whether untreated or not responding to therapy.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must document the relationship between hypertension and heart conditions to determine if they are related or unrelated.
- Documentation must specify if hypertension is under control, uncontrolled, untreated, or not responding to therapy.
- Specific heart condition codes (I50.-, I51.4-I51.9) must be identified to determine if an additional code is required alongside I11.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning an additional code from category I50 or I51 when the heart condition is classified as I51.5 (Myocardial degeneration) or I51.7 (Cardiomegaly) in the presence of hypertension.
- Coding hypertension and heart conditions as a single code when the provider has documented them as unrelated.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to sequence codes based on the circumstances of the admission or encounter when coding unrelated hypertension and heart conditions.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Definition Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version focused primarily on distinguishing between controlled and uncontrolled hypertension using categories I10-I15. The new version expands this definition by introducing specific logic for 'Hypertensive Disease Coding with Heart Conditions,' detailing when to use category I11 versus separate coding for unrelated conditions. It explicitly defines scenarios where additional codes from I50 or I51 are required versus when they are deprecated, adding granular clinical logic absent in the older text.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for existing hypertension under control by therapy or uncontrolled states. -> icd (New Version): Assign code from category I11 for hypertension with heart conditions classified to I50.-, I51.4, I51.89, or I51.9, and use additional codes from I50 or I51 to identify the heart condition.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Differentiate between controlled and uncontrolled states based on therapeutic response documentation. -> icd (New Version): Code hypertension and heart conditions separately if documented as unrelated, sequencing based on admission circumstances, while deprecating the assignment of additional codes for I51.5 or I51.7 in the presence of hypertension.

*Traceability: Chunk `172_173` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `593_600_601` (icd (New Version))*

### 47. Diabetes Mellitus Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Diabetes Mellitus coding guidelines, specifically focusing on combination codes, sequencing, and Z79 medication usage rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `129_132_135`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes that include the type of diabetes mellitus, the body system affected, and the complications affecting that body system. As many codes within a particular category as are necessary to describe all of the complications of the disease may be used. They should be sequenced based on the reason for a particular encounter. Assign as many codes from categories E08 - E13 as needed to identify all of the associated conditions that the patient has.
>
> If the type of diabetes mellitus is not documented in the medical record the default is E11.-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
>
> - 3) Diabetes mellitus and the use of insulin, oral hypoglycemics, and injectable non-insulin drugs
>
> If the documentation in a medical record does not indicate the type of diabetes but does indicate that the patient uses insulin, code E11-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, should be assigned. Additional code(s) should be assigned from category Z79 to identify the long-term (current) use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin antidiabetic, as follows:
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both code Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and code Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, should be assigned.
>
> If the patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.
>
> - 4) Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy and gestational diabetes
>
> See Section I.C.15. Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy. See Section I.C.15. Gestational (pregnancy induced) diabetes
>
> Codes under categories E08, Diabetes mellitus due to underlying condition, E09, Drug or chemical induced diabetes mellitus, and E13, Other specified diabetes mellitus, identify complications/manifestations associated with secondary diabetes mellitus. Secondary diabetes is always caused by another condition or event (e.g., cystic fibrosis, malignant neoplasm of pancreas, pancreatectomy, adverse effect of drug, or poisoning).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Diabetes Mellitus Combination and Sequencing Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes that include the type of diabetes, the body system affected, and the complications affecting that body system.
- Codes should be sequenced based on the reason for a particular encounter.
- As many codes within a particular category as are necessary to describe all of the complications of the disease may be used.
- Assign as many codes from categories E08 - E13 as needed to identify all of the associated conditions that the patient has.
- If the type of diabetes mellitus is not documented in the medical record, the default is E11.-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must indicate the type of diabetes mellitus; if absent, E11.- is mandatory.
- Documentation must specify the use of insulin, oral hypoglycemics, or injectable non-insulin drugs to assign additional Z79 codes.
- Both Z79.4 and Z79.84 must be assigned if the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin.
- Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.
- Secondary diabetes mellitus codes (E08, E09, E13) require documentation of the underlying condition or event causing the diabetes.
- Documentation must specify the use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs to assign code Z79.85.
- Documentation must specify the use of oral hypoglycemic drugs to assign code Z79.84.

**New in this version:**
- Specific rules for assigning Z79 codes when patients are treated with combinations of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, and injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
- Mandatory assignment of Z79.85 for long-term use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs when combined with other treatments.
- Explicit prohibition of assigning Z79.4 for temporary insulin use in type 2 diabetes management during an encounter.
- Specific rules for assigning Z79.85 for long-term use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs when combined with other treatments.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `555_559`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes that include the type of diabetes mellitus, the body system affected, and the complications affecting that body system. As many codes within a particular category as are necessary to describe all of the complications of the disease may be used. They should be sequenced based on the reason for a particular encounter. Assign as many codes from categories E08 - E13 as needed to identify all of the associated conditions that the patient has.
>
> If the type of diabetes mellitus is not documented in the medical record the default is E11.-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
>
> - 3) Diabetes mellitus and the use of insulin, oral hypoglycemics, and injectable non-insulin drugs
>
> If the documentation in a medical record does not indicate the type of diabetes but does indicate that the patient uses insulin, code E11-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, should be assigned. Additional code(s) should be assigned from category Z79 to identify the long-term (current) use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin antidiabetic, as follows:
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both code Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and code Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, should be assigned.
>
> If the patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.
>
> - 4) Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy and gestational diabetes
>
> See Section I.C.15. Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy. See Section I.C.15. Gestational (pregnancy induced) diabetes

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Diabetes Mellitus Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes including type, body system affected, and complications, sequenced based on the reason for the encounter.
- If the type of diabetes is not documented, the default code E11.- (Type 2 diabetes mellitus) must be assigned.
- Additional codes from category Z79 must be assigned to identify long-term use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
- Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.
- As many codes from categories E08 - E13 as necessary should be assigned to identify all associated conditions the patient has.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the type of diabetes mellitus; if absent, E11.- is mandatory.
- Documentation must indicate the specific medications used (insulin, oral hypoglycemics, injectable non-insulin) to trigger Z79 codes.
- All associated conditions must be identified using codes from categories E08 - E13.
- Coding for temporary insulin use in type 2 diabetes is prohibited under Z79.4.
- Encounter reason must determine the sequencing of the primary diabetes code.
- If a patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both Z79.4 and Z79.84 must be assigned.
- If a patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, both Z79.4 and Z79.85 must be assigned.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning unspecified diabetes codes when the type is not documented.
- Omitting Z79 codes when long-term medication use is documented.
- Assigning Z79.4 for temporary insulin administration during an encounter.
- Failing to sequence codes based on the reason for the encounter.
- Using fewer than necessary codes from categories E08 - E13 to describe all complications.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to assign Z79.84 for long-term use of oral hypoglycemic drugs.
- Explicit requirement to assign Z79.85 for long-term use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
- Clarification that Z79.4 is excluded for temporary insulin use in type 2 diabetes.
- Mandate to use combination codes that integrate type, system, and complications.
- Guidance to query providers if diabetes type is missing rather than defaulting without documentation.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided general rules for assigning Z79 codes for insulin, oral hypoglycemics, and injectable non-insulin drugs but lacked explicit definitions for the specific Z79.84 and Z79.85 codes. The newer version clarifies these rules by explicitly requiring the assignment of Z79.84 for long-term oral hypoglycemic use and Z79.85 for long-term injectable non-insulin antidiabetic use, reducing coder variance. Both versions maintain the prohibition on using Z79.4 for temporary insulin use, but the newer version adds specific mandates for the other drug categories previously only implied.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): General instruction to assign Z79 codes for insulin, oral hypoglycemics, and injectable non-insulin drugs -> icd (New Version): Explicit requirement to assign Z79.84 for long-term oral hypoglycemic drugs and Z79.85 for long-term injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions assigning Z79 codes if patient uses insulin and oral hypoglycemics -> icd (New Version): Explicitly states both Z79.4 and Z79.84 must be assigned if treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin.

*Traceability: Chunk `129_132_135` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `555_559` (icd (New Version))*

### 48. Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding sequence for insulin pump failures (underdose/overdose) using T85.6 and T38.3 codes.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `133_134`

> **Actual Text**
>
> An underdose of insulin due to an insulin pump failure should be assigned to a code from subcategory T85.6, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, that specifies the type of pump malfunction, as the principal or first-listed code, followed by code T38.3X6-, Underdosing of insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs. Additional codes for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications due to the underdosing should also be assigned.
>
> The principal or first-listed code for an encounter due to an insulin pump malfunction resulting in an overdose of insulin, should also be T85.6-, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, followed by code T38.3X1-, Poisoning by insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs, accidental (unintentional).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code from subcategory T85.6 as the principal or first-listed code for insulin pump failure underdosing, followed by code T38.3X6-.
- Assign code from subcategory T85.6 as the principal or first-listed code for insulin pump failure overdose, followed by code T38.3X1-.
- Additional codes must be assigned for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications resulting from the underdosing or overdose.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the type of pump malfunction to select the correct code from subcategory T85.6.
- The encounter must be due to an insulin pump failure resulting in either an underdose or an overdose of insulin.
- Codes for diabetes mellitus and associated complications must be included if applicable to the underdosing or overdose event.

---

> **Actual Text**
>
> - (a) Underdose of insulin due to insulin pump failure
>
> An underdose of insulin due to an insulin pump failure should be assigned to a code from subcategory T85.6, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, that specifies the type of pump malfunction, as the principal or first-listed code, followed by code T38.3X6-, Underdosing of insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs. Additional codes for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications due to the underdosing should also be assigned.
>
> The principal or first-listed code for an encounter due to an insulin pump malfunction resulting in an overdose of insulin, should also be T85.6-, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, followed by code T38.3X1-, Poisoning by insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs, accidental (unintentional).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol

- Assign code from subcategory T85.6 as the principal or first-listed code for underdose due to insulin pump failure, followed by code T38.3X6-.
- Assign code from subcategory T85.6 as the principal or first-listed code for overdose due to insulin pump malfunction, followed by code T38.3X1-.
- Additional codes must be assigned for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications resulting from the underdosing or overdosing.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the type of pump malfunction to select the correct code from subcategory T85.6.
- The encounter must be due to an insulin pump failure resulting in either an underdose or an overdose of insulin.
- Codes for diabetes mellitus and associated complications must be included to fully capture the clinical picture.

- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): An underdose of insulin due to an insulin pump failure should be assigned to a code from subcategory T85.6... followed by code T38.3X6- -> icd (New Version): - (a) Underdose of insulin due to insulin pump failure: An underdose of insulin due to an insulin pump failure should be assigned to a code from subcategory T85.6... followed by code T38.3X6-
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes for diabetes mellitus and associated complications must be included if applicable to the underdosing or overdose event. -> icd (New Version): Codes for diabetes mellitus and associated complications must be included to fully capture the clinical picture.

### 49. Resistant Hypertension Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the definition, documentation requirements, and coding sequence for resistant hypertension using code I1A.0.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `170_176`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Secondary hypertension is due to an underlying condition. Two codes are required: one to identify the underlying etiology and one from category I15 to identify the hypertension. Sequencing of codes is determined by the reason for admission/encounter.
>
> Resistant hypertension refers to blood pressure of a patient with hypertension that remains above goal in spite of the use of antihypertensive medications. Assign code I1A.0, Resistant hypertension, as an additional code when apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider. A code for the specific type of existing hypertension is sequenced first, if known.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Resistant Hypertension Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code I1A.0 as an additional code when apparent, treatment resistant, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider.
- Sequence a code for the specific type of existing hypertension first if known, followed by the resistant hypertension code.
- Require two codes for secondary hypertension: one for the underlying etiology and one from category I15.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must explicitly document 'apparent treatment resistant hypertension', 'treatment resistant hypertension', or 'true resistant hypertension'.
- Documentation must confirm blood pressure remains above goal despite the use of antihypertensive medications.
- For secondary hypertension, both the underlying etiology code and the category I15 code must be present.
- Sequencing of codes is determined by the reason for admission or encounter.

**New in this version:**
- Code I1A.0 is designated for resistant hypertension scenarios.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `604`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Resistant hypertension refers to blood pressure of a patient with hypertension that remains above goal in spite of the use of antihypertensive medications. Assign code I1A.0, Resistant hypertension, as an additional code when apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider. A code for the specific type of existing hypertension is sequenced first, if known.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Resistant Hypertension Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code I1A.0 as an additional code when apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider.
- Sequence a code for the specific type of existing hypertension first if known.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must document apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension.
- Patient must have hypertension with blood pressure remaining above goal despite use of antihypertensive medications.

**New in this version:**
- Code I1A.0 is designated for resistant hypertension scenarios.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version included specific sequencing instructions for secondary hypertension (requiring two codes for etiology and category I15), which are not present in the newer version's extracted profile. The newer version focuses strictly on the definition of resistant hypertension and the assignment of I1A.0 as an additional code. Since the core clinical logic for resistant hypertension remains unchanged and the difference lies in the scope of the extracted text rather than a modification of the rule itself, this is classified as an administrative update regarding the guideline's presentation.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes specific sequencing rules for secondary hypertension requiring two codes (etiology and category I15) -> icd (New Version): Omits secondary hypertension sequencing rules, focusing exclusively on resistant hypertension definition and I1A.0 assignment.

*Traceability: Chunk `170_176` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `604` (icd (New Version))*

### 50. I13 Hypertensive Heart and Kidney Disease Coding
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles explicitly describe the coding rules for I13 Hypertensive Heart and Kidney Disease, including the requirement for I12/N18 sequencing and the handling of acute renal failure.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `166_167`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign codes from category I12, Hypertensive chronic kidney disease, when both hypertension and a condition classifiable to category N18, Chronic kidney disease (CKD), are present. CKD should not be coded as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18 should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I12 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> If a patient has hypertensive chronic kidney disease and acute renal failure, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
>
> Assign codes from combination category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, when there is hypertension with both heart and kidney involvement. If heart failure is present, assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18, Chronic kidney disease, should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> The codes in category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, are combination codes that include hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease. The Includes note at I13 specifies that the conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13. If a patient has hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease, then a code from I13 should be used, not individual codes for hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease, or codes from I11 or I12.
>
> For patients with both acute renal failure and chronic kidney disease, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** I13 Hypertensive Heart and Kidney Disease Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from category I13 when hypertension is present with both heart and kidney involvement.
- Use the appropriate code from category N18 as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
- If heart failure is present, assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure.
- Do not use individual codes for hypertension, heart disease, and chronic kidney disease if a code from I13 is available.
- Code acute renal failure in addition to chronic kidney disease and sequence according to the circumstances of the admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Both hypertension and a condition classifiable to category N18 (Chronic kidney disease) must be present to assign I12.
- CKD should not be coded as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.
- A code from category I13 must be used instead of individual codes for hypertension, heart disease, and chronic kidney disease when all three are present.
- The Includes note at I13 specifies that conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13.
- Acute renal failure must be coded in addition to chronic kidney disease when both are present.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using individual codes for hypertension, heart disease, and chronic kidney disease when a combination code from I13 is available.
- Coding CKD as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.
- Failing to assign an additional code from category I50 when heart failure is present with hypertensive heart and kidney disease.
- Not sequencing acute renal failure according to the circumstances of the admission when both acute and chronic renal failure are present.
- Omitting the stage of chronic kidney disease when assigning a code from category I13.

**New in this version:**
- The requirement to use the appropriate code from category N18 as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
- The specification that the Includes note at I13 specifies that the conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13.
- The mandate to code acute renal failure in addition to chronic kidney disease when both are present.
- The instruction to sequence acute renal failure according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
- The clarification that CKD should not be coded as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.

> **Actual Text**

> Assign codes from category I12, Hypertensive chronic kidney disease, when both hypertension and a condition classifiable to category N18, Chronic kidney disease (CKD), are present. CKD should not be coded as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18 should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I12 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> If a patient has hypertensive chronic kidney disease and acute renal failure, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.

- Do not code CKD as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.

**Requirements / Properties:**

- Provider documentation must explicitly indicate the relationship between CKD and hypertension to avoid misclassification.

- Sequencing must reflect the primary reason for the admission or encounter.
- The stage of chronic kidney disease must be identified using the appropriate N18 code.

- Omitting the coding of acute renal failure when it is present alongside hypertensive chronic kidney disease.
- Failing to sequence acute renal failure according to the circumstances of the admission or encounter.
- Using unspecified codes for the stage of chronic kidney disease when specific N18 codes are available.

- Requirement to sequence acute renal failure based on admission or encounter circumstances.

### 55. Hypertension Comorbidity Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe identical coding rules for hypertension comorbidities with no behavioral differences.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `164`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The classification presumes a causal relationship between hypertension and heart involvement and between hypertension and kidney involvement, as the two conditions are linked by the term 'with' in the Alphabetic Index. These conditions should be coded as related even in the absence of provider documentation explicitly linking them, unless the documentation clearly states the conditions are unrelated.
>
> For hypertension and conditions not specifically linked by relational terms such as 'with,' 'associated with' or 'due to' in the classification, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as related.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertension Comorbidity Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Presume a causal relationship between hypertension and heart/kidney involvement if linked by the term 'with' in the Alphabetic Index, even without explicit provider documentation.
- Code hypertension and linked conditions as related unless documentation clearly states they are unrelated.
- Require explicit provider documentation linking conditions to code them as related when no relational terms (e.g., 'with', 'associated with') are present in the classification.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly link conditions to code them as related if the classification does not provide a relational term.
- Documentation must clearly state conditions are unrelated to override the presumption of a causal relationship.

---

### 58. Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69) with identical behavioral rules and default logic.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `179`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category I69 is used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits), themselves classified elsewhere. These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67. The neurologic deficits caused by cerebrovascular disease may be present from the onset or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
>
> Codes from category I69, Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease, that specify hemiplegia, hemiparesis and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected. Should the affected side be documented, but not specified as dominant or nondominant, and the classification system does not indicate a default, code selection is as follows:
>
> - For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant.
> - If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant.
> - If the right side is affected, the default is dominant.
> - 2) Codes from category I69 with codes from I60-I67
>
> Codes from category I69 may be assigned on a health care record with codes from I60-I67, if the patient has a current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease.
>
> - 3) Codes from category I69 and Personal history of transient ischemic attack (TIA) and cerebral infarction (Z86.73)
>
> Codes from category I69 should not be assigned if the patient does not have neurologic deficits.
>
> See Section I.C.21.4. History (of) for use of personal history codes

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes from category I69 indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67 as causes of sequela (neurologic deficits) classified elsewhere.
- Codes specifying hemiplegia, hemiparesis, and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected.
- If the affected side is documented but not specified as dominant or nondominant, default to dominant for ambidextrous patients, non-dominant for left side, and dominant for right side.
- Codes from category I69 may be assigned with codes from I60-I67 if the patient has current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease.
- Codes from category I69 should not be assigned if the patient does not have neurologic deficits.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The patient must have neurologic deficits to qualify for a code from category I69.
- The neurologic deficits must be caused by conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- Documentation must specify the affected side (dominant or nondominant) if possible to ensure accurate coding.
- Codes from I69 can be used alongside I60-I67 only when there is a mix of current and old cerebrovascular disease.
- Personal history codes (Z86.73) should not be combined with I69 codes for the same condition if active deficits exist.
- Codes from category I69 must not be assigned if the patient does not have neurologic deficits.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning I69 codes without documented neurologic deficits.
- Using unspecified laterality codes when side information is available.
- Combining I69 codes with Z86.73 for active deficits instead of using I69 with I60-I67.
- Failing to apply default rules for dominant/nondominant side when documentation is incomplete.
- Coding I69 for deficits that are an integral part of the initial disease process rather than late effects.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit default rules for assigning dominant or nondominant side when documentation is incomplete.
- Clarification on combining I69 with I60-I67 for patients with both current and old cerebrovascular disease.
- Mandatory exclusion of I69 codes if no neurologic deficits are present.
- Updated guidance on using personal history codes (Z86.73) in conjunction with I69.
- Enhanced emphasis on documenting the specific type of neurologic deficit for accurate I69 coding.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `607`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category I69 is used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits), themselves classified elsewhere. These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67. The neurologic deficits caused by cerebrovascular disease may be present from the onset or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
>
> Codes from category I69, Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease, that specify hemiplegia, hemiparesis and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected. Should the affected side be documented, but not specified as dominant or nondominant, and the classification system does not indicate a default, code selection is as follows:
>
> - For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant.
> - If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant.
> - If the right side is affected, the default is dominant.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes from category I69 are used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits) classified elsewhere.
- These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after the initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- If the affected side is documented but not specified as dominant or nondominant, default rules apply based on patient ambidexterity or side affected.
- For ambidextrous patients, the default side is dominant.
- If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant; if the right side is affected, the default is dominant.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The condition must be a sequela (neurologic deficit) persisting after the initial onset of a cerebrovascular disease.
- The underlying cause must be classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- The specific neurologic deficit must be documented as a late effect rather than an acute onset.
- If side is unspecified, the coder must apply the default rules (ambidextrous=dominant, left=non-dominant, right=dominant) if no default is indicated in the classification system.
- The code must specify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected when possible.
- The neurologic deficits may be present from the onset of the cerebrovascular disease or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition.

---

The older and newer versions present identical definitions for Category I69, including the rules for assigning dominant or nondominant sides when documentation is incomplete. Both versions explicitly state that deficits may be present from the onset or arise later, and both mandate the exclusion of I69 codes if no neurologic deficits exist. The text excerpts provided for both versions are nearly verbatim copies of each other, indicating no substantive change in clinical logic or coding behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes from category I69 may be assigned on a health care record with codes from I60-I67, if the patient has a current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease. -> icd (New Version): The neurologic deficits may be present from the onset of the cerebrovascular disease or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition.

### 59. Principal Diagnosis Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe identical Principal Diagnosis Coding Rules regarding respiratory failure sequencing and Chapter 18 symptom restrictions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `188_383`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Respiratory failure may be listed as a secondary diagnosis if it occurs after admission, or if it is present on admission, but does not meet the definition of principal diagnosis.
>
> Codes for symptoms, signs, and ill-defined conditions from Chapter 18 are not to be used as principal diagnosis when a related definitive diagnosis has been established.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Principal Diagnosis Selection and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Respiratory failure may be listed as a secondary diagnosis if it occurs after admission, or if it is present on admission, but does not meet the definition of principal diagnosis.
- Codes for symptoms, signs, and ill-defined conditions from Chapter 18 are not to be used as principal diagnosis when a related definitive diagnosis has been established.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Principal diagnosis must meet the definition of the condition that occasioned the admission or encounter.
- Definitive diagnoses must be established before assigning codes for symptoms or signs as principal diagnoses.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using symptoms, signs, or ill-defined conditions from Chapter 18 as principal diagnosis when a related definitive diagnosis exists.

### 60. COVID-19 Respiratory Infection Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding COVID-19 respiratory infection coding rules with matching behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `83_85`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For a patient with pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19, assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82, Pneumonia due to coronavirus disease 2019.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a lower respiratory infection, not otherwise specified (NOS), or an acute respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J22, Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection, should be assigned.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J98.8, Other specified respiratory disorders, should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Respiratory Infection Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82 for pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J22 for COVID-19 associated with lower respiratory infection NOS or acute respiratory infection NOS.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J98.8 for COVID-19 associated with respiratory infection NOS.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Pneumonia must be confirmed as due to COVID-19 to assign J12.82.
- Documentation must specify the relationship between COVID-19 and the respiratory infection type (e.g., pneumonia, NOS, acute).

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of code U07.1 for all documented COVID-19 cases.
- Specific code J12.82 introduced for pneumonia due to coronavirus disease 2019.
- Specific code J22 introduced for unspecified acute lower respiratory infection in the context of COVID-19.

### 61. Respiratory Failure Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles address the assignment of respiratory failure codes as principal diagnoses, with the new version adding specific definitions for acute exacerbations.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `187`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A code from subcategory J96.0, Acute respiratory failure, or subcategory J96.2, Acute and chronic respiratory failure, may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when it is the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital, and the selection is supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List. However, chapter- specific coding guidelines (such as obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) that provide sequencing direction take precedence.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Respiratory Failure Principal Diagnosis Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from subcategory J96.0 or J96.2 as principal diagnosis when the condition is chiefly responsible for hospital admission.
- Ensure selection is supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.
- Yield to chapter-specific coding guidelines (e.g., obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) that provide sequencing direction.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Condition must be established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission.
- Selection must be supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.
- Chapter-specific guidelines must take precedence over general respiratory failure guidelines.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `616_617`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The codes in categories J44 and J45 distinguish between uncomplicated cases and those in acute exacerbation. An acute exacerbation is a worsening or a decompensation of a chronic condition. An acute exacerbation is not equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition, though an exacerbation may be triggered by an infection.
>
> A code from subcategory J96.0, Acute respiratory failure, or subcategory J96.2, Acute and chronic respiratory failure, may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when it is the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital, and the selection is supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List. However, chapter- specific coding guidelines (such as obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) that provide sequencing direction take precedence.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Respiratory Failure Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in categories J44 and J45 distinguish between uncomplicated cases and those in acute exacerbation.
- An acute exacerbation is defined as a worsening or decompensation of a chronic condition, not equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition.
- Codes from subcategory J96.0 or J96.2 may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when chiefly responsible for hospital admission.
- Selection of principal diagnosis must be supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.
- Chapter-specific coding guidelines (e.g., obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) take precedence over general respiratory failure sequencing.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The condition must be established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital.
- The selection must be supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.
- Chapter-specific guidelines must be checked for precedence before assigning principal diagnosis.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding an acute exacerbation as equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the general rule for sequencing acute respiratory failure (J96.0/J96.2) as a principal diagnosis but lacked specific definitions for related chronic conditions. The newer version clarifies the distinction between uncomplicated cases and acute exacerbations in categories J44 and J45, explicitly stating that an exacerbation is a worsening of a chronic condition and not equivalent to a superimposed infection. This addition reduces coder variance by preventing the misclassification of exacerbations as simple infections, while maintaining the precedence of chapter-specific guidelines.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): General rule for sequencing J96.0/J96.2 as principal diagnosis without specific definitions for J44/J45 exacerbations. -> icd (New Version): Specific definition added that acute exacerbation in J44/J45 is a worsening of a chronic condition and is not equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): No mention of deprecated coding practices. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly deprecates coding an acute exacerbation as equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition.

*Traceability: Chunk `187` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `616_617` (icd (New Version))*

### 62. Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Coding Guidelines with matching behaviors and text.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `191`

> **Actual Text**
>
> As with all procedural or postprocedural complications, code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the procedure.
>
> Code J95.851, Ventilator associated pneumonia, should be assigned only when the provider has documented ventilator associated pneumonia (VAP). An additional code to identify the organism (e.g., Pseudomonas aeruginosa, code B96.5) should also be assigned. Do not assign an additional code from categories J12-J18 to identify the type of pneumonia.
>
> Code J95.851 should not be assigned for cases where the patient has pneumonia and is on a mechanical ventilator and the provider has not specifically stated that the pneumonia is ventilator-associated pneumonia. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a pneumonia that is a complication attributable to the mechanical ventilator, query the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the procedure.
- Code J95.851 should be assigned only when the provider has documented ventilator associated pneumonia (VAP).
- An additional code to identify the organism should also be assigned.
- Do not assign an additional code from categories J12-J18 to identify the type of pneumonia.
- If documentation is unclear regarding whether pneumonia is attributable to the mechanical ventilator, query the provider.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must specifically state that the pneumonia is ventilator-associated pneumonia.
- An additional code identifying the organism must be assigned.
- Documentation must establish the relationship between the condition and the procedure.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning additional codes from categories J12-J18 to identify the type of pneumonia.
- Assigning code J95.851 without specific provider documentation of VAP.

**New in this version:**
- Code J95.851 for Ventilator associated pneumonia.
- Mandatory assignment of an additional code to identify the organism (e.g., B96.5).

### 63. Vaping-Related Disorder Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the coding of vaping-related disorders, including the specific use of code U07.0 and the handling of respiratory versus gastrointestinal symptoms.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `193`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients presenting with condition(s) related to vaping, assign code U07.0, Vaping-related disorder, as the principal diagnosis. For lung injury due to vaping, assign only code U07.0. Assign additional codes for other manifestations, such as acute respiratory failure (subcategory J96.0-) or pneumonitis (code J68.0).
>
> Associated respiratory signs and symptoms due to vaping, such as cough, shortness of breath, etc., are not coded separately, when a definitive diagnosis has been established. However, it would be appropriate to code separately any gastrointestinal symptoms, such as diarrhea and abdominal pain.
>
> See Section I.C.1.g.1.c.i. for Pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Vaping-Related Disorder Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.0 as the principal diagnosis for patients presenting with conditions related to vaping.
- Assign only code U07.0 for lung injury due to vaping without additional codes for the injury itself.
- Code additional manifestations separately, such as acute respiratory failure (J96.0-) or pneumonitis (J68.0).
- Do not code associated respiratory signs and symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath) separately when a definitive diagnosis is established.
- Code gastrointestinal symptoms (e.g., diarrhea, abdominal pain) separately when present.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- A definitive diagnosis must be established before coding associated respiratory signs and symptoms as separate conditions.
- Code U07.0 must be used as the principal diagnosis for any condition related to vaping.
- Specific manifestation codes (e.g., J96.0-, J68.0) are required only for conditions other than the primary vaping-related disorder or lung injury.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding associated respiratory signs and symptoms separately when a definitive vaping-related diagnosis is established.
- Assigning multiple codes for lung injury due to vaping beyond code U07.0.

**New in this version:**
- Introduction of code U07.0 specifically for Vaping-related disorder.
- Mandatory assignment of U07.0 as the principal diagnosis for vaping-related conditions.
- Explicit guidance to exclude separate coding for respiratory symptoms when a definitive diagnosis exists.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `51`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only confirmed cases of HIV infection/illness. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H.
>
> In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of positive serology or culture for HIV; the provider's diagnostic statement that the patient is HIV positive or has an HIV-related illness is sufficient.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Inpatient Guideline Exception

**Key Behaviors:**

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient is admitted for an HIV-related condition, the principal diagnosis should be B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease followed by additional diagnosis codes for all reported HIV-related conditions.
>
> An exception to this guideline is if the reason for admission is hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV disease. Assign code D59.31, Infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome, followed by code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Admission Diagnosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**

- Code additional diagnoses for all reported HIV-related conditions
- Assign D59.31 followed by B20 for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV

**Requirements / Properties:**

- All reported HIV-related conditions must be coded as additional diagnoses
- Hemolytic-uremic syndrome must be explicitly associated with HIV disease

#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `486`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient is admitted for an HIV-related condition, the principal diagnosis should be B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease followed by additional diagnosis codes for all reported HIV-related conditions.
>
> An exception to this guideline is if the reason for admission is hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV disease. Assign code D59.31, Infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome, followed by code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease.
>
> - (c) Patient with HIV disease admitted for unrelated condition condition (such as a traumatic injury), the code for the unrelated condition (e.g., the nature of injury code) should be the principal
>
> If a patient with HIV disease is admitted for an unrelated diagnosis. Code B20 would be reported as a secondary diagnosis. Codes for other documented conditions should also be reported as secondary diagnoses.
>
> - (d) Patient newly diagnosed with HIV disease admissions/encounters for HIV conditions is irrelevant to the
>
> Whether the patient is newly diagnosed or has had previous sequencing decision.
>
> - (e) Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus When 'HIV positive,' 'HIV test positive,' or similar terminology is documented, and there is no documentation of symptoms or HIV-related illness, code Z21, Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] infection status, should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Admission Diagnosis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign B20 as principal diagnosis for HIV-related condition admissions, followed by additional HIV-related condition codes.
- Assign D59.31 followed by B20 if admission is for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV disease.
- Code unrelated conditions (e.g., traumatic injury) as principal diagnosis with B20 as secondary.
- Report B20 as secondary diagnosis for unrelated admissions regardless of new or previous HIV diagnosis.
- Assign Z21 for asymptomatic HIV status when only 'HIV positive' is documented without symptoms or illness.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the condition is HIV-related to assign B20 as principal diagnosis.
- Documentation must specify hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV to assign D59.31.
- Documentation must confirm the condition is unrelated to HIV to assign the unrelated condition as principal.
- Documentation must confirm absence of symptoms or HIV-related illness to assign Z21.
- Coding must not be influenced by whether the patient is newly diagnosed or has a previous HIV diagnosis.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning B20 as principal diagnosis for unrelated conditions.
- Using HIV diagnosis status to determine principal diagnosis when an unrelated condition is the reason for admission.
- Assigning B20 as principal diagnosis for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with HIV disease.
- Using new diagnosis status to alter principal diagnosis sequencing.
- Assigning symptom-based HIV codes when only 'HIV positive' is documented without illness.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit exception for hemolytic-uremic syndrome requiring D59.31 before B20.
- Mandatory assignment of Z21 for asymptomatic status with specific terminology.
- Clarification that unrelated condition coding takes precedence over HIV status for principal diagnosis.
- Guidance that new diagnosis status does not affect principal diagnosis sequencing.
- Requirement to code all reported HIV-related conditions as additional diagnoses when B20 is principal.

### 67. Influenza Coding Guidelines for Confirmed Cases
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Influenza Coding Guidelines for Confirmed Cases with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `190`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only confirmed cases of influenza due to certain identified influenza viruses (category J09), and due to other identified influenza virus (category J10). This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. (Uncertain Diagnosis).
>
> In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of positive laboratory testing specific for avian or other novel influenza A or other identified influenza virus. However, coding should be based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the patient has avian influenza, or other novel influenza A , for category J09, or has another particular identified strain of influenza, such as H1N1 or H3N2, but not identified as novel or variant, for category J10.
>
> If the provider records 'suspected ' or ' possible ' or ' probable ' avian influenza, or novel influenza, or other identified influenza, then the appropriate influenza code from category J11, Influenza due to unidentified influenza virus, should be assigned. A code from category J09, Influenza due to certain identified influenza viruses, should not be assigned nor should a code from category J10, Influenza due to other identified influenza virus.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Influenza Coding for Confirmed Cases

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code only confirmed cases of influenza due to certain identified influenza viruses (category J09) and due to other identified influenza virus (category J10).
- Coding is based on the provider's diagnostic statement regarding specific strains (e.g., avian, H1N1, H3N2) rather than requiring positive laboratory testing documentation.
- If the provider records 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' influenza, assign category J11 (Influenza due to unidentified influenza virus) instead of J09 or J10.
- Category J09 should not be assigned if the influenza is not confirmed as due to a certain identified virus.
- Category J10 should not be assigned if the influenza is not confirmed as due to another particular identified strain.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The provider must make a diagnostic statement confirming the patient has a specific identified strain of influenza.
- The diagnosis must be confirmed, not merely suspected, probable, or possible.
- Laboratory testing documentation is not strictly required for confirmation if the provider's statement is sufficient.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning category J09 or J10 codes based solely on 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' provider statements.
- Requiring positive laboratory testing documentation as a mandatory condition for assigning J09 or J10 codes.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. (Uncertain Diagnosis) for confirmed influenza cases.
- Mandatory use of category J11 for any influenza diagnosis lacking confirmation of a specific identified virus.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `620`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only confirmed cases of influenza due to certain identified influenza viruses (category J09), and due to other identified influenza virus (category J10). This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. (Uncertain Diagnosis).
>
> In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of positive laboratory testing specific for avian or other novel influenza A or other identified influenza virus. However, coding should be based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the patient has avian influenza, or other novel influenza A , for category J09, or has another particular identified strain of influenza, such as H1N1 or H3N2, but not identified as novel or variant, for category J10.
>
> If the provider records 'suspected ' or ' possible ' or ' probable ' avian influenza, or novel influenza, or other identified influenza, then the appropriate influenza code from category J11, Influenza due to unidentified influenza virus, should be assigned. A code from category J09, Influenza due to certain identified influenza viruses, should not be assigned nor should a code from category J10, Influenza due to other identified influenza virus.

- If the provider records 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' influenza, assign category J11 (Influenza due to unidentified influenza virus) instead of J09 or J10.
- Category J09 codes must not be assigned for suspected or probable cases of avian or novel influenza.
- Category J10 codes must not be assigned for suspected or probable cases of other identified influenza strains.

- Using category J09 or J10 for cases where the influenza virus is unidentified or the diagnosis remains uncertain.

There are no functional differences in the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or deprecated behaviors between the two provided text chunks.

### 68. Principal Diagnosis Selection for Respiratory Failure
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical guideline logic for selecting the principal diagnosis when respiratory failure co-occurs with other acute conditions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `189`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted with respiratory failure and another acute condition, (e.g., myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, aspiration pneumonia), the principal diagnosis will not be the same in every situation. This applies whether the other acute condition is a respiratory or nonrespiratory condition. Selection of the principal diagnosis will be dependent on the circumstances of admission. If both the respiratory failure and the other acute condition are equally responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital, and there are no chapter-specific sequencing rules, the guideline regarding two or more diagnoses that equally meet the definition for principal diagnosis (Section II, C.) may be applied in these situations.
>
> If the documentation is not clear as to whether acute respiratory failure and another condition are equally responsible for occasioning the admission, query the provider for clarification.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Principal Diagnosis Selection for Respiratory Failure

**Key Behaviors:**
- Principal diagnosis selection depends on the circumstances of admission when respiratory failure and another acute condition are present.
- If respiratory failure and another acute condition are equally responsible for admission, apply the guideline for two or more diagnoses equally meeting the definition for principal diagnosis.
- Query the provider for clarification if documentation is unclear regarding which condition is equally responsible for the admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must clearly indicate the circumstances of admission to determine the principal diagnosis.
- If conditions are equally responsible, both must meet the definition for principal diagnosis per Section II, C.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `619`

> **Actual Text**
>

This applies whether the other acute condition is a respiratory or nonrespiratory condition. Selection of the principal diagnosis will be dependent on the circumstances of admission. If both the respiratory failure and the other acute condition are equally responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital, and there are no chapter-specific sequencing rules, the guideline regarding two or more diagnoses that equally meet the definition for principal diagnosis (Section II, C.) may be applied in these situations.
>
> If the documentation is not clear as to whether acute respiratory failure and another condition are equally responsible for occasioning the admission, query the provider for clarification.

- If conditions are equally responsible, both must meet the definition for principal diagnosis per Section II, C.

Since the content is a direct copy, the change is purely administrative (e.g., versioning or metadata update) rather than a modification to clinical logic.

### 69. VAP Coding and Principal Diagnosis Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Respiratory and Infectious Disease Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical logic for assigning principal and additional diagnoses for VAP versus admission pneumonia.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `192`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A patient may be admitted with one type of pneumonia (e.g., code J13, Pneumonia due to Streptococcus pneumonia) and subsequently develop VAP. In this instance, the principal diagnosis would be the appropriate code from categories J12-J18 for the pneumonia diagnosed at the time of admission. Code J95.851, Ventilator associated pneumonia, would be assigned as an additional diagnosis when the provider has also documented the presence of ventilator associated pneumonia.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** VAP Coding and Principal Diagnosis Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Principal diagnosis is assigned the pneumonia code (J12-J18) diagnosed at the time of admission if the patient is admitted with pneumonia.
- Ventilator associated pneumonia (J95.851) is assigned as an additional diagnosis only if the provider documents its presence.
- The development of VAP after admission does not change the principal diagnosis from the initial pneumonia code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must document the presence of ventilator associated pneumonia to assign code J95.851.
- Principal diagnosis must reflect the condition diagnosed at the time of admission.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `622`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A patient may be admitted with one type of pneumonia (e.g., code J13, Pneumonia due to Streptococcus pneumonia) and subsequently develop VAP. In this instance, the principal diagnosis would be the appropriate code from categories J12-J18 for the pneumonia diagnosed at the time of admission. Code J95.851, Ventilator associated pneumonia, would be assigned as an additional diagnosis when the provider has also documented the presence of ventilator associated pneumonia.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** VAP Coding and Principal Diagnosis Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Principal diagnosis is assigned the pneumonia code (J12-J18) diagnosed at admission if the patient is admitted with pneumonia.
- Ventilator associated pneumonia (J95.851) is assigned as an additional diagnosis only if documented by the provider.
- The presence of VAP does not override the admission diagnosis if the patient was admitted for a different type of pneumonia.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must document the presence of ventilator associated pneumonia for code J95.851 to be valid.
- Admission diagnosis must be confirmed as pneumonia (categories J12-J18) at the time of admission.
