### 70. Comprehensive Drug Coding Protocol
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Substance Use and Adverse Effect Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Comprehensive Drug Coding Protocol with matching behaviors, requirements, and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `303_306`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Use as many codes as necessary to describe completely all drugs, medicinal or biological substances.
>
> When coding an adverse effect of a drug that has been correctly prescribed and properly administered, assign the appropriate code for the nature of the adverse effect followed by the appropriate code for the adverse effect of the drug (T36-T50). The code for the drug should have a 5 th or 6 th character '5' (for example T36.0X5-) Examples of the nature of an adverse effect are tachycardia, delirium, gastrointestinal hemorrhaging, vomiting, hypokalemia, hepatitis, renal failure, or respiratory failure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Comprehensive Drug Coding Protocol

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use as many codes as necessary to describe completely all drugs, medicinal or biological substances.
- Assign the appropriate code for the nature of the adverse effect followed by the appropriate code for the adverse effect of the drug (T36-T50).
- The code for the drug should have a 5th or 6th character '5' to indicate an adverse effect.
- Examples of the nature of an adverse effect include tachycardia, delirium, gastrointestinal hemorrhaging, vomiting, hypokalemia, hepatitis, renal failure, or respiratory failure.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Adverse effect must be of a drug that has been correctly prescribed and properly administered.
- Documentation must clearly identify the nature of the adverse effect (e.g., tachycardia, renal failure).
- The drug code must be modified with the 5th or 6th character '5' to denote an adverse effect.

### 71. Drug and Chemical Poisoning Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Substance Use and Adverse Effect Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the prohibition of coding directly from the Table of Drugs and Chemicals and the requirement to use the Tabular List.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `302_305`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Do not code directly from the Table of Drugs and Chemicals. Always refer back to the Tabular List.
>
> If two or more drugs, medicinal or biological substances are taken, code
>
> each individually unless a combination code is listed in the Table of
>
> Drugs and Chemicals.
>
> If multiple unspecified drugs, medicinal or biological substances were taken, assign the appropriate code from subcategory T50.91, Poisoning by, adverse effect of and underdosing of multiple unspecified drugs, medicaments and biological substances.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Drug and Chemical Poisoning Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Do not code directly from the Table of Drugs and Chemicals; always refer back to the Tabular List.
- Code each drug, medicinal, or biological substance individually if two or more are taken, unless a combination code is listed.
- Assign code from subcategory T50.91 for multiple unspecified drugs, medicaments, or biological substances taken.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Verification that the substance is listed in the Tabular List rather than the Table of Drugs and Chemicals.
- Confirmation of whether a specific combination code exists before assigning individual codes.
- Documentation must specify the number of unspecified substances to justify use of subcategory T50.91.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding directly from the Table of Drugs and Chemicals without referencing the Tabular List.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of subcategory T50.91 for multiple unspecified drugs, medicaments, and biological substances.

### 72. Toxic Effect and Sequela Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Substance Use and Adverse Effect Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature regarding toxic effect coding sequences and intent requirements with no behavioral differences.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `309`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a harmful substance is ingested or comes in contact with a person, this is classified as a toxic effect. The toxic effect codes are in categories T51-T65. When coding a toxic effect, assign the toxic effect code first, followed by codes for all associated manifestations of the toxic effect.
>
> Toxic effect codes have an associated intent: accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined.
>
> For Sequela (Late Effects) see Section I.B.10. Sequela

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Toxic Effect and Sequela Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Classify ingestion or contact with harmful substances as toxic effect codes in categories T51-T65.
- Assign the toxic effect code first, followed by codes for all associated manifestations.
- Include an associated intent code (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, or undetermined) for toxic effects.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the substance was ingested or came in contact with the person.
- All associated manifestations of the toxic effect must be coded.
- Intent must be documented to select the correct category (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, undetermined).

> Toxic effect codes have an associated intent: accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined.
>

- Include an associated intent code (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, or undetermined) for toxic effects.

Both versions explicitly reference Section I.B.10 for Sequela without modifying the core guidelines for toxic effects themselves.

### 73. Substance-Induced Disorder Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Substance Use and Adverse Effect Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding logic for substance-induced disorders with identical behavioral rules and examples.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `143`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Medical conditions due to substance use, abuse, and dependence are not classified as substance-induced disorders. Assign the diagnosis code for the medical condition as directed by the Alphabetical Index along with the appropriate psychoactive substance use, abuse or dependence code. For example, for alcoholic pancreatitis due to alcohol dependence, assign the appropriate code from subcategory K85.2, Alcohol induced acute pancreatitis, and the appropriate code from subcategory F10.2, such as code F10.20, Alcohol dependence, uncomplicated. It would not be appropriate to assign code F10.288, Alcohol dependence with other alcohol-induced disorder.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Substance-Induced Disorder Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Medical conditions due to substance use, abuse, and dependence are not classified as substance-induced disorders.
- Assign the diagnosis code for the medical condition as directed by the Alphabetical Index along with the appropriate psychoactive substance use, abuse or dependence code.
- Do not assign a code for substance dependence with other substance-induced disorder when a specific code for the medical condition exists.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The medical condition must be explicitly linked to the substance use, abuse, or dependence.
- The specific medical condition code must be selected from the appropriate subcategory in the Alphabetic Index.
- The substance use, abuse, or dependence code must be selected from the corresponding subcategory (e.g., F10-F19).

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a code for substance dependence with other substance-induced disorder (e.g., F10.288) when a specific code for the medical condition exists.
- Classifying medical conditions due to substance use, abuse, and dependence as substance-induced disorders.

**New in this version:**
- Requirement to assign the diagnosis code for the medical condition as directed by the Alphabetical Index.
- Mandatory pairing of the specific medical condition code with the appropriate psychoactive substance use, abuse or dependence code.

---

> **Actual Text**

> Medical conditions due to substance use, abuse, and dependence are not classified as substance-induced disorders. ### 74. Substance Use Coding Hierarchy
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Substance Use and Adverse Effect Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Substance Use Coding Hierarchy rules with identical behavioral instructions and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `141`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the provider documentation refers to use, abuse and dependence of the same substance (e.g. alcohol, opioid, cannabis, etc.), only one code should be assigned to identify the pattern of use based on the following hierarchy:
>
> - If both use and abuse are documented, assign only the code for abuse
> - If both abuse and dependence are documented, assign only the code for dependence
> - If use, abuse and dependence are all documented, assign only the code for dependence
> - If both use and dependence are documented, assign only the code for dependence.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Substance Use Coding Hierarchy

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign only the code for abuse if both use and abuse are documented
- Assign only the code for dependence if both abuse and dependence are documented
- Assign only the code for dependence if use, abuse, and dependence are all documented
- Assign only the code for dependence if both use and dependence are documented

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly refer to use, abuse, and/or dependence of the same substance
- Only one code should be assigned to identify the pattern of use based on the hierarchy

---

> When the provider documentation refers to use, abuse and dependence of the same substance (e.g. alcohol, opioid, cannabis, etc.), only one code should be assigned to identify the pattern of use based on the following hierarchy:
>

> - If both abuse and dependence are documented, assign only the code for dependence
> - If use, abuse and dependence are all documented, assign only the code for dependence
> - If both use and dependence are documented, assign only the code for dependence.

- Assign only the code for dependence if both abuse and dependence are documented

- Assign only the code for dependence if both use and dependence are documented

- Only one code should be assigned to identify the pattern of use based on the hierarchy

The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain identical text regarding the hierarchy for assigning codes when use, abuse, and dependence are documented. There are no functional changes to the coding logic, priority rules, or specific behaviors described in the excerpts. The only distinction is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which slightly varies in phrasing but conveys the same meaning, indicating an administrative update rather than a clinical guideline change.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Taxonomy description states 'prioritizes dependence over abuse, and both over simple use' -> icd (New Version): Taxonomy description states 'prioritizes assigning the code for dependence over abuse or use when multiple conditions are documented'
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements state 'Provider documentation must explicitly refer to use, abuse, and/or dependence' -> icd (New Version): Requirements state 'Provider documentation must explicitly refer to use, abuse, or dependence'

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `142`

> **Actual Text**
>
> As with all other unspecified diagnoses, the codes for unspecified psychoactive substance use (F10.9-, F11.9-, F12.9-, F13.9-, F14.9-, F15.9-, F16.9-, F18.9-, F19.9-) should only be assigned based on provider documentation and when they meet the definition of a reportable diagnosis (see Section III, Reporting Additional Diagnoses). These codes are to be used only when the psychoactive substance use is associated with a substance related disorder (chapter 5 disorders such as sexual dysfunction, sleep disorder, or a mental or behavioral disorder) or medical condition, and such a relationship is documented by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unspecified Psychoactive Substance Diagnosis Reporting

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes for unspecified psychoactive substance use should only be assigned based on provider documentation.
- These codes must meet the definition of a reportable diagnosis.
- Codes are used only when substance use is associated with a substance-related disorder or medical condition.
- The relationship between substance use and the associated condition must be documented by the provider.
- Codes are restricted to cases where the unspecified substance use is linked to specific disorders like sexual dysfunction, sleep disorder, or mental/behavioral disorders.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly link the unspecified psychoactive substance use to a reportable diagnosis.
- The association must be between the substance use and a substance-related disorder or medical condition.
- The relationship must be clearly documented by the provider to validate the code assignment.

### 77. Traumatic Coma Scale Coding Implementation
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the implementation of traumatic coma scale codes (R40.21-R40.24) with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `278`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The coma scale codes (R40.21- to R40.24-) can be used in conjunction with traumatic brain injury codes. These codes cannot be used with code R40.2A, Nontraumatic coma due to underlying condition. They are primarily for use by trauma registries, but they may be used in any setting where this information is collected. The coma scale codes should be sequenced after the diagnosis code(s).
>
> These codes, one from each subcategory, are needed to complete the scale. The 7 th character indicates when the scale was recorded. The 7 th character should match for all three codes.
>
> At a minimum, report the initial score documented on presentation at your facility. This may be a score from the emergency medicine technician (EMT) or in the emergency department. If desired, a facility may choose to capture multiple coma scale scores.
>
> Assign code R40.24 -, Glasgow coma scale, total score, when only the total score is documented in the medical record and not the individual score(s).
>
> If multiple coma scores are captured within the first 24 hours after hospital admission, assign only the code for the score at the time of admission. ICD-10-CM does not classify coma scores that are reported after admission but less than 24 hours later.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for coma scale documentation by clinicians other than patient's provider

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Traumatic Coma Scale Coding Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Coma scale codes (R40.21- to R40.24-) must be sequenced after the primary diagnosis code(s) and used in conjunction with traumatic brain injury codes.
- The 7th character indicating when the scale was recorded must match across all three codes used to complete the scale.
- At a minimum, report the initial score documented on presentation at the facility (e.g., by EMT or in the emergency department).
- If only the total score is documented, assign code R40.24-; if multiple scores are captured within the first 24 hours, assign only the code for the admission time score.
- Coma scores reported after admission but less than 24 hours later are not classified by ICD-10-CM.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Codes cannot be used with R40.2A (Nontraumatic coma due to underlying condition).
- Documentation must include the specific time the scale was recorded to determine the correct 7th character.
- For multiple scores within the first 24 hours, the admission time score is the mandatory selection.
- Total score documentation alone requires the specific R40.24- code assignment.
- Clinician documentation must follow Section I.B.14 guidelines if not provided by the patient's provider.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using coma scale codes with R40.2A.
- Reporting coma scores occurring after admission but before 24 hours as valid ICD-10-CM classifications.
- Assigning multiple coma scale codes for scores captured within the first 24 hours of admission.
- Sequencing coma scale codes before the primary diagnosis code.
- Failing to ensure the 7th character matches across all three codes in the scale.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to sequence coma scale codes after the diagnosis code(s).
- Mandatory use of the 7th character to indicate the timing of the scale recording.
- Specific instruction to assign R40.24- when only the total score is available.
- Clarification that initial presentation scores (EMT/ED) are the minimum reporting requirement.
- Reference to Section I.B.14 for non-provider clinician documentation protocols.

---

> **Actual Text**
>

They are primarily for use by trauma registries, but they may be used in any setting where this information is collected. The coma scale codes should be sequenced after the diagnosis code(s).

> These codes, one from each subcategory, are needed to complete the scale. The 7 th character indicates when the scale was recorded. The 7 th character should match for all three codes.

**New in this version:**

- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Assigning multiple coma scale codes for scores captured within the first 24 hours of admission' as deprecated. -> icd (New Version): Lists 'Assigning unspecified coma scale codes when specific subcategory codes (R40.21- to R40.24-) are available' as deprecated.

*Traceability: Chunk `278` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `705` (icd (New Version))*

### 78. Y93 Activity Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature 'Y93 Activity Code Assignment' with nearly identical behavioral rules and constraints.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `327`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign a code from category Y93, Activity code, to describe the activity of the patient at the time the injury or other health condition occurred.
>
> An activity code is used only once, at the initial encounter for treatment. Only one code from Y93 should be recorded on a medical record.
>
> The activity codes are not applicable to poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures or sequela.
>
> Do not assign Y93.9, Unspecified activity, if the activity is not stated.
>
> A code from category Y93 is appropriate for use with external cause and intent codes if identifying the activity provides additional information about the event.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y93 Activity Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a code from category Y93 to describe the patient's activity at the time the injury or health condition occurred.
- An activity code is used only once, at the initial encounter for treatment.
- Only one code from Y93 should be recorded on a medical record.
- Do not assign Y93.9, Unspecified activity, if the activity is not stated.
- Activity codes are not applicable to poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or sequela.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The activity must be explicitly stated to avoid using Y93.9.
- The code must be assigned at the initial encounter for treatment.
- Only a single Y93 code is permitted per medical record.
- The code must not be used for poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or sequela.
- The code is appropriate for use with external cause and intent codes if it provides additional information about the event.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `326`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes from category Y92, Place of occurrence of the external cause, are secondary codes for use after other external cause codes to identify the location of the patient at the time of injury or other condition.
>
> Generally, a place of occurrence code is assigned only once, at the initial encounter for treatment. However, in the rare instance that a new injury occurs during hospitalization, an additional place of occurrence code may be assigned. No 7 th characters are used for Y92.
>
> Do not use place of occurrence code Y92.9 if the place is not stated or is not applicable.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y92 Place of Occurrence Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**

- In rare instances where a new injury occurs during hospitalization, an additional place of occurrence code may be assigned.
- No 7th characters are used for Y92 codes.
- Do not use place of occurrence code Y92.9 if the place is not stated or is not applicable.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Y92 codes must be assigned as secondary codes following other external cause codes.
- The code must identify the specific location of the patient at the time of the injury or condition.
- Assignment is mandatory only at the initial encounter unless a new injury occurs during hospitalization.

### 80. Fracture Complication Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the exact same feature regarding 7th character assignment for fracture complications with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `207`

> **Actual Text**
>
> 7 th character A is for use as long as the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture. While the patient may be seen by a new or different provider over the course of treatment for a pathological fracture, assignment of the 7 th character is based on whether the patient is undergoing active treatment and not whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.
>
> 7th character D is to be used for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment for the fracture and is receiving routine care for the fracture during the healing or recovery phase. The other 7 th characters, listed under each subcategory in the Tabular List, are to be used for subsequent encounters for treatment of problems associated with the healing, such as malunions , nonunions, and sequelae.
>
> Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.
>
> See Section I.C.19. Coding of traumatic fractures.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Fracture Complication Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- 7th character A is assigned when the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture, regardless of provider continuity.
- 7th character D is assigned for encounters after active treatment completion during routine healing or recovery care.
- Other 7th characters are used for subsequent encounters treating problems associated with healing, such as malunions, nonunions, and sequelae.
- Complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase must be coded with appropriate complication codes.
- Assignment of the 7th character is based on active treatment status rather than whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient is undergoing active treatment to justify 7th character A.
- Documentation must confirm active treatment has completed to justify 7th character D.
- Specific problems associated with healing (e.g., malunion, nonunion) must be documented to select appropriate subsequent encounter codes.
- Surgical complications must be explicitly identified as occurring during the healing or recovery phase.
- Coding must adhere to Section I.C.19 guidelines for traumatic fractures.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `636`

> **Actual Text**
>

### 81. Complication Code Assignment Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding complication code assignment with identical behavioral rules and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `43`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure , unless otherwise instructed by the classification. The guideline extends to any complications of care, regardless of the chapter the code is located in. It is important to note that not all conditions that occur during or following medical care or surgery are classified as complications. There must be a cause-and-effect relationship between the care provided and the condition, and the documentation must support that the condition is clinically significant. It is not necessary for the provider to explicitly document the term 'complication.' For example, if the condition alters the course of the surgery as documented in the operative report, then it would be appropriate to report a complication code. Query the provider for clarification if the documentation is not clear as to the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Complication Code Assignment Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure.
- The guideline extends to any complications of care, regardless of the chapter the code is located in.
- It is not necessary for the provider to explicitly document the term 'complication' if the condition alters the course of surgery.
- Query the provider for clarification if the documentation is not clear as to the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure.
- Conditions must have a cause-and-effect relationship with care and be clinically significant to be classified as complications.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- There must be a cause-and-effect relationship between the care provided and the condition.
- The documentation must support that the condition is clinically significant.
- The condition must not be expected or an integral part of the disease process.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `475`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure , unless otherwise instructed by the classification. The guideline extends to any complications of care, regardless of the chapter the code is located in. Since the content is effectively identical, this represents an administrative update or versioning artifact rather than a substantive guideline change.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `280`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code R99, Ill-defined and unknown cause of mortality, is only for use in the very limited circumstance when a patient who has already died is brought into an emergency department or other healthcare facility and is pronounced dead upon arrival. It does not represent the discharge disposition of death.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Code R99 Usage Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code R99 is used only when a deceased patient is brought to a facility and pronounced dead upon arrival.
- Code R99 is not used to represent the discharge disposition of death.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The patient must have already died prior to arrival at the healthcare facility.
- Death must be pronounced at the facility upon arrival.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using Code R99 for discharge disposition of death.

### 84. Intraoperative Complication Code Sequencing
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical sequencing rules for intraoperative complications with verbatim matching text.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `316`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Intraoperative and postprocedural complication codes are found within the body system chapters with codes specific to the organs and structures of that body system. These codes should be sequenced first, followed by a code(s) for the specific complication, if applicable.
>
> Complication codes from the body system chapters should be assigned for intraoperative and postprocedural complications (e.g., the appropriate complication code from chapter 9 would be assigned for a vascular intraoperative or postprocedural complication) unless the complication is specifically indexed to a T code in chapter 19.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Intraoperative Complication Code Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Intraoperative and postprocedural complication codes should be sequenced first, followed by a code for the specific complication if applicable.
- Complication codes from body system chapters must be assigned for intraoperative and postprocedural complications unless the complication is specifically indexed to a T code in chapter 19.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The complication must be intraoperative or postprocedural in nature.
- The complication must be specific to the organs and structures of the relevant body system chapter.
- If the complication is indexed to a T code in chapter 19, that T code takes precedence over body system chapter codes.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `744`

> **Actual Text**

### 85. Injury Coding and Sequencing Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Injury Coding and Sequencing guidelines with identical behaviors and deprecated rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `283`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When coding injuries, assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided, in which case the combination code is assigned. Codes from category T07, Unspecified multiple injuries should not be assigned in the inpatient setting unless information for a more specific code is not available. Traumatic injury codes (S00- T14.9) are not to be used for normal, healing surgical wounds or to identify complications of surgical wounds.
>
> The code for the most serious injury, as determined by the provider and the focus of treatment, is sequenced first.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Injury Coding and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided.
- Do not assign codes from category T07 (Unspecified multiple injuries) in the inpatient setting unless more specific information is unavailable.
- Do not use traumatic injury codes (S00-T14.9) for normal, healing surgical wounds or to identify complications of surgical wounds.
- Sequence the code for the most serious injury first, as determined by the provider and the focus of treatment.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must determine the most serious injury based on the focus of treatment.
- Documentation must support the assignment of specific injury codes rather than unspecified multiple injury codes in inpatient settings.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using codes from category T07 for inpatient claims when specific injury information is available.
- Coding normal, healing surgical wounds or surgical wound complications using traumatic injury codes (S00-T14.9).

---

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided.
- Do not assign codes from category T07 (Unspecified multiple injuries) in the inpatient setting unless more specific information is unavailable.
- Do not use traumatic injury codes (S00-T14.9) for normal, healing surgical wounds or to identify complications of surgical wounds.
- Sequence the code for the most serious injury first, as determined by the provider and the focus of treatment.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must determine the most serious injury based on the focus of treatment.
- Documentation must support the assignment of specific injury codes rather than unspecified multiple injury codes in inpatient settings.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using codes from category T07 for inpatient claims when specific injury information is available.
- Coding normal, healing surgical wounds or surgical wound complications using traumatic injury codes (S00-T14.9).

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the assignment of separate injury codes, restrictions on T07 codes in inpatient settings, and prohibitions on using traumatic injury codes for surgical wounds. There are no functional changes to the clinical logic, coding rules, or sequencing requirements between the two versions. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a versioning or administrative update rather than a substantive guideline change.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided. -> icd (New Version): Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided.

### 87. Postprocedural Infection Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Injury and Complication Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the mandatory documentation of causal relationships for postprocedural infection codes.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `69`

> **Actual Text**
>
> As with all postprocedural complications, code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the infection and the procedure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Postprocedural Infection Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the infection and the procedure.
- Infections must be explicitly linked to a specific procedure to be classified as postprocedural complications.
- Coder must verify the temporal and causal connection between the procedure and the infection diagnosis.
- Documentation must distinguish between expected postoperative infections and unrelated infections.
- Absence of documented relationship precludes assignment of postprocedural infection codes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly state the infection is a result of the procedure.
- Clinical evidence must support the causal link between the procedure and the infection.
- Documentation must specify the timing of the infection relative to the procedure.
- Provider clarification is required if the relationship between infection and procedure is unclear.
- Codes cannot be assigned without verified documentation of the procedural etiology.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning postprocedural codes based solely on temporal proximity without documented causality.
- Coding infections as postprocedural complications when they are known sequelae of the disease process.
- Using unspecified codes for postprocedural infections without provider clarification.
- Assuming infection is procedure-related without explicit provider statement.
- Combining unrelated infection codes with procedure codes without establishing the complication relationship.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory requirement for explicit documentation of the infection-procedure relationship.
- Enhanced query protocols for unclear postprocedural infection documentation.
- Updated definitions for distinguishing expected vs. unexpected postprocedural infections.
- Additional data elements required to support the causal link in claims processing.
- Stricter validation rules for postprocedural infection code assignment.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `497`

> **Actual Text**
>
> As with all postprocedural complications, code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the infection and the procedure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Postprocedural Infection Code Assignment

- Coder must verify the temporal and causal connection between the procedure and the infection diagnosis.
- Documentation must distinguish between expected postoperative infections and unrelated infections.
- Absence of documented relationship precludes assignment of postprocedural infection codes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly state the infection is related to the procedure.
- Clinical evidence must support the causal link between the procedure and the infection.
- Documentation must specify the timing of the infection relative to the procedure.
- Provider clarification is required if the relationship between infection and procedure is unclear.
- Code assignment is invalid without documented confirmation of the postprocedural nature.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning postprocedural codes without explicit provider documentation of the relationship.
- Assuming an infection is postprocedural based solely on temporal proximity.
- Coding infections as postprocedural complications when they are known sequelae of the disease.
- Using unspecified codes for postprocedural infections without documented laterality or causality.
- Combining unrelated infection codes with procedure codes without establishing a causal link.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory requirement for explicit documentation of the infection-procedure relationship.
- Enhanced query protocols for unclear postprocedural infection documentation.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `178`

> **Actual Text**

> Medical record documentation should clearly specify the cause- and-effect relationship between the medical intervention and the cerebrovascular accident in order to assign a code for intraoperative or postprocedural cerebrovascular accident.
>
> Proper code assignment depends on whether it was an infarction or hemorrhage and whether it occurred intraoperatively or postoperatively. If it was a cerebral hemorrhage, code assignment depends on the type of procedure performed.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Cerebrovascular Accident Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Medical record documentation must clearly specify the cause- and-effect relationship between the medical intervention and the cerebrovascular accident.
- Code assignment depends on whether the event was an infarction or hemorrhage.
- Code assignment depends on whether the event occurred intraoperatively or postoperatively.
- For cerebral hemorrhage, code assignment depends on the specific type of procedure performed.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must establish a clear causal link between the intervention and the stroke.
- The clinical record must distinguish between infarction and hemorrhage.
- The timing of the event (intraoperative vs. postoperative) must be explicitly documented.
- The specific type of procedure performed must be identified for hemorrhage cases.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** STEMI Myocardial Infarction Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code I21.9 is the default for unspecified acute myocardial infarction or unspecified type.
- If only type 1 STEMI or transmural MI without the site is documented, assign code I21.3.

- Site of infarction must be documented to assign a more specific code than I21.3.

**Feature:** STEMI Myocardial Infarction Coding Rules

### 93. POA Status Determination Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical POA status determination logic with matching definitions for codes Y, N, U, and W.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `423`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Y = present at the time of inpatient admission
>
> N = not present at the time of inpatient admission
>
> U = documentation is insufficient to determine if condition is present on admission
>
> W = provider is unable to clinically determine whether condition was present on admission or not

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** POA Status Determination Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'Y' when condition is present at the time of inpatient admission
- Assign 'N' when condition is not present at the time of inpatient admission
- Assign 'U' when documentation is insufficient to determine if condition is present on admission
- Assign 'W' when provider is unable to clinically determine whether condition was present on admission or not

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state presence or absence of condition at admission
- Provider clinical judgment is required when documentation is ambiguous
- Insufficient documentation triggers 'U' status rather than defaulting to 'N'
- Provider inability to determine status triggers 'W' status
- Inpatient admission timing is the reference point for status determination

### 94. POA Reporting Exemption Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical POA Reporting Exemption Logic feature with matching definitions for status codes Y, N, U, W, and the exemption category.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `422`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Y - Yes
>
> N - No
>
> U - Unknown
>
> W - Clinically undetermined
>
> Unreported/Not used - (Exempt from POA reporting)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** POA Reporting Exemption Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'Y' (Yes) when the condition is present on admission
- Assign 'N' (No) when the condition is not present on admission
- Assign 'U' (Unknown) when the presence on admission cannot be determined
- Assign 'W' (Clinically undetermined) when the condition is clinically undetermined
- Mark as 'Unreported/Not used' to exempt the condition from POA reporting requirements

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Accurate determination of condition presence at the time of admission is mandatory for valid coding
- Clinically undetermined status requires specific clinical justification for the 'W' assignment
- Exemption status must be explicitly designated to avoid compliance penalties for unreported conditions

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning 'Y' or 'N' without clinical documentation supporting the timing of onset
- Using 'U' or 'W' as a default when admission status is clearly documented
- Failing to distinguish between clinically undetermined and unknown status

**New in this version:**
- Incorporation of 'Unreported/Not used' status to streamline reporting for exempt conditions
- Standardized definitions for 'Clinically undetermined' to reduce ambiguity in coding assignments

### 95. POA Indicator Assignment Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding POA indicator assignment, exemptions, and provider accountability with no substantive behavioral differences.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `419`

> **Actual Text**
>
> These guidelines are to be used as a supplement to the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting to facilitate the assignment of the Present on Admission (POA) indicator for each diagnosis and external cause of injury code reported on claim forms (UB-04 and 837 Institutional).
>
> These guidelines are not intended to replace any guidelines in the main body of the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting . The POA guidelines are not intended to provide guidance on when a condition should be coded, but rather, how to apply the POA indicator to the final set of diagnosis codes that have been assigned in accordance with Sections I, II, and III of the official coding guidelines. Subsequent to the assignment of the ICD-10-CM codes, the POA indicator should then be assigned to those conditions that have been coded.
>
> As stated in the Introduction to the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting, a joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Medical record documentation from any provider involved in the care and treatment of the patient may be used to support the determination of whether a condition was present on admission or not. In the context of the official coding guidelines, the term 'provider' means a physician or any qualified healthcare practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.
>
> These guidelines are not a substitute for the provider's clinical judgment as to the determination of whether a condition was/was not present on admission. The provider should be queried regarding issues related to the linking of signs/symptoms, timing of test results, and the timing of findings.
>
> Please see the CDC website for the detailed list of ICD-10-CM codes that do not require the use of a POA indicator (https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/icd/icd-10-cm/files.html) . The codes and categories on this exempt list are for circumstances regarding the healthcare encounter or factors influencing health status that do not represent a current disease or injury or that describe conditions that are always present on admission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** POA Indicator Assignment and Exemptions

**Key Behaviors:**
- The POA indicator is assigned to diagnosis and external cause codes after ICD-10-CM codes are assigned in accordance with official coding guidelines.
- Medical record documentation from any provider legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis may be used to support the determination of POA status.
- Providers must be queried regarding issues related to the linking of signs/symptoms, timing of test results, and the timing of findings.
- The guidelines supplement but do not replace the main body of ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting.
- A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation and reporting.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The provider must be legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis to support the determination of whether a condition was present on admission.
- The POA indicator must be assigned to conditions that have been coded in accordance with Sections I, II, and III of the official coding guidelines.
- Detailed documentation is required to determine if a condition was present on admission versus acquired during the healthcare encounter.
- External cause of injury codes must also have a POA indicator assigned unless exempted.
- The determination of POA status relies on clinical judgment and documentation from the provider involved in the care and treatment of the patient.
- The term 'provider' is explicitly defined as a physician or any qualified healthcare practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.
- The CDC website must be consulted to obtain the detailed list of ICD-10-CM codes exempt from POA indicator usage.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using the POA guidelines to determine when a condition should be coded rather than applying them to the final set of assigned codes.
- Relying solely on the POA guidelines without referencing the main body of ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting.
- Assigning POA indicators without considering the joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder.
- Ignoring the CDC website list of ICD-10-CM codes that do not require the use of a POA indicator.
- Failing to query the provider regarding the timing of findings when documentation is unclear.

**New in this version:**
- The specific requirement to use the CDC website for the detailed list of ICD-10-CM codes exempt from POA indicator usage.
- The explicit inclusion of external cause of injury codes in the scope of POA indicator assignment.
- The mandate that medical record documentation from any provider involved in the care and treatment of the patient may be used to support POA determination.
- The clarification that the term 'provider' includes any qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.
- The emphasis on querying providers regarding the linking of signs/symptoms and timing of test results as part of the POA determination process.

### 96. Outpatient to Inpatient Principal Diagnosis Selection
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding principal diagnosis selection for patients transitioning from outpatient to inpatient care at the same facility.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `392`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient receives surgery in the hospital's outpatient surgery department and is subsequently admitted for continuing inpatient care at the same hospital, the following guidelines should be followed in selecting the principal diagnosis for the inpatient admission:
>
> - If the reason for the inpatient admission is a complication, assign the complication as the principal diagnosis.
> - If no complication, or other condition, is documented as the reason for the inpatient admission, assign the reason for the outpatient surgery as the principal diagnosis.
> - If the reason for the inpatient admission is another condition unrelated to the surgery, assign the unrelated condition as the principal diagnosis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient to Inpatient Principal Diagnosis Selection

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the complication as the principal diagnosis if the reason for inpatient admission is a complication.
- Assign the reason for the outpatient surgery as the principal diagnosis if no complication or other condition is documented as the reason for admission.
- Assign an unrelated condition as the principal diagnosis if the reason for inpatient admission is a condition unrelated to the surgery.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly identify the reason for the inpatient admission.
- The complication must be documented as the reason for the inpatient admission to be selected as the principal diagnosis.
- The outpatient surgery reason must be the only documented reason for admission if no complication exists.
- The unrelated condition must be clearly documented as the reason for admission.
- The admission must occur at the same hospital where the outpatient surgery was performed.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `825`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient receives surgery in the hospital's outpatient surgery department and is subsequently admitted for continuing inpatient care at the same hospital, the following guidelines should be followed in selecting the principal diagnosis for the inpatient admission:
>
> - If the reason for the inpatient admission is a complication, assign the complication as the principal diagnosis.
> - If no complication, or other condition, is documented as the reason for the inpatient admission, assign the reason for the outpatient surgery as the principal diagnosis.
>
> - If the reason for the inpatient admission is another condition unrelated to the surgery, assign the unrelated condition as the principal diagnosis.

- The complication must be documented as the reason for the inpatient admission to be selected as the principal diagnosis.
- The outpatient surgery reason must be the only documented reason for admission if no complication exists.
- The unrelated condition must be clearly documented as the reason for admission.
- The admission must occur at the same hospital where the outpatient surgery was performed.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The new version appears to be a reformatting of the older text, splitting the second bullet point into two distinct lines for clarity without altering the clinical logic, requirements, or hierarchy of the guidelines.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The guideline lists the rule for unrelated conditions as a single bullet point following the surgery reason rule. -> icd (New Version): The guideline separates the rule for unrelated conditions into its own distinct bullet point, improving visual separation of the three decision paths.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The text flows as a continuous list of three items. -> icd (New Version): The text is formatted with an explicit line break between the surgery reason rule and the unrelated condition rule, though the content remains identical.

**Feature:** Observation to Inpatient Diagnosis Logic

- When a patient is admitted to an observation unit for a medical condition, which either worsens or does not improve, and is subsequently admitted as an inpatient of the same hospital for this same medical condition, the principal diagnosis would be the medical condition which led to the hospital admission.

- The condition must either worsen or fail to improve during the observation period.
- The patient must be subsequently admitted as an inpatient to the same hospital.
- The inpatient admission must be for the same medical condition that led to the observation admission.

> **Actual Text**

> Assign 'Y' for acute conditions that are present at time of admission and N for acute conditions that are not present at time of admission.
>
> Assign 'Y' for chronic conditions, even though the condition may not be diagnosed until after admission.
>
> If a single code identifies both an acute and chronic condition, see the POA guidelines for codes that contain multiple clinical concepts.

- Assign 'Y' for acute conditions present at time of admission and 'N' for those not present.
- Assign 'Y' for chronic conditions even if diagnosed after admission.
- Refer to POA guidelines for codes containing multiple clinical concepts.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Acute conditions must be documented as present at the time of admission to receive 'Y'.
- Chronic conditions require 'Y' assignment regardless of diagnosis timing relative to admission.
- Codes identifying both acute and chronic conditions must follow specific POA guidelines.

### 99. Present on Admission Clinical Assignment Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical 'Present on Admission' logic with matching behaviors, requirements, and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `427`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign 'N' if at least one of the clinical concepts included in the code was not present on admission (e.g., COPD with acute exacerbation and the exacerbation was not present on admission; gastric ulcer that does not start bleeding until after admission; asthma patient develops status asthmaticus after admission).
>
> Assign 'Y' if all of the clinical concepts included in the code were present on admission (e.g., duodenal ulcer that perforates prior to admission).
>
> For infection codes that include the causal organism, assign 'Y' if the infection (or signs of the infection) were present on admission, even though the culture results may not be known until after admission (e.g., patient is admitted with pneumonia and the provider documents Pseudomonas as the causal organism a few days later).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Present on Admission Clinical Assignment Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'N' if at least one clinical concept in the code was not present on admission.
- Assign 'Y' if all clinical concepts in the code were present on admission.
- Assign 'Y' for infection codes with causal organisms if infection signs were present on admission, even if culture results are pending.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must clearly distinguish between conditions present on admission and those developing after admission.
- For infection codes, documentation of the causal organism must be linked to signs present on admission to justify 'Y' assignment.
- All clinical concepts within a single code must be evaluated collectively to determine the 'Y' or 'N' status.

---

> **Actual Text**
>

>
> Assign 'Y' if all of the clinical concepts included in the code were present on admission (e.g., duodenal ulcer that perforates prior to admission).
>
> For infection codes that include the causal organism, assign 'Y' if the infection (or signs of the infection) were present on admission, even though the culture results may not be known until after admission (e.g., patient is admitted with pneumonia and the provider documents Pseudomonas as the causal organism a few days later).

### 100. Present on Admission (POA) Indicator Logic
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the exact same feature (POA Indicator Logic) with identical behavioral definitions and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `421`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Present on admission is defined as present at the time the order for inpatient admission occurs -- conditions that develop during an outpatient encounter, including emergency department, observation, or outpatient surgery, are considered as present on admission.
>
> POA indicator is assigned to principal and secondary diagnoses (as defined in Section II of the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting) and the external cause of injury codes.
>
> Issues related to inconsistent, missing, conflicting or unclear documentation must still be resolved by the provider.
>
> If a condition would not be coded and reported based on UHDDS definitions and current official coding guidelines, then the POA indicator would not be reported.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Present on Admission (POA) Indicator Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Conditions developing during outpatient encounters, including ED, observation, or surgery, are considered present on admission.
- POA indicators are assigned to principal and secondary diagnoses and external cause of injury codes.
- Inconsistent, missing, conflicting, or unclear documentation issues must be resolved by the provider.
- POA indicators are not reported for conditions that would not be coded based on UHDDS definitions and official guidelines.
- POA status is determined at the time the order for inpatient admission occurs.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Condition must be present at the time the order for inpatient admission occurs.
- Documentation must be consistent, present, and clear to avoid provider queries.
- Condition must be reportable under UHDDS definitions and current official coding guidelines.
- POA indicator must be assigned to principal and secondary diagnoses as defined in Section II of the Official Guidelines.
- External cause of injury codes must also receive a POA indicator assignment.

### 101. UHDDS Principal Diagnosis Definitions
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical UHDDS Principal Diagnosis definition and its application scope without any behavioral differences.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `382`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The circumstances of inpatient admission always govern the selection of principal diagnosis. The principal diagnosis is defined in the Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) as 'that condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission of the patient to the hospital for care.'
>
> The UHDDS definitions are used by hospitals to report inpatient data elements in a standardized manner. These data elements and their definitions can be found in the July 31, 1985, Federal Register (Vol. 50, No, 147), pp. 31038-40.
>
> Since that time, the application of the UHDDS definitions has been expanded to include all non-outpatient settings (acute care, short term, long term care and psychiatric hospitals; home health agencies; rehab facilities; nursing homes, etc.). The UHDDS definitions also apply to hospice services (all levels of care).
>
> In determining principal diagnosis , coding conventions in the ICD-10-CM, the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index take precedence over these official coding guidelines.
>
> (See Section I.A., Conventions for the ICD-10-CM)
>
> The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation the application of all coding guidelines is a difficult, if not impossible, task.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** UHDDS Principal Diagnosis Selection

**Key Behaviors:**
- The circumstances of inpatient admission always govern the selection of principal diagnosis.
- The principal diagnosis is defined as the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission of the patient to the hospital for care.
- Coding conventions in the ICD-10-CM, the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index take precedence over these official coding guidelines.
- Consistent and complete documentation in the medical record is mandatory for the application of all coding guidelines.
- UHDDS definitions apply to all non-outpatient settings including acute care, short term, long term care, psychiatric hospitals, home health, rehab facilities, nursing homes, and hospice services.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The condition must be established after study to be chiefly responsible for the admission.
- Documentation must be consistent and complete in the medical record.
- ICD-10-CM conventions must take precedence over UHDDS definitions when determining the principal diagnosis.
- The UHDDS definitions are legally grounded in the July 31, 1985, Federal Register (Vol. 50, No. 147), pp. 31038-40.

---

### 102. UHDDS Principal Diagnosis Definition for Observation-to-Inpatient Transfers
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Principal Diagnosis Assignment Logic  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical UHDDS principal diagnosis rule for observation-to-inpatient transfers following outpatient surgery with verbatim matching text.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `391`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted to an observation unit to monitor a condition (or complication) that develops following outpatient surgery, and then is subsequently admitted as an inpatient of the same hospital, hospitals should apply the Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis as "that condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission of the patient to the hospital for care."

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** UHDDS Principal Diagnosis Definition for Observation-to-Inpatient Transfers

**Key Behaviors:**
- Apply UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis as the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital for care.
- Monitor conditions or complications that develop following outpatient surgery during the observation stay.
- Subsequently admit the patient as an inpatient of the same hospital after the observation period.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The condition must be established after study to be chiefly responsible for the admission.
- The patient must have been admitted to an observation unit prior to inpatient status.
- The condition must have developed following outpatient surgery.
- The admission must occur within the same hospital.
- The condition must be the primary reason for the inpatient care.

---

### 103. ICD-10 Chapter 15 Sepsis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10 Chapter 15 Sepsis Coding Guidelines regarding infection type assignment and severe sepsis classification.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `73_206_234`

> **Actual Text**
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sepsis and septic shock complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> Many musculoskeletal conditions are a result of previous injury or trauma to a site, or are recurrent conditions. Bone, joint or muscle conditions that are the result of a healed injury are usually found in chapter 13. Recurrent bone, joint or muscle conditions are also usually found in chapter 13. Any current, acute injury should be coded to the appropriate injury code from chapter 19. Chronic or recurrent conditions should generally be coded with a code from chapter 13. If it is difficult to determine from the documentation in the record which code is best to describe a condition, query the provider.
>
> When assigning a chapter 15 code for sepsis complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, a code for the specific type of infection should be assigned as an additional diagnosis. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, and code(s) for associated organ dysfunction(s) should also be assigned as additional diagnoses.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Chapter 15 Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a code for the specific type of infection as an additional diagnosis when coding sepsis complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium.
- If severe sepsis is present, assign a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, as an additional diagnosis.
- Assign codes for associated organ dysfunction(s) as additional diagnoses when severe sepsis is present.
- Query the provider if it is difficult to determine from the documentation which code best describes a condition.
- Code current, acute injuries to the appropriate injury code from chapter 19 rather than chapter 13.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the specific type of infection to assign the correct additional diagnosis code.
- Clinical documentation must confirm the presence of severe sepsis to trigger the use of subcategory R65.2.
- Documentation must identify associated organ dysfunction(s) to support the assignment of additional organ dysfunction codes.
- Provider query is mandatory when documentation is insufficient to distinguish between acute injury (chapter 19) and chronic/recurrent conditions (chapter 13).
- Coding of sepsis complicating pregnancy-related conditions requires adherence to Section I.C.15 guidelines.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding musculoskeletal conditions resulting from healed injuries to chapter 19 instead of chapter 13.
- Coding recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions to chapter 19 instead of chapter 13.
- Failing to assign a code for the specific type of infection alongside a sepsis code in pregnancy-related complications.
- Omitting codes for associated organ dysfunction(s) when severe sepsis is documented.
- Assigning unspecified codes without querying the provider when documentation is ambiguous.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of subcategory R65.2 for severe sepsis cases.
- Requirement to code associated organ dysfunction(s) as additional diagnoses in severe sepsis scenarios.
- Explicit instruction to query providers for ambiguous documentation regarding acute vs. chronic conditions.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `635_663`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Many musculoskeletal conditions are a result of previous injury or trauma to a site, or are recurrent conditions. Bone, joint or muscle conditions that are the result of a healed injury are usually found in chapter 13. Recurrent bone, joint or muscle conditions are also usually found in chapter 13. Any current, acute injury should be coded to the appropriate injury code from chapter 19. Chronic or recurrent conditions should generally be coded with a code from chapter 13. If it is difficult to determine from the documentation in the record which code is best to describe a condition, query the provider.
>
> When assigning a chapter 15 code for sepsis complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, a code for the specific type of infection should be assigned as an additional diagnosis. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, and code(s) for associated organ dysfunction(s) should also be assigned as additional diagnoses.

- Assign a code for the specific type of infection as an additional diagnosis when coding sepsis complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium.
- If severe sepsis is present, assign a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, and codes for associated organ dysfunction(s) as additional diagnoses.
- Code current, acute injuries to the appropriate injury code from chapter 19 rather than chapter 13.
- Code chronic or recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions with codes from chapter 13.
- Query the provider if it is difficult to determine from the documentation which code best describes a condition.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the specific type of infection for sepsis complicating pregnancy-related conditions.
- Documentation must explicitly indicate severe sepsis to trigger the use of subcategory R65.2.

- Provider query is mandatory when documentation is insufficient to determine the best descriptive code.

- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions to chapter 19 instead of chapter 13. -> icd (New Version): Coding recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions to chapter 13 instead of chapter 19.

### 104. Excludes2 Note Logic Implementation
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the implementation of Excludes2 note logic with identical core definitions and behaviors.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `20`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.' An excludes2 note indicates that the condition excluded is not part of the condition represented by the code, but a patient may have both conditions at the same time. When an Excludes2 note appears under a code, it is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together, when appropriate.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Excludes2 Note Logic Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.'
- The excluded condition is not part of the condition represented by the code.
- A patient may have both the coded condition and the excluded condition at the same time.
- It is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together when appropriate.
- The Excludes2 note indicates that the exclusion is not mandatory.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must support the presence of both conditions simultaneously.
- The conditions must be clinically distinct and not integral parts of each other.
- The coder must verify that the exclusion is not a mandatory 'Excludes1' type.
- Both codes must be assigned only if the patient meets the criteria for both.
- The assignment must align with the 'Not included here' definition of the Excludes2 note.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning only the primary code when both conditions are present.
- Treating Excludes2 notes as mandatory exclusions.
- Assuming the excluded condition is part of the primary condition.
- Failing to code the excluded condition when clinical evidence supports its presence.
- Using Excludes2 notes to prevent dual coding of unrelated conditions.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit permission to code both the primary and excluded conditions together.
- Clarification that Excludes2 notes indicate non-inclusion rather than mutual exclusivity.
- Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding under Excludes2 notes.
- Updated interpretation allowing simultaneous assignment of excluded codes.
- Guidance to treat Excludes2 notes as informational rather than restrictive.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `452`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.' An excludes2 note indicates that the condition excluded is not part of the condition represented by the code, but a patient may have both conditions at the same time. When an Excludes2 note appears under a code, it is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together, when appropriate.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Excludes2 Note Logic Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.'
- The excluded condition is not part of the condition represented by the code.
- A patient may have both the coded condition and the excluded condition at the same time.
- It is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together when appropriate.
- The Excludes2 note indicates that the excluded condition is distinct from the primary condition.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must support the presence of both conditions simultaneously.
- The conditions must be clinically distinct and not integral parts of each other.
- The coder must verify that the patient meets criteria for both the primary code and the excluded code.
- Both codes must be assigned only if the clinical scenario justifies concurrent occurrence.
- The Excludes2 note must be interpreted as allowing, not prohibiting, dual coding.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning only the primary code when the excluded condition is also present.
- Treating Excludes2 notes as mandatory exclusions preventing dual coding.
- Assuming the excluded condition is a component of the primary condition.
- Failing to code the excluded condition when clinical evidence supports its presence.
- Using Excludes2 notes to justify coding only one condition when both are documented.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit permission to code both the primary condition and the excluded condition concurrently.
- Clarification that Excludes2 notes indicate non-inclusion rather than mutual exclusivity.
- Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding when an Excludes2 note is present.
- Updated guidance emphasizing that Excludes2 notes do not preclude simultaneous diagnosis.
- Mandatory verification of distinct clinical entities when applying Excludes2 logic.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same core definition for Excludes2 notes, stating that the excluded condition is not part of the primary code but may coexist. While the newer version includes slightly more verbose phrasing in the 'New items' and 'Requirements' sections (e.g., 'Mandatory verification'), the actual clinical logic, deprecated behaviors, and raw text excerpts are functionally identical. There is no substantive change in the coding rule itself, only a reinforcement of the existing guidance.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit permission to code both the primary and excluded conditions together. -> icd (New Version): Explicit permission to code both the primary condition and the excluded condition concurrently.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding under Excludes2 notes. -> icd (New Version): Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding when an Excludes2 note is present.
