### 127. Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines with identical behaviors, requirements, and deprecated items.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `145`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome is a disorder in which a person falsely reports or causes his or her own physical or psychological signs or symptoms. For patients with documented factitious disorder on self or Munchausen's syndrome, assign the appropriate code from subcategory F68.1-, Factitious disorder imposed on self.
>
> Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) is a disorder in which a caregiver (perpetrator) falsely reports or causes an illness or injury in another person (victim) under his or her care, such as a child, an elderly adult, or a person who has a disability. The condition is also referred to as 'factitious disorder imposed on another' or 'factitious disorder by proxy.' The perpetrator, not the victim, receives this diagnosis. Assign code F68.A, Factitious disorder imposed on another, to the perpetrator's record. For the victim of a patient suffering from MSBP, assign the appropriate code from categories T74, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, confirmed, or T76, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, suspected.
>
> See Section I.C.19.f. Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code F68.1- for patients with documented factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome.
- Assign code F68.A to the perpetrator's record for Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) or factitious disorder imposed on another.
- Assign codes from categories T74 or T76 to the victim of a patient suffering from MSBP.
- Distinguish between the perpetrator (diagnosis recipient) and the victim (maltreatment recipient) in MSBP cases.
- Reference Section I.C.19.f for adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment guidelines.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the disorder is imposed on self for F68.1- assignment.
- Documentation must identify the perpetrator as the one causing illness in another for F68.A assignment.
- Documentation must confirm or suspect maltreatment for T74 or T76 assignment to the victim.
- The diagnosis must be assigned to the perpetrator's record, not the victim's, in MSBP cases.
- Clinical documentation must support the distinction between factitious disorder and other abuse categories.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning factitious disorder codes to the victim instead of the perpetrator in MSBP cases.
- Using unspecified codes for factitious disorder without specific documentation of self-imposed or other-imposed nature.
- Coding MSBP under general abuse categories without assigning the specific F68.A code to the perpetrator.
- Failing to reference Section I.C.19.f when coding for adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment.
- Assigning factitious disorder codes to patients without documented evidence of false reporting or causing of symptoms.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit use of F68.1- for factitious disorder imposed on self.
- Explicit use of F68.A for factitious disorder imposed on another.
- Mandatory assignment of T74 or T76 codes to victims of MSBP.
- Clear distinction between perpetrator and victim roles in coding MSBP.
- Integration of Section I.C.19.f guidelines for maltreatment coding.

---

### 128. Substance Use Disorder Remission Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding rules for Substance Use Disorder Remission in categories F10-F19 with identical behavioral instructions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `140`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Selection of codes describing 'in remission' for categories F10-F19, Mental and behavioral disorders due to psychoactive substance use (categories F10-F19 with -.11, -.21, -.91) requires the provider's clinical judgment and are assigned only on the basis of provider documentation (as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting), unless otherwise instructed by the classification.
>
> Mild substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance abuse in remission, and moderate or severe substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance dependence in remission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Substance Use Disorder Remission Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes describing 'in remission' for categories F10-F19 require provider's clinical judgment and are assigned only on the basis of provider documentation.
- Mild substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance abuse in remission.
- Moderate or severe substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance dependence in remission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly support the clinical judgment that the disorder is in remission.
- Classification must distinguish between mild (substance abuse) and moderate/severe (substance dependence) severity levels.
- Codes must utilize the specific remission extensions (e.g., -.11, -.21, -.91) for categories F10-F19.

---

### 129. ICD-10 History Z Code Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the implementation, sequencing, and usage rules for ICD-10 History Z codes.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `350_351`

> **Actual Text**
>
> There are two types of history Z codes, personal and family. Personal history codes explain a patient's past medical condition that no longer exists and is not receiving any treatment, but that has the potential for recurrence, and therefore may require continued monitoring.
>
> Family history codes are for use when a patient has a family member(s) who has had a particular disease that causes the patient to be at higher risk of also contracting the disease.
>
> Personal history codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up codes and family history codes may be used in conjunction with screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure. History codes are also acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit. A history of an illness, even if no longer present, is important information that may alter the type of treatment ordered.
>
> The reason for the encounter (for example, screening or counseling) should be sequenced first and the appropriate personal and/or family history code(s) should be assigned as additional diagnos(es).
>
> The history Z code categories are:
>
> {"Z80": "Z81", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of mental and behavioral disorders"}
> {"Z80": "Z82", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of certain disabilities and chronic diseases (leading to disablement)"}
> {"Z80": "Z83", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of other specific disorders"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 History Z Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Personal history codes explain past medical conditions no longer present but with potential for recurrence requiring monitoring.
- Family history codes are used when a patient has a family member with a disease causing higher risk of contracting it.
- History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit.
- The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening) should be sequenced first, with history codes assigned as additional diagnoses.
- History codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up or screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure.
- Personal history codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up codes to explain the need for a test or procedure.
- Family history codes may be used in conjunction with screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The condition must be a past medical history that no longer exists and is not receiving active treatment.
- The condition must have the potential for recurrence to justify the use of a personal history code.
- The patient must have a family member with a specific disease to justify the use of a family history code.
- The reason for the encounter must be documented to determine the primary sequencing order.
- The history code must be relevant to the current clinical context or monitoring needs.
- The condition must not be receiving any treatment to qualify for a personal history code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding history Z codes as the primary reason for the encounter when a specific reason (e.g., screening) exists.
- Using history codes for conditions that are currently active and receiving treatment.
- Assigning unspecified history codes without clear documentation of the specific past or familial condition.
- Placing history codes before the reason for the encounter in the sequence of diagnosis.
- Using family history codes when there is no documented familial link to the specific disease.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit categorization of Z codes into 'Personal History' (Z80-Z87) and 'Family History' (Z80-Z87) subgroups.
- Mandatory sequencing rule requiring the reason for encounter to be listed before history codes.
- Requirement to document the specific disease or condition for both personal and family history codes.
- Guidance on using history codes in conjunction with follow-up and screening codes to justify procedures.
- Inclusion of specific Z code ranges for family history of primary malignant neoplasms and other specific disorders.
- Specific mapping examples showing Family history of primary malignant neoplasm (Z80) paired with Family history of mental and behavioral disorders (Z81), certain disabilities (Z82), and other specific disorders (Z83).

---

### 130. ICD-10 Coding for Retained Products of Conception
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for retained products of conception, including specific code assignments and complication handling.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `249`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Subsequent encounters for retained products of conception following a spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy , without complications are assigned O03.4, Incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication, or code O07.4, Failed attempted termination of pregnancy without complication. This advice is appropriate even when the patient was discharged previously with a discharge diagnosis of complete abortion. If the patient has a specific complication associated with the spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy in addition to retained products of conception, assign the appropriate complication code (e.g., O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of code O03.4 or O07.4.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Coding for Retained Products of Conception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign O03.4 for subsequent encounters of retained products of conception following spontaneous abortion without complications.
- Assign O07.4 for subsequent encounters of retained products of conception following elective termination of pregnancy without complications.
- Assign appropriate complication codes (O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of O03.4 or O07.4 if a specific complication is present.
- Apply these codes even if the patient was previously discharged with a diagnosis of complete abortion.
- Do not use O03.4 or O07.4 when a specific complication associated with the abortion or termination exists.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the encounter is subsequent to a spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy.
- Documentation must confirm the absence of complications to utilize O03.4 or O07.4.
- If complications are present, documentation must specify the complication to assign the correct O03.-, O04.-, or O07.- code.
- Previous discharge diagnosis of complete abortion does not preclude coding for retained products of conception in subsequent encounters.
- The specific complication code must be assigned in preference to O03.4 or O07.4 when complications are documented.

> Subsequent encounters for retained products of conception following a spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy , without complications are assigned O03.4, Incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication, or code O07.4, Failed attempted termination of pregnancy without complication. If the patient has a specific complication associated with the spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy in addition to retained products of conception, assign the appropriate complication code (e.g., O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of code O03.4 or O07.4.

- Do not assign O03.4 or O07.4 if a specific complication is present, even if the patient was previously discharged with a complete abortion diagnosis.
- Apply these codes for subsequent encounters regardless of prior discharge diagnoses.

**Requirements / Properties:**

### 133. ICD-10-CM Chapter Usage Restrictions
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

*Both chunks describe the exact same feature regarding ICD-10-CM Chapter Usage Restrictions with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `255`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record. Codes from Chapter 15, the obstetric chapter, are never permitted on the newborn record. Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Chapter Usage Restrictions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record.
- Codes from Chapter 15, the obstetric chapter, are never permitted on the newborn record.
- Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Maternal record must not contain codes from the chapter in question.
- Newborn record must not contain codes from Chapter 15 (obstetric chapter).
- Chapter 16 codes require the condition to be still present to be used.

- Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

### 134. ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification with matching definitions and behaviors.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `342`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category Z20 indicates contact with, and suspected exposure to, communicable diseases. These codes are for patients who are suspected to have been exposed to a disease by close personal contact with an infected individual or are in an area where a disease is epidemic.
>
> Category Z77, Other contact with and (suspected) exposures hazardous to health, indicates contact with and suspected exposures hazardous to health.
>
> Contact/exposure codes may be used as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing, or, more commonly, as a secondary code to identify a potential risk.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Category Z20 codes are used for suspected exposure to communicable diseases via close personal contact or epidemic areas.
- Category Z77 codes indicate contact with or suspected exposure to hazards other than communicable diseases.
- Contact/exposure codes may serve as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing.
- Contact/exposure codes are commonly used as secondary codes to identify potential risk factors.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the nature of the exposure (e.g., communicable disease vs. other hazard).
- Documentation must indicate whether the exposure is confirmed or suspected.
- Documentation must identify the source of exposure (e.g., infected individual, epidemic area, hazardous agent).
- The encounter must be related to the exposure (e.g., testing, counseling, or observation).

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `772`

>

>

- Category Z77 codes indicate contact with or suspected exposure to hazards other than communicable diseases.
- Contact/exposure codes may be assigned as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing.
- Contact/exposure codes are commonly assigned as secondary codes to identify a potential risk factor.

**Requirements / Properties:**

- Exposure must be linked to close personal contact with an infected individual or presence in an epidemic area for Z20.
- Exposure must be hazardous to health for Z77 classification.

### 135. ICD-10 T86 Transplant Complication Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature regarding T86 coding requirements with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `313`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes under category T86, Complications of transplanted organs and tissues, are for use for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs. A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ. Two codes are required to fully describe a transplant complication : the appropriate code from category T86 and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> Pre-existing conditions or conditions that develop after the transplant are not coded as complications unless they affect the function of the transplanted organs.
>
> See I.C.21. for transplant organ removal status
>
> See I.C.2. for malignant neoplasm associated with transplanted organ.
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 T86 Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes under category T86 are used for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs.
- A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ.
- Two codes are required to fully describe a transplant complication: the appropriate code from category T86 and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
- Pre-existing conditions or conditions developing after the transplant are not coded as complications unless they affect the function of the transplanted organs.
- Refer to specific guidelines I.C.21, I.C.2, and I.C.1.d.4 for organ removal status, malignant neoplasm association, and sepsis sequencing.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The complication must affect the function of the transplanted organ to be coded.
- Both a T86 category code and a secondary complication code must be assigned.
- Conditions must be verified as affecting organ function before being classified as complications.
- Refer to guideline I.C.21 for determining transplant organ removal status.
- Refer to guideline I.C.2 for coding malignant neoplasms associated with transplanted organs.
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.d.4 for sequencing sepsis due to infection in transplanted organs.

---

### 136. ICD-10 Status Code Definitions
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding ICD-10 Status Code definitions, application rules, and specific sequencing examples.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `344_345_346`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Status codes indicate that a patient is either a carrier of a disease or has the sequelae or residual of a past disease or condition. This includes such things as the presence of prosthetic or mechanical devices resulting from past treatment. A status code is informative, because the status may affect the course of treatment and its outcome. A status code is distinct from a history code. The history code indicates that the patient no longer has the condition.
>
> A status code should not be used with a diagnosis code from one of the body system chapters, if the diagnosis code includes the information provided by the status code. For example, code Z94.1, Heart transplant status, should not be used with a code from subcategory T86.2, Complications of heart transplant. The status code does not provide additional information. The complication code indicates that the patient is a heart transplant patient.
>
> For encounters for weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign a code from subcategory J96.1, Chronic respiratory failure, followed by code Z99.11, Dependence on respirator [ventilator] status.
>
> The status Z codes/categories are:
>
> Z14
>
> Z15
>
> Z16
>
> Z17
>
> Z18
>
> Z19
>
> Genetic carrier
>
> Genetic carrier status indicates that a person carries a gene, associated with a particular disease, which may be passed to offspring who may develop that disease. The person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing the disease.
>
> Genetic susceptibility to disease
>
> Genetic susceptibility indicates that a person has a gene that increases the risk of that person developing the disease.
>
> Codes from category Z15 should not be used as principal or first-listed codes. If the patient has the condition to which he/she is susceptible, and that condition is the reason for the encounter, the code for the current condition should be sequenced first. If the patient is being seen for follow-up after completed treatment for this condition, and the condition no longer exists, a follow-up code should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate personal history and genetic susceptibility codes. If the purpose of the encounter is genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5, Encounter for genetic counseling, should be assigned as the first-listed code, followed by a code from category Z15. Additional codes should be assigned for any applicable family or personal history.
>
> Resistance to antimicrobial drugs
>
> This code indicates that a patient has a condition that is resistant to antimicrobial drug treatment. Sequence the infection code first.
>
> Estrogen , and other hormones and factors receptor status
>
> Retained foreign body fragments
>
> Hormone sensitivity malignancy status
>
> - Z21 Asymptomatic HIV infection status This code indicates that a patient has tested positive for HIV but has manifested no signs or symptoms of the disease.
> - Z22 Carrier of infectious disease Carrier status indicates that a person harbors the specific organisms of a disease without manifest symptoms and is capable of transmitting the infection.
> - Z28.3 Underimmunization status
>
> See Section I.B.14. for underimmunization documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider .
>
> - Z33.1 Pregnant state, incidental This code is a secondary code only for use when the pregnancy is in no way complicating the reason for visit. Otherwise, a code from the obstetric chapter is required.
> - Z66 Do not resuscitate This code may be used when it is documented by the provider that a patient is on do not resuscitate status at any time during the stay.
> - Z67 Blood type Z68 Body mass index (BMI) BMI codes should only be assigned when there is an associated, reportable diagnosis (such as obesity). Do not assign BMI codes during pregnancy.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for BMI documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider .
>
> {"Z74.01": "Z76.82 Z78", "Bed confinement status": "Awaiting organ transplant status Other specified health status Code Z78.1, Physical restraint status, may be used when it is documented by the provider that a patient has been put in restraints during the current encounter. Please note that this code should not be reported when it is documented by the provider that a patient is temporarily restrained during a procedure."}
> {"Z74.01": "Z79", "Bed confinement status": "Long-term (current) drug therapy Codes from this category indicate a patient's continuous use of a prescribed drug (including such things as aspirin therapy) for the long-term treatment of a condition or for prophylactic use. It is not for use for patients who have addictions to drugs. This subcategory is not for use of medications for detoxification or maintenance programs to prevent withdrawal symptoms (e.g., methadone maintenance for opiate dependence). Assign the appropriate code for the drug use, abuse, or dependence instead."}
>
> {"": "Z88", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Allergy status to drugs, medicaments and biological substances"}
> {"": "Z89", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Acquired absence of limb"}
> {"": "Z90", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Acquired absence of organs, not elsewhere classified"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Status Code Definitions and Application

**Key Behaviors:**
- Status codes indicate a patient is a carrier of a disease, has sequelae of a past condition, or has prosthetic/mechanical devices, and are distinct from history codes which indicate the condition no longer exists.
- Status codes should not be used with diagnosis codes from body system chapters if the diagnosis code already includes the information provided by the status code.
- For encounters for weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign code J96.1 (Chronic respiratory failure) followed by Z99.11 (Dependence on respirator status).
- Codes from category Z15 (Genetic susceptibility) should not be used as principal or first-listed codes unless the patient is being seen for genetic counseling associated with procreative management.
- BMI codes (Z68) should only be assigned when there is an associated, reportable diagnosis (such as obesity) and must not be assigned during pregnancy.
- Genetic carrier status indicates the person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing it.
- Resistance to antimicrobial drugs requires sequencing the infection code first.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient is a carrier, has sequelae, or has a prosthetic device to justify a status code.
- If a diagnosis code includes the information provided by a status code, the status code must not be used.
- For genetic susceptibility (Z15), the condition must not be the reason for the encounter unless it is a follow-up after completed treatment where the condition no longer exists.
- BMI codes require an associated reportable diagnosis and are prohibited during pregnancy.
- Physical restraint status (Z78.1) may only be reported if documented by the provider during the current encounter, not for temporary restraints during a procedure.
- Documentation must confirm the patient is a carrier, has sequelae, or has a prosthetic device.
- Z15 codes cannot be principal/first-listed unless for genetic counseling associated with procreative management.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using status codes with diagnosis codes from body system chapters when the diagnosis code already provides the necessary information.
- Using Z15 codes as principal or first-listed codes for patients with the condition to which they are susceptible.
- Assigning BMI codes (Z68) during pregnancy.
- Using Z79 for medication administered for a brief period to treat an acute illness or injury.
- Reporting physical restraint status (Z78.1) when the restraint was temporary and occurred during a procedure.

**New in this version:**
- Specific status codes for genetic carriers (Z14), genetic susceptibility (Z15), and resistance to antimicrobial drugs.
- Status codes for retained foreign body fragments and hormone sensitivity malignancy status.
- Status code Z33.1 for incidental pregnant state when pregnancy does not complicate the reason for visit.
- Status code Z66 for Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) status documented by the provider.
- Status codes for blood type (Z67) and body mass index (Z68) with specific assignment rules.

>
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z14 Genetic carrier Genetic carrier status indicates that a person carries a gene, associated with a particular disease, which may be passed to offspring who may develop that disease. The person does not"}
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z15 Genetic susceptibility to disease Genetic susceptibility indicates that a person has a gene that increases the risk of that person developing the disease."}
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z16 Codes from category Z15 should generally not be used as principal or first-listed codes. If the patient has the condition to which he/she is susceptible, and that condition is the reason for the encounter, the code for the current condition should sequenced first. If the patient is being seen for follow-up after completed treatment for this condition, and the condition no longer exists, a follow-up code should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate personal history and genetic susceptibility codes. If the purpose of the encounter is genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5, Encounter for genetic counseling, should be assigned as the first-listed code, followed by a code from category Z15. Additional codes should be assigned for any applicable family or personal history."}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Status Code Definitions and Application

**Key Behaviors:**
- Status codes indicate a patient is a carrier of a disease or has sequelae/residuals of a past condition, including prosthetic devices.
- Status codes are distinct from history codes, which indicate the patient no longer has the condition.
- Status codes should not be used with diagnosis codes from body system chapters if the diagnosis code already includes the status information.
- For encounters involving weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign Chronic respiratory failure followed by Dependence on respirator status.
- Genetic susceptibility codes (Z15) generally should not be used as principal or first-listed codes unless the patient has the condition or is being seen for follow-up after treatment.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Status codes must be assigned when the patient carries a gene associated with a disease that may be passed to offspring.
- Status codes must be assigned when the patient has sequelae or residuals of a past disease or condition.
- Status codes must be assigned for the presence of prosthetic or mechanical devices resulting from past treatment.
- When a status code is assigned, it must not duplicate information already provided by a specific diagnosis code from a body system chapter.
- For genetic susceptibility encounters, sequencing rules require the current condition or follow-up code to be listed first over the susceptibility code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using status codes in conjunction with diagnosis codes from body system chapters when the diagnosis code already encompasses the status information.
- Assigning genetic susceptibility codes (Z15) as principal or first-listed codes when the patient has the actual condition or is in follow-up after treatment.
- Failing to distinguish between status codes (current carrier/residual) and history codes (no longer has condition).

**New in this version:**
- Explicit definition of status codes covering carriers, sequelae, residuals, and prosthetic devices.
- Specific instruction to sequence Chronic respiratory failure (J96.1) followed by Dependence on respirator status (Z99.11) for weaning encounters.
- Clarified sequencing hierarchy for genetic susceptibility (Z15) versus current conditions and genetic counseling encounters (Z31.5).
- Genetic carrier status (Z14) is defined as carrying a gene associated with a disease that may be passed to offspring, where the person does not currently have the disease.
- Genetic susceptibility (Z15) is defined as having a gene that increases the risk of developing a disease.
- For genetic counseling encounters (Z31.5), this code must be assigned as the first-listed code followed by a code from category Z15.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided a general definition of status codes and basic sequencing rules, while the newer version refines these by explicitly defining 'Genetic carrier' (Z14) as carrying a gene without the disease and 'Genetic susceptibility' (Z15) as having a gene that increases risk. The newer version also clarifies the sequencing hierarchy for genetic counseling encounters (Z31.5) versus susceptibility codes, which was less explicit in the older text. Both versions maintain the core rule that status codes should not be combined with diagnosis codes that already include the status information, but the newer version provides more granular definitions to reduce coder variance.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Status codes indicate a patient is a carrier of a disease or has sequelae, with general sequencing rules. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly defines 'Genetic carrier' (Z14) as carrying a gene without the disease and 'Genetic susceptibility' (Z15) as having a gene that increases risk, with specific sequencing hierarchy for genetic counseling encounters.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions BMI codes (Z68) require an associated reportable diagnosis and must not be assigned during pregnancy. -> icd (New Version): Focuses on the explicit definition of status codes covering carriers, sequelae, residuals, and prosthetic devices, with clarified sequencing for weaning encounters.

*Traceability: Chunk `344_345_346` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `774_775` (icd (New Version))*

### 137. ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines feature with consistent behaviors and requirements across versions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `307`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When coding a poisoning or reaction to the improper use of a medication (e.g., overdose, wrong substance given or taken in error, wrong route of administration), first assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50. The poisoning codes have an associated intent as their 5 th or 6 th character (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined). If the intent of the poisoning is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. The undetermined intent is only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined. Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
>
> If there is also a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance, the abuse or dependence is assigned as an additional code.
>
> Examples of poisoning include:
>
> - (i)
> - Error was made in drug prescription
>
> Errors made in drug prescription or in the administration of the drug by provider, nurse, patient, or other person.
>
> - (ii) Overdose of a drug intentionally taken administered and resulted in drug toxicity, it would be
>
> If an overdose of a drug was intentionally taken or coded as a poisoning.
>
> - (iii)Nonprescribed drug taken with correctly prescribed and properly administered drug
>
> If a nonprescribed drug or medicinal agent was taken in combination with a correctly prescribed and properly administered drug, any drug toxicity or other reaction resulting from the interaction of the two drugs would be classified as a poisoning.
>
> - (iv)
> - Interaction of drug(s) and alcohol When a reaction results from the interaction of a drug(s)
>
> and alcohol, this would be classified as poisoning.
>
> See Section I.C.4. if poisoning is the result of insulin pump malfunctions.
>
> For Sequela (Late Effects) see Section I.B.10.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- First assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50 for poisoning or reaction to improper use of medication.
- Assign the associated intent (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, undetermined) as the 5th or 6th character.
- Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined if documentation explicitly states intent cannot be determined.
- Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
- Assign abuse or dependence as an additional code if a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance exists.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the intent of the poisoning to assign the correct 5th or 6th character.
- If intent is not documented, it must be coded as accidental unless the record explicitly states it cannot be determined.
- All manifestations of the poisoning must be coded with additional codes.
- Abuse or dependence diagnoses must be coded as additional codes when present alongside poisoning.
- Drug interactions (including with alcohol) resulting in toxicity must be classified as poisoning.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding poisoning as a disease process rather than an external cause.
- Assigning unspecified intent when documentation allows for determination of accidental, intentional, or undetermined.
- Failing to code manifestations of poisoning as additional codes.
- Omitting additional codes for abuse or dependence when present.
- Classifying drug interactions or nonprescribed drug combinations as non-poisoning conditions.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to code intent as accidental if unspecified or unknown.
- Mandatory use of undetermined intent only when documentation explicitly states intent cannot be determined.
- Requirement to code all manifestations of poisoning as additional codes.
- Inclusion of abuse or dependence as additional codes when present.
- Classification of drug-alcohol interactions and nonprescribed drug combinations as poisoning.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `734`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When coding a poisoning or reaction to the improper use of a medication (e.g., overdose, wrong substance given or taken in error, wrong route of administration), first assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50. The poisoning codes have an associated intent as their 5 th or 6 th character (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined). If the intent of the poisoning is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. The undetermined intent is only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined. Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
>
> If there is also a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance, the abuse or dependence is assigned as an additional code.
>
> Examples of poisoning include:
>
> - (i) Error was made in drug prescription Errors made in drug prescription or in the administration of the drug by provider, nurse, patient, or other person.
> - (ii) Overdose of a drug intentionally taken If an overdose of a drug was intentionally taken or administered and resulted in drug toxicity, it would be coded as a poisoning.
>
> - (iii)Nonprescribed drug taken with correctly prescribed and properly administered drug
>
> If a nonprescribed drug or medicinal agent was taken in combination with a correctly prescribed and properly administered drug, any drug toxicity or other reaction resulting from the interaction of the two drugs would be classified as a poisoning.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- First assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50 for poisoning or reaction to improper use of medication.
- Assign the associated intent (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, undetermined) as the 5th or 6th character.
- Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined if documentation explicitly states intent cannot be determined.
- Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
- Assign abuse or dependence as an additional code if a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance exists.
- Classify toxicity resulting from the interaction between a nonprescribed drug and a correctly prescribed drug as poisoning.
- Include errors made in drug prescription or administration by providers, nurses, patients, or other persons as examples of poisoning.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the intent of the poisoning to assign the correct 5th or 6th character.
- If intent is not documented, it must be coded as accidental unless the record explicitly states it cannot be determined.
- All manifestations of the poisoning must be coded using additional codes.
- Abuse or dependence diagnoses must be coded as additional codes when present alongside poisoning.
- Toxicity resulting from drug interactions (nonprescribed with prescribed) must be classified as poisoning.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical logic for assigning poisoning codes, including the rule to default to accidental intent when unspecified and the requirement to code manifestations as additional codes. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the examples and a slight rephrasing of the drug interaction rule, which do not alter the underlying coding behavior. No new clinical restrictions, definitions, or reversals were introduced between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Classifies drug interactions (including with alcohol) resulting in toxicity as poisoning. -> icd (New Version): Classifies toxicity resulting from the interaction between a nonprescribed drug and a correctly prescribed drug as poisoning.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Error was made in drug prescription' as a separate example item. -> icd (New Version): Combines 'Error was made in drug prescription' and 'Errors made in drug prescription or in the administration...' into a single consolidated example item.

*Traceability: Chunk `307` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `734` (icd (New Version))*

### 138. ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding ICD-10-CM prophylactic surgery coding rules across versions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `371`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For encounters specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ (such as prophylactic removal of breasts due to a genetic susceptibility to cancer or a family history of cancer), the principal or first-listed code should be a code from category Z40, Encounter for prophylactic surgery, followed by the appropriate codes to identify the associated risk factor (such as genetic susceptibility or family history).
>
> If the patient has a malignancy of one site and is having prophylactic removal at another site to prevent either a new primary malignancy or metastatic disease, a code for the malignancy should also be assigned in addition to a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms. A Z40.0 code should not be assigned if the patient is having organ removal for treatment of a malignancy, such as the removal of the testes for the treatment of prostate cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign category Z40 as the principal or first-listed code for encounters specifically for prophylactic organ removal.
- Follow the Z40 code with appropriate codes to identify associated risk factors such as genetic susceptibility or family history.
- Assign a code for an existing malignancy in addition to a Z40.0 code when prophylactic removal is performed at a different site to prevent new primary or metastatic disease.
- Do not assign a Z40.0 code if the organ removal is for the treatment of an existing malignancy.
- Use subcategory Z40.0 specifically for encounters related to risk factors for malignant neoplasms.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The encounter must be specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ.
- Documentation must identify the associated risk factor (e.g., genetic susceptibility, family history).
- If an existing malignancy is present, it must be documented and coded alongside the prophylactic surgery code.
- The procedure must not be for the treatment of a malignancy at the same site.
- The Z40.0 code must be used only when the surgery is for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding organ removal for treatment of a malignancy as a prophylactic surgery (Z40) instead of a treatment code.
- Omitting the code for an existing malignancy when prophylactic surgery is performed at a different site.
- Using Z40 codes without identifying the associated risk factor.
- Assigning Z40.0 for non-malignant risk factors when the guideline specifies its use for malignant neoplasm risk factors.
- Listing the prophylactic surgery code as secondary when it is the principal reason for the encounter.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to code the associated risk factor (genetic susceptibility or family history) alongside the Z40 code.
- Clarification that Z40.0 is reserved for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms.
- Mandatory inclusion of an existing malignancy code when prophylactic surgery is performed to prevent metastasis or new primary at another site.
- Strict prohibition against using Z40 for therapeutic organ removal related to active malignancy treatment.
- Designation of Z40 as the principal diagnosis for encounters solely for prophylactic organ removal.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `801`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For encounters specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ (such as prophylactic removal of breasts due to a genetic susceptibility to cancer or a family history of cancer), the principal or first-listed code should be a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms, or subcategory Z40.8, Encounter for other prophylactic surgery. If applicable, assign additional code(s) to identify any associated risk factor (such as genetic susceptibility or family history).
>
> If the patient has a malignancy of one site and is having prophylactic removal at another site to prevent either a new primary malignancy or metastatic disease, a code for the malignancy should also be assigned in addition to a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms. A Z40.0 code should not be assigned if the patient is having organ removal for treatment of a malignancy, such as the removal of the testes for the treatment of prostate cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign principal code from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8 for encounters specifically for prophylactic organ removal due to risk factors.
- Assign additional codes to identify associated risk factors such as genetic susceptibility or family history.
- Assign a code for an existing malignancy at one site in addition to a Z40.0 code when prophylactic removal is performed at another site to prevent new primary or metastatic disease.
- Do not assign a Z40.0 code if the organ removal is for the treatment of an existing malignancy.
- Ensure the encounter is specifically for prophylactic removal, not therapeutic treatment of a known cancer.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the encounter is specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ.
- Documentation must identify the specific risk factor (e.g., genetic susceptibility, family history) if applicable.
- Documentation must distinguish between prophylactic removal and removal for treatment of an existing malignancy.
- If a malignancy exists at a different site, documentation must support the intent to prevent new primary or metastatic disease.
- The principal diagnosis must be selected from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8 based on the specific prophylactic procedure.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a Z40.0 code when the organ removal is for the treatment of a malignancy.
- Failing to assign additional codes for associated risk factors when present.
- Coding prophylactic surgery as a complication or sequela of a malignancy rather than a distinct encounter.
- Using unspecified codes for prophylactic surgery without specifying the risk factor or organ.
- Omitting the code for an existing malignancy when prophylactic surgery is performed at a different site.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to assign additional codes for associated risk factors like genetic susceptibility or family history.
- Clarification that Z40.0 codes are not to be used for therapeutic organ removal related to existing malignancies.
- Mandatory inclusion of a code for an existing malignancy when prophylactic surgery is performed at a different site to prevent metastasis.
- Specific guidance to use subcategory Z40.0 for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms and Z40.8 for other prophylactic surgeries.
- Emphasis on distinguishing prophylactic encounters from therapeutic encounters in the principal diagnosis selection.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the core rules for Z40 coding but did not explicitly list Z40.8 as a valid principal diagnosis option in its raw text excerpt. The newer version clarifies this by explicitly including subcategory Z40.8 (Encounter for other prophylactic surgery) alongside Z40.0 in the principal diagnosis selection instructions. Both versions maintain the strict prohibition against using Z40 for therapeutic removal of existing malignancies and the requirement to code concurrent malignancies at different sites, but the newer version expands the explicit scope of acceptable principal codes.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Principal code should be from category Z40, followed by risk factor codes. -> icd (New Version): Principal code should be from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8, followed by risk factor codes.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Z40.0 is used for malignant neoplasm risk factors. -> icd (New Version): Z40.0 is used for malignant neoplasm risk factors, while Z40.8 is explicitly added for other prophylactic surgeries.

*Traceability: Chunk `371` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `801` (icd (New Version))*

### 139. ICD-10-CM Pressure Ulcer Classification
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10-CM Pressure Ulcer Classification with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `194`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes in category L89, Pressure ulcer, identify the site and stage of the pressure ulcer.
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies pressure ulcer stages based on severity, which is designated by stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury , unspecified stage, and unstageable.
>
> Assign as many codes from category L89 as needed to identify all the pressure ulcers the patient has, if applicable.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for pressure ulcer stage documentation by clinicians other than patient's provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Pressure Ulcer Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in category L89 must identify both the anatomical site and the stage of the pressure ulcer.
- Staging is designated by severity levels: stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified stage, and unstageable.
- Multiple codes from category L89 should be assigned to capture all applicable pressure ulcers a patient has.
- Clinicians other than the patient's provider must follow specific documentation guidelines referenced in Section I.B.14.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the anatomical site of the pressure ulcer.
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the stage of the pressure ulcer (1-4, deep tissue, unspecified, or unstageable).
- All pressure ulcers present must be identified and coded individually if applicable.

### 141. ICD-10-CM Gestation Coding Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical ICD-10-CM Gestation Coding Rules regarding the assignment of codes based on full completed weeks.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `220`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In ICD-10-CM, 'completed' weeks of gestation refers to full weeks. For example, if the provider documents gestation at 39 weeks and 6 days, the code for 39 weeks of gestation should be assigned, as the patient has not yet reached 40 completed weeks.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Gestation Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes based on full completed weeks of gestation only, ignoring additional days.
- If gestation is documented as 39 weeks and 6 days, assign the code for 39 weeks.
- Do not round up to the next week if the patient has not reached the full number of completed weeks.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify the exact number of completed weeks.
- The patient must not have reached the next full week of gestation for the lower week code to be valid.

---

- These codes address occasions where non-disease circumstances are documented as the primary reason for the encounter.

- The circumstance must not be a disease or injury.

### 143. ICD-10-CM Z Code Reporting Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Z code assignment guidelines with identical behavioral rules and code lists.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `49`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes (other reasons for healthcare encounters) may be assigned as appropriate to further explain the reasons for presenting for healthcare services, including transfers between healthcare facilities, or provide additional information relevant to a patient encounter. The ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting identify which codes maybe assigned as principal or first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or principal/first-listed or secondary (depending on the circumstances). Possible applicable Z codes include:
>
> - Z59.0-, Homelessness
> - Z59.1, Inadequate housing
> - Z59.5, Extreme poverty
> - Z75.1, Person awaiting admission to adequate facility elsewhere
> - Z75.3, Unavailability and inaccessibility of health-care facilities
> - Z75.4, Unavailability and inaccessibility of other helping agencies
> - Z76.2, Encounter for health supervision and care of other healthy infant and child
> - Z99.12, Encounter for respirator [ventilator] dependence during power failure
>
> The external cause of morbidity codes and the Z codes listed above are not an all-inclusive list. Other codes may be applicable to the encounter based upon the documentation. Assign as many codes as necessary to fully explain each healthcare encounter. Since patient history information may be very limited, use any available documentation to assign the appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Z Code Reporting Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes may be assigned as appropriate to further explain reasons for healthcare services, including transfers between facilities.
- Assign as many codes as necessary to fully explain each healthcare encounter.
- Use any available documentation to assign appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes when patient history is limited.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Codes must be assigned based on whether they are designated as principal/first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or both depending on circumstances.
- Documentation must support the assignment of Z codes to provide additional information relevant to the patient encounter.

**New in this version:**
- Specific Z codes listed include Z59.0- (Homelessness), Z59.1 (Inadequate housing), Z59.5 (Extreme poverty), Z75.1-Z75.4 (Facility/Agency unavailability), Z76.2 (Health supervision), and Z99.12 (Respirator dependence during power failure).
- External cause of morbidity codes and the listed Z codes are not an all-inclusive list; other applicable codes may be used based on documentation.

### 144. Leukemia and Myeloma Remission Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature regarding leukemia and myeloma remission coding with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `121`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The categories for leukemia, and category C90, Multiple myeloma and malignant plasma cell neoplasms, have codes indicating whether or not the leukemia has achieved remission. There are also codes Z85.6, Personal history of leukemia, and Z85.79, Personal history of other malignant neoplasms of lymphoid, hematopoietic and related tissues. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the leukemia has achieved remission, the provider should be queried.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Leukemia and Myeloma Remission Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign specific remission status codes for leukemia and multiple myeloma based on documented achievement of remission.
- Utilize personal history codes Z85.6 and Z85.79 when the current condition is not active.
- Query the provider for clarification if documentation is unclear regarding whether remission has been achieved.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state whether the leukemia or myeloma has achieved remission.
- Codes must be selected from the specific categories for leukemia and category C90 for multiple myeloma.
- If remission status is ambiguous, provider clarification is a mandatory prerequisite for code assignment.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory inclusion of personal history codes Z85.6 and Z85.79 for past malignancies of lymphoid, hematopoietic, and related tissues.

---

> **Actual Text**
>

If the documentation is unclear as to whether the leukemia has achieved remission, the provider should be queried.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

- Personal history codes (Z85.6, Z85.79) are required for past malignancies of lymphoid, hematopoietic, and related tissues.

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes E10.A-, Type 1 diabetes mellitus, presymptomatic, are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Presymptomatic Type 1 Diabetes Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms.

**Requirements / Properties:**

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `557`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes E10.A-, Type 1 diabetes mellitus, presymptomatic, are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Presymptomatic Type 1 Diabetes Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms.
- Coding is restricted to cases where the diagnosis is confirmed before clinical symptoms appear.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly confirm the diagnosis of Type 1 diabetes mellitus prior to symptom onset.
- The condition must be identified as presymptomatic to utilize the E10.A- code series.

#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the rule that codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes predating symptoms. The newer version retains this core logic but adds explicit constraints requiring clinical documentation to confirm the diagnosis prior to symptom onset and explicitly stating the condition must be identified as presymptomatic to utilize the code series. This represents a clarification that reinforces the specific documentation requirements to prevent misclassification of symptomatic cases.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms. -> icd (New Version): Codes E10.A- are assigned for early-stage type 1 diabetes that predates the onset of symptoms; coding is restricted to cases where the diagnosis is confirmed before clinical symptoms appear.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Clinical documentation must confirm the diagnosis is early-stage type 1 diabetes. -> icd (New Version): Clinical documentation must explicitly confirm the diagnosis of Type 1 diabetes mellitus prior to symptom onset; the condition must be identified as presymptomatic to utilize the E10.A- code series.

### 146. Unqualified Visual Loss Coding Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical coding logic for unqualified visual loss using the exact same behavioral rules and code assignments.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `163`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If 'blindness' or 'low vision' of both eyes is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign code H54.3, Unqualified visual loss, both eyes. If 'blindness' or 'low vision' in one eye is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign a code from H54.6-, Unqualified visual loss, one eye. If 'blindness' or 'visual loss' is documented without any information about whether one or both eyes are affected, assign code H54.7, Unspecified visual loss.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unqualified Visual Loss Coding Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a visual impairment category.
- Assign a code from H54.6- if blindness or low vision in one eye is documented without a visual impairment category.
- Assign code H54.7 if visual loss is documented without information on whether one or both eyes are affected.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify 'blindness' or 'low vision' to trigger unqualified visual loss codes.
- Documentation must indicate the number of eyes affected (one or both) to distinguish between H54.3 and H54.6-.
- Absence of a documented visual impairment category is required to default to unqualified codes.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `591`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If 'blindness' or 'low vision' of both eyes is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign code H54.3, Unqualified visual loss, both eyes. If 'blindness' or 'low vision' in one eye is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign a code from H54.6-, Unqualified visual loss, one eye. If 'blindness' or 'visual loss' is documented without any information about whether one or both eyes are affected, assign code H54.7, Unspecified visual loss.

- Assign code H54.7 if visual loss is documented without information on whether one or both eyes are affected.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must specify 'blindness' or 'low vision' to trigger unqualified visual loss codes.
- Documentation must indicate the number of eyes affected (both or one) to distinguish between H54.3 and H54.6-.
- Absence of a documented visual impairment category is required to apply the unqualified visual loss codes.

There are no substantive changes to the clinical logic, code selection criteria, or documentation requirements between the two texts. The differences are limited to minor phrasing variations in the 'Requirements' section (e.g., 'distinguish' vs 'distinguish', 'apply' vs 'default to'), which do not alter the operational outcome.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a visual impairment category. -> icd (New Version): Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a visual impairment category.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code H54.7 if visual loss is documented without information on whether one or both eyes are affected. -> icd (New Version): Assign code H54.7 if visual loss is documented without information on whether one or both eyes are affected.

### 147. Fracture Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature, 'Fracture Coding Guidelines', with identical behaviors and requirements across the two versions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `287_288_290`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The principles of multiple coding of injuries should be followed in coding fractures. Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with both the provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92 and the level of detail furnished by medical record content.
>
> A fracture not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed. A fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
>
> More specific guidelines are as follows:
>
> Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture. The appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter should also be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
>
> Fractures are coded using the appropriate 7 th character for subsequent care for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the fracture and is receiving routine care for the fracture during the healing or recovery phase.
>
> Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.
>
> Care of complications of fractures, such as malunion and nonunion, should be reported with the appropriate 7 th character for subsequent care with nonunion (K, M, N,) or subsequent care with malunion (P, Q, R).
>
> Malunion/nonunion: The appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter should also be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
>
> The open fracture designations in the assignment of the 7th character for fractures of the forearm, femur and lower leg, including ankle are based on the Gustilo open fracture classification. When the Gustilo classification type is not specified for an open fracture, the 7th character for open fracture type I or II should be assigned (B, E, H, M, Q).
>
> A code from category M80, not a traumatic fracture code, should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma, if that fall or trauma would not usually break a normal, healthy bone.
>
> See Section I.C.13. Osteoporosis.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures. For aftercare of a traumatic fracture, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7 th character.
>
> For physeal fractures, assign only the code identifying the type of physeal fracture. Do not assign a separate code to identify the specific bone that is fractured.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Fracture Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with category provisions and medical record detail.
- Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- Fractures not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Fractures must be coded individually by site in accordance with provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92.
- The appropriate 7th character for initial encounter must be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
- Fractures must be coded using the appropriate 7th character for subsequent care for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment.
- Open fracture designations for the forearm, femur, and lower leg must be based on the Gustilo open fracture classification.
- A code from category M80 must be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using unspecified laterality codes when documentation does not specify the side.
- Using aftercare Z codes for aftercare of traumatic fractures.
- Assigning a separate code to identify the specific bone fractured for physeal fractures.
- Coding a fracture not indicated as open or closed to open.
- Coding a fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced to nondisplaced.

**New in this version:**
- The appropriate 7th character for subsequent care with nonunion (K, M, N) or subsequent care with malunion (P, Q, R) should be used for care of complications of fractures.
- When the Gustilo classification type is not specified for an open fracture, the 7th character for open fracture type I or II should be assigned (B, E, H, M, Q).
- For physeal fractures, only the code identifying the type of physeal fracture should be assigned.
- The principles of multiple coding of injuries should be followed in coding fractures.
- Care of complications of fractures, such as malunion and nonunion, should be reported with the appropriate 7th character for subsequent care.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `714_715_717`

When the Gustilo classification type is not specified for an open fracture, the 7th character for open fracture type I or II should be assigned (B, E, H, M, Q).
>

>
> See Section I.C.13. Osteoporosis.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures. For aftercare of a traumatic fracture, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7 th character.
>

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Fracture Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with category provisions and medical record detail.
- Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- Fractures not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Fractures must be coded individually by site in accordance with provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92.
- The 7th character for initial encounter must be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
- For open fractures of the forearm, femur, and lower leg, the Gustilo classification type must be used to determine the 7th character.
- A code from category M80 must be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures; instead, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7th character.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using unspecified laterality codes when documentation does not specify the side.
- Using aftercare Z codes for aftercare of traumatic fractures.
- Assigning a separate code to identify the specific bone fractured for physeal fractures.
- Coding a fracture not indicated as open or closed to open.
- Coding a fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced to nondisplaced.

**New in this version:**
- The appropriate 7th character for subsequent care with nonunion (K, M, N) or subsequent care with malunion (P, Q, R) should be used for care of complications of fractures.
- The 7th character for initial encounter should also be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
- When the Gustilo classification type is not specified for an open fracture, the 7th character for open fracture type I or II should be assigned (B, E, H, M, Q).
- For physeal fractures, only the code identifying the type of physeal fracture should be assigned.
- A code from category M80 should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The two versions present identical clinical logic regarding fracture coding, including default rules for open/closed status, displaced/non-displaced status, and the use of 7th characters. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the 'Requirements' and 'New items' sections, such as the explicit mention of 'Gustilo classification type' in the new version versus a general reference to the classification in the older version, and slight rewording of the osteoporosis rule. These changes do not alter the clinical logic or code assignment rules but serve to clarify or standardize the administrative presentation of the guidelines.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Open fracture designations for the forearm, femur, and lower leg must be based on the Gustilo open fracture classification. -> icd (New Version): For open fractures of the forearm, femur, and lower leg, the Gustilo classification type must be used to determine the 7th character.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): A code from category M80 must be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma. -> icd (New Version): A code from category M80 should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma.

*Traceability: Chunk `287_288_290` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `714_715_717` (icd (New Version))*
