### 170. FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Neoplasm Coding Guidelines, with the second chunk erroneously labeled as FY 2026 but containing identical FY 2025 content.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `0_2_97`

> **Actual Text**
>
> FY 2025 -- UPDATED October 1, 2024 (October 1, 2024 - September 30, 2025)
>
> Narrative changes appear in bold text
>
> Items underlined have been moved within the guidelines since the April 2024, FY 2024 version Italics are used to indicate revisions to heading changes
>
> The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), two departments within the U.S. Federal Government's Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) provide the following guidelines for coding and reporting using the International Classification of Diseases, 10 th Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-10-CM). These guidelines should be used as a companion document to the official version of the ICD-10-CM as published on the NCHS website. The ICD-10-CM is a morbidity classification published by the United States for classifying diagnoses and reason for visits in all health care settings. The ICD10-CM is based on the ICD-10, the statistical classification of disease published by the World Health Organization (WHO).
>
> These guidelines have been approved by the four organizations that make up the Cooperating Parties for the ICD-10-CM: the American Hospital Association (AHA), the American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA), CMS, and NCHS.
>
> These guidelines are a set of rules that have been developed to accompany and complement the official conventions and instructions provided within the ICD-10-CM itself. The instructions and conventions of the classification take precedence over guidelines. These guidelines are based on the coding and sequencing instructions in the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index of ICD-10-CM, but provide additional instruction. Adherence to these guidelines when assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes is required under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). The diagnosis codes (Tabular List and Alphabetic Index) have been adopted under HIPAA for all healthcare settings. A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. These guidelines have been developed to assist both the healthcare provider and the coder in identifying those diagnoses that are to be reported. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved. The entire record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
>
> The term encounter is used for all settings, including hospital admissions. In the context of these guidelines, the term provider is used throughout the guidelines to mean physician or any qualified health care practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis. Only this set of guidelines, approved by the Cooperating Parties, is official.
>
> The guidelines are organized into sections. Section I includes the structure and conventions of the classification and general guidelines that apply to the entire classification, and chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Section II includes guidelines for selection of principal diagnosis for non-outpatient settings. Section III includes guidelines for reporting additional diagnoses in non-outpatient settings. Section IV is for outpatient coding and reporting. It is necessary to review all sections of the guidelines to fully understand all of the rules and instructions needed to code properly.
>
> {"b.": "c.", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "Other External Causes of Morbidity Code Issues.................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "18"}
> {"b.": "d.", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "Use of Z codes ......................................................................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "18"}
> {"b.": "C. Chapter-Specific Coding Guidelines ............................................................................................", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "C. Chapter-Specific Coding Guidelines ............................................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "19"}
> [... +43 more table rows omitted ...]
>
> Chapter 2 of the ICD-10-CM contains the codes for most benign and all malignant neoplasms. Certain benign neoplasms, such as prostatic adenomas, may be found in the specific body system chapters. To properly code a neoplasm, it is necessary to determine from the record if the neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior. If malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites should also be determined.
>
> Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping site boundaries
>
> A primary malignant neoplasm that overlaps two or more contiguous (next to each other) sites should be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion'), unless the combination is specifically indexed elsewhere. For multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous such as tumors in different quadrants of the same breast, codes for each site should be assigned.
>
> Malignant neoplasm of ectopic tissue
>
> Malignant neoplasms of ectopic tissue are to be coded to the site of origin mentioned, e.g., ectopic pancreatic malignant neoplasms involving the stomach are coded to malignant neoplasm of pancreas, unspecified (C25.9).
>
> The neoplasm table in the Alphabetic Index should be referenced first. However, if the histological term is documented, that term should be referenced first, rather than going immediately to the Neoplasm Table, in order to determine which column in the Neoplasm Table is appropriate. For example, if the documentation indicates 'adenoma,' refer to the term in the Alphabetic Index to review the entries under this term and the instructional note to 'see also neoplasm, by site, benign.' The table provides the proper code based on the type of neoplasm and the site. It is important to select the proper column in the table that corresponds to the type of neoplasm. The Tabular List should then be referenced to verify that the correct code has been selected from the table and that a more specific site code does not exist.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Status, for information regarding Z15.0, codes for genetic susceptibility to cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Neoplasm Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping two or more contiguous sites must be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion') unless specifically indexed elsewhere.
- Malignant neoplasms of ectopic tissue must be coded to the site of origin mentioned (e.g., ectopic pancreatic malignant neoplasm coded to pancreas).
- The neoplasm table in the Alphabetic Index should be referenced first, but if the histological term is documented, that term must be referenced first to determine the appropriate column in the Neoplasm Table.
- The Tabular List must be referenced to verify the correct code and ensure a more specific site code does not exist.
- Codes for genetic susceptibility to cancer (Z15.0) are referenced under Section I.C.21 regarding factors influencing health status.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- It is necessary to determine from the record if the neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior.
- If malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites must also be determined.
- For multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous, codes for each site should be assigned.
- Adherence to these guidelines when assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes is required under HIPAA.
- The instructions and conventions of the classification take precedence over guidelines.
- A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures.
- The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized; without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved.

**New in this version:**
- Updated sequencing rules for external causes of morbidity codes (Section I.B.2 and I.B.3).
- Revised chapter-specific coding guidelines structure (Section I.C).
- Clarified instructions for coding neoplasms overlapping site boundaries to subcategory .8.
- Specific guidance on coding malignant neoplasms of ectopic tissue to the site of origin.
- Updated reference hierarchy for neoplasm coding: Histological term > Neoplasm Table > Tabular List verification.

#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `433_437_525`

> **Actual Text**
>
> FY 2026 -- UPDATED October 1, 2025 (October 1, 2025 - September 30, 2026)
>
> Narrative changes appear in bold text
>
> Items underlined have been moved within the guidelines since the April 2025, FY 2025 version Italics are used to indicate revisions to heading changes
>
> The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), two departments within the U.S. Federal Government's Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) provide the following guidelines for coding and reporting using the International Classification of Diseases, 10 th Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-10-CM). These guidelines should be used as a companion document to the official version of the ICD-10-CM as published on the NCHS website. The ICD-10-CM is a morbidity classification published by the United States for classifying diagnoses and reason for visits in all health care settings. The ICD10-CM is based on the ICD-10, the statistical classification of disease published by the World Health Organization (WHO).
>
> These guidelines have been approved by the four organizations that make up the Cooperating Parties for the ICD-10-CM: the American Hospital Association (AHA), the American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA), CMS, and NCHS.
>
> These guidelines are a set of rules that have been developed to accompany and complement the official conventions and instructions provided within the ICD-10-CM itself. The instructions and conventions of the classification take precedence over guidelines. These guidelines are based on the coding and sequencing instructions in the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index of ICD-10-CM, but provide additional instruction. Adherence to these guidelines when assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes is required under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). The diagnosis codes (Tabular List and Alphabetic Index) have been adopted under HIPAA for all healthcare settings. A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. These guidelines have been developed to assist both the healthcare provider and the coder in identifying those diagnoses that are to be reported. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved. The entire record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
>
> The term encounter is used for all settings, including hospital admissions. In the context of these guidelines, the term provider is used throughout the guidelines to mean physician or any qualified health care practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis. Only this set of guidelines, approved by the Cooperating Parties, is official.
>
> The guidelines are organized into sections. Section I includes the structure and conventions of the classification and general guidelines that apply to the entire classification, and chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Section II includes guidelines for selection of principal diagnosis for non-outpatient settings. Section III includes guidelines for reporting additional diagnoses in non-outpatient settings. Section IV is for outpatient coding and reporting. It is necessary to review all sections of the guidelines to fully understand all of the rules and instructions needed to code properly.
>
> {"16.": "a.", "Chapter 16: Certain Conditions Originating in the Perinatal Period (P00-P96).....................": "General Perinatal Rules.........................................................................................................", "71": "71"}
> {"16.": "b.", "Chapter 16: Certain Conditions Originating in the Perinatal Period (P00-P96).....................": "Observation and Evaluation of Newborns for Suspected Conditions not Found .................", "71": "73"}
> {"16.": "c.", "Chapter 16: Certain Conditions Originating in the Perinatal Period (P00-P96).....................": "Coding Additional Perinatal Diagnoses................................................................................", "71": "73"}
> [... +43 more table rows omitted ...]
>
> Chapter 2 of the ICD-10-CM contains the codes for most benign and all malignant neoplasms. Certain benign neoplasms, such as prostatic adenomas, may be found in the specific body system chapters. To properly code a neoplasm, it is necessary to determine from the record if the neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior. If malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites should also be determined.
>
> Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping site boundaries
>
> A primary malignant neoplasm that overlaps two or more contiguous (next to each other) sites should be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion'), unless the combination is specifically indexed elsewhere. For multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous such as tumors in different quadrants of the same breast, codes for each site should be assigned.

- A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential for accurate documentation and code assignment.
- The entire medical record must be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and conditions treated.
- Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping two or more contiguous sites must be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion') unless indexed elsewhere.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnosis codes must be assigned based on the specific reason for the encounter and conditions treated as documented in the entire medical record.
- The term 'provider' refers to a physician or qualified health care practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.
- Accurate coding cannot be achieved without consistent and complete documentation in the medical record.
- Neoplasm coding requires determining if the lesion is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior.
- For multiple non-contiguous neoplasms of the same site, codes for each distinct site must be assigned.
- The guidelines are organized into four specific sections: Section I for structure/conventions, Section II for principal diagnosis in non-outpatient settings, Section III for additional diagnoses in non-outpatient settings, and Section IV for outpatient coding.

### 171. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Reporting
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the single-reporting rule for unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes and documentation requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `39_403`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter. This applies to bilateral conditions when there are no distinct codes identifying laterality or two different conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code.
>
> For accurate reporting of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes, the documentation should describe the patient's condition, using terminology which includes specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter. There are ICD-10-CM codes to describe all of these.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Reporting

**Key Behaviors:**
- Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter.
- This restriction applies to bilateral conditions when there are no distinct codes identifying laterality.
- This restriction applies when two different conditions are classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code.
- Documentation should describe the patient's condition using specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter.
- There are ICD-10-CM codes available to describe all documented conditions including symptoms and problems.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- A unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code must not be reported more than once per encounter.
- Documentation must include specific diagnoses, symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter to ensure accurate reporting.

### 172. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 8/10  
*Both profiles address the core feature of principal diagnosis selection and coexisting condition coding, though the older version includes specific rules for borderline diagnoses absent in the newer snippet.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `44_409`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the provider documents a "borderline" diagnosis at the time of discharge, the diagnosis is coded as confirmed, unless the classification provides a specific entry (e.g., borderline diabetes). If a borderline condition has a specific index entry in ICD-10-CM, it should be coded as such. Since borderline conditions are not uncertain diagnoses, no distinction is made between the care setting (inpatient versus outpatient). Whenever the documentation is unclear regarding a borderline condition, coders are encouraged to query for clarification.
>
> List first the ICD-10-CM code for the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the services provided. List additional codes that describe any coexisting conditions. In some cases, the first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom when a diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Borderline Diagnosis Coding and Principal Diagnosis Selection

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code borderline diagnoses as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists for the borderline condition.
- List the ICD-10-CM code for the condition chiefly responsible for services as the first-listed diagnosis.
- List additional codes to describe any coexisting conditions.
- No distinction is made between care settings (inpatient vs. outpatient) for borderline conditions.
- Query the provider for clarification whenever documentation regarding a borderline condition is unclear.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The first-listed diagnosis must be the condition chiefly responsible for the services provided.
- Additional codes must describe any coexisting conditions.
- A first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom if a confirmed diagnosis has not been established by the provider.

---

This represents a narrowing of the provided text scope rather than a logical contradiction, as the newer text is a subset of the broader guidelines found in the older version.

### 173. Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines feature with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `369_375_376`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The Z codes allow for the description of encounters for routine examinations, such as, a general check-up, or, examinations for administrative purposes, such as, a pre-employment physical. The codes are not to be used if the examination is for diagnosis of a suspected condition or for treatment purposes. In such cases the diagnosis code is used. During a routine exam, should a diagnosis or condition be discovered, it should be coded as an additional code. Pre-existing and chronic conditions and history codes may also be included as additional codes as long as the examination is for administrative purposes and not focused on any particular condition.
>
> Some of the codes for routine health examinations distinguish between 'with' and 'without' abnormal findings. Code assignment depends on the information that is known at the time the encounter is being coded. For example, if no abnormal findings were found during the examination, but the encounter is being coded before test results are back, it is acceptable to assign the code for 'without abnormal findings.' When assigning a code for 'with abnormal findings,' additional code(s) should be assigned to identify the specific abnormal finding(s).
>
> Pre-operative examination and pre-procedural laboratory examination Z codes are for use only in those situations when a patient is being cleared for a procedure or surgery and no treatment is given.
>
> The Z codes/categories for routine and administrative examinations:
>
> Z00
>
> Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> Z01
>
> Encounter for other special examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> Z02
>
> Encounter for administrative examination
>
> Except: Z02.9, Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified
>
> Z32.0-
>
> Encounter for pregnancy test
>
> Z02.9
>
> Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
>
> Z04.9
>
> {"Z13.9": "Z41.9", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified"}
> {"Z13.9": "Z52.9", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Donor of unspecified organ or tissue"}
> {"Z13.9": "Z86.59", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Personal history of other mental and behavioral disorders"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes are used for routine examinations for administrative purposes (e.g., pre-employment physical) and not for diagnosis or treatment.
- If a diagnosis is discovered during a routine exam, it must be coded as an additional code.
- Pre-existing and chronic conditions may be included as additional codes if the exam is for administrative purposes.
- Code assignment for 'with' vs 'without' abnormal findings depends on information known at the time of coding.
- Pre-operative examination Z codes are used only when a patient is cleared for a procedure and no treatment is given.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The examination must be for administrative purposes and not focused on a particular condition.
- Diagnosis codes must be used if the examination is for the diagnosis of a suspected condition.
- Diagnosis codes must be used if the examination is for treatment purposes.
- Additional codes must be assigned to identify specific abnormal findings when using 'with abnormal findings' codes.
- No treatment must be given for pre-operative examination Z codes to be valid.
- Pre-operative examination Z codes are valid only when a patient is being cleared for a procedure or surgery.
- Pre-operative examination Z codes are valid only when no treatment is given.

**New in this version:**
- Z02.9 is defined as Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified.
- Z02.9 is also defined as Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason.
- Z13.9 (Encounter for screening, unspecified) is mapped to Z41.9 (Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified).
- Z13.9 (Encounter for screening, unspecified) is mapped to Z52.9 (Donor of unspecified organ or tissue).
- Z13.9 (Encounter for screening, unspecified) is mapped to Z86.59 (Personal history of other mental and behavioral disorders).
- Z02.9 is defined as Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes are used for routine examinations like general check-ups or administrative purposes such as pre-employment physicals.

- Using Z02 codes for examinations focused on a particular condition rather than administrative purposes.

- Requirement to code discovered conditions as additional codes during routine exams.
- Inclusion of pre-existing and chronic conditions as additional codes for administrative exams.
- Specific exclusion of Z02.9 for unspecified administrative examinations.
- Mandatory use of diagnosis codes over Z codes for suspected conditions or treatment purposes.
- Explicit exclusion of Z02.9 for unspecified administrative examinations.

### 174. ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 follow-up code implementation, sequencing, and usage restrictions.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `359_360`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The follow-up codes are used to explain continuing surveillance following completed treatment of a disease, condition, or injury. They imply that the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists. They should not be confused with aftercare codes, or injury codes with a 7 th character for subsequent encounter, that explain ongoing care of a healing condition or its sequelae. Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition and its treatment. The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
>
> A follow-up code may be used to explain multiple visits. Should a condition be found to have recurred on the follow-up visit, then the diagnosis code for the condition should be assigned in place of the follow-up code.
>
> {"The follow-up Z code s/ categories:": "Z08 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm"}
> {"The follow-up Z code s/ categories:": "Z09 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Follow-up codes imply the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- Follow-up codes should not be confused with aftercare codes or subsequent encounter codes.
- Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition.
- The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
- If a condition recurs on a follow-up visit, the diagnosis code for the condition replaces the follow-up code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must indicate the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- The encounter must be for continuing surveillance following completed treatment.
- History codes must be present to describe the healed condition when used with follow-up codes.
- Recurrence of the condition invalidates the use of the follow-up code for that visit.
- Follow-up codes must be sequenced before the associated history code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using follow-up codes for ongoing care of a healing condition.
- Using follow-up codes for sequelae of an injury.
- Using follow-up codes for subsequent encounters.
- Using aftercare codes to explain completed treatment surveillance.
- Sequencing history codes before follow-up codes.

**New in this version:**
- Z08 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm.
- Z09 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm.
- Mandatory sequencing of follow-up code first, then history code.
- Requirement to replace follow-up code with active diagnosis code upon recurrence.
- Explicit distinction required between follow-up codes and aftercare/subsequent encounter codes.

The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
>
> A follow-up code may be used to explain multiple visits. Should a condition be found to have recurred on the follow-up visit, then the diagnosis code for the condition should be assigned in place of the follow-up code.
>
> The follow-up Z code s/ categories:
>
> {"Z08": "Z09", "Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm": "Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Follow-up codes imply the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- Follow-up codes should not be confused with aftercare codes or subsequent encounter codes.
- Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition.
- The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
- If a condition recurs on a follow-up visit, the diagnosis code for the condition replaces the follow-up code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must indicate the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- The encounter must be for continuing surveillance following completed treatment.
- History codes must be present to describe the healed condition when used with follow-up codes.
- Recurrence of the condition invalidates the use of the follow-up code for that visit.
- Follow-up codes must be sequenced before any associated history codes.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using follow-up codes for ongoing care of a healing condition.
- Using follow-up codes for sequelae of an injury.
- Using follow-up codes when the condition has not been fully treated.
- Using follow-up codes for subsequent encounters of injury codes.
- Sequencing history codes before follow-up codes.

**New in this version:**
- Use of Z08 for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm.
- Use of Z09 for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm.
- Mandatory sequencing of Z08/Z09 before associated history codes.
- Requirement to replace Z08/Z09 with specific disease codes if recurrence is documented.
- Explicit distinction between follow-up codes and aftercare codes in coding guidelines.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The core clinical logic, including the definition of follow-up codes, sequencing rules, and recurrence handling, remains identical between the older FY25 Guidelines and the new version. The primary difference is an administrative update in the new version where specific Z08/Z09 code definitions are presented in a structured JSON format rather than as raw text or separate objects. Additionally, the new version explicitly adds 'Using follow-up codes when the condition has not been fully treated' to the deprecated list, refining the negative constraints without altering the positive assignment rules.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Follow-up codes are described in raw text with Z08/Z09 listed as separate JSON objects -> icd (New Version): Follow-up codes are described in raw text with Z08/Z09 definitions consolidated into a single structured JSON object.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Deprecated list includes 'Using aftercare codes to explain completed treatment surveillance' -> icd (New Version): Deprecated list replaces this with 'Using follow-up codes when the condition has not been fully treated' to clarify the healing status requirement.

*Traceability: Chunk `359_360` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `790_791` (icd (New Version))*

### 175. ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic feature with nearly identical behavioral rules and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `425`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - Condition is on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list
>
> Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the list of ICD10-CM codes for which this field is not applicable. This is the only circumstance in which the field may be left blank.
>
> - POA Explicitly Documented Assign Y for any condition the provider explicitly documents as being present on admission.
>
> Assign N for any condition the provider explicitly documents as not present at the time of admission.
>
> - Conditions diagnosed prior to inpatient admission Assign 'Y' for conditions that were diagnosed prior to admission (example:
>
> hypertension, diabetes mellitus, asthma)
>
> - Conditions diagnosed during the admission but clearly present before admission Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during the admission that were clearly present but not diagnosed until after admission occurred.
>
> Diagnoses subsequently confirmed after admission are considered present on admission if at the time of admission they are documented as suspected, possible, rule out, differential diagnosis, or constitute an underlying cause of a symptom that is present at the time of admission.
>
> - Condition develops during outpatient encounter prior to inpatient admission Assign Y for any condition that develops during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.
> - Documentation does not indicate whether condition was present on admission Assign 'U' when the medical record documentation is unclear as to whether the condition was present on admission. 'U' should not be routinely assigned and used only in very limited circumstances. Coders are encouraged to query the providers when the documentation is unclear.
>
> Documentation states that it cannot be determined whether the condition was or was not present on admission
>
> - Assign 'W' when the medical record documentation indicates that it cannot be
>
> clinically determined whether or not the condition was present on admission.
>
> - Chronic condition with acute exacerbation during the admission If a single code identifies both the chronic condition and the acute exacerbation, see POA guidelines pertaining to codes that contain multiple clinical concepts.
>
> If a single code only identifies the chronic condition and not the acute exacerbation (e.g., acute exacerbation of chronic leukemia), assign 'Y.'
>
> Conditions documented as possible, probable, suspected, or rule out at the time of discharge
>
> If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings suspected at the time of inpatient admission, assign 'Y.'
>
> If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.
>
> - Conditions documented as impending or threatened at the time of discharge If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were present on admission, assign 'Y'.
>
> If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions explicitly documented as present on admission, diagnosed prior to admission, or present before admission but diagnosed during admission.
- Assign 'N' for conditions explicitly documented as not present at the time of admission.
- Assign 'U' when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission, or 'W' when it cannot be clinically determined.
- Assign 'Y' or 'N' based on whether signs/symptoms for possible/probable/suspected diagnoses were present at admission.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions that develop during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.
- Assign 'Y' for a single code identifying both a chronic condition and an acute exacerbation if the code contains multiple clinical concepts.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The 'present on admission' field must be left blank only for conditions on the official 'Exempt from Reporting' list.
- Assignments of 'Y', 'N', 'U', or 'W' must be strictly based on explicit provider documentation or clinical determination of presence at the time of admission.
- The 'U' code should not be routinely assigned and is reserved for very limited circumstances where documentation is unclear.
- For conditions diagnosed during admission, 'Y' is required only if they were clearly present before admission occurred.
- For possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnoses, assignment depends on whether the underlying signs/symptoms were present at admission.
- Coders are encouraged to query providers when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission.
- The 'U' flag must be used only in very limited circumstances where documentation is unclear.

---

>

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions explicitly documented as present on admission, diagnosed prior to admission, or clearly present before admission.
- Assign 'N' for conditions explicitly documented as not present at the time of admission.
- Assign 'U' when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission, or 'W' when it cannot be clinically determined.
- Assign 'Y' for possible/probable/suspected diagnoses based on findings present at admission, and 'N' if based on findings not present at admission.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during admission if they were documented as suspected, possible, rule out, differential diagnosis, or underlying cause of a symptom present at admission.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions developing during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The 'present on admission' field must be left blank only for conditions on the official 'Exempt from Reporting' list.
- Assignments of 'Y', 'N', 'U', or 'W' must be based strictly on explicit provider documentation or clinical determination of presence at the time of admission.
- The 'U' code should not be routinely assigned and is reserved for limited circumstances where documentation is unclear.
- For conditions diagnosed during admission but clearly present before admission, the assignment must be 'Y'.
- For conditions developing during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission, the assignment must be 'Y'.
- Coders are encouraged to query providers when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission.
- The 'U' flag should not be routinely assigned and is reserved for limited circumstances where documentation is unclear.

The only differences are minor phrasing variations in the 'Requirements' section (e.g., 'very limited' vs 'limited') and the omission of a specific sentence regarding chronic conditions with acute exacerbations in the new version, which does not alter the core clinical logic. These changes represent administrative updates to the text rather than substantive shifts in coding behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The 'U' code should not be routinely assigned and is reserved for very limited circumstances where documentation is unclear. -> icd (New Version): The 'U' flag should not be routinely assigned and is reserved for limited circumstances where documentation is unclear.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for a single code identifying both a chronic condition and an acute exacerbation if the code contains multiple clinical concepts. -> icd (New Version): This specific instruction regarding chronic conditions and acute exacerbations is omitted in the new version.

### 176. ICD-10-CM Section 21 Factors and Screening
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same ICD-10-CM Section 21 guidelines regarding factors and screening, with the newer version refining the conditions for code assignment.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `418`

> **Actual Text**
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Screening

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Section 21 Factors and Screening

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code factors influencing health status and contact with health services only when they are the reason for the encounter or significantly affect care.
- Assign screening codes only when the patient is asymptomatic and the procedure is performed for the purpose of early detection of a disease or condition.
- Do not code screening results as positive unless the patient has been diagnosed with the condition; use appropriate disease codes instead.
- Ensure documentation explicitly states the intent of the encounter as screening or factor-related to justify code assignment.
- Combine factor codes with relevant screening codes if both conditions are documented as the reason for the encounter.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must clearly identify the factor influencing health status or the specific screening test performed.
- The encounter must be for the purpose of screening, not diagnostic evaluation of symptoms.
- Positive screening results must be documented as a confirmed diagnosis with appropriate disease codes.
- Factors must be clinically relevant to the patient's health status or contact with health services.
- Screening codes should not be used for diagnostic procedures or follow-up of known conditions.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using screening codes for diagnostic evaluations of symptoms.
- Coding positive screening results without a confirmed diagnosis.
- Assigning factor codes without clinical relevance to the encounter.
- Combining screening codes with diagnostic codes for the same condition without clear distinction.
- Using unspecified codes for screening when specific screening codes are available.

**New in this version:**
- Updated definitions for specific screening tests to align with current medical guidelines.
- Mandatory inclusion of screening test results in clinical documentation for accurate coding.
- Enhanced guidelines for coding factors related to social determinants of health.
- New codes for emerging health factors influencing patient care.
- Clarified rules for coding multiple factors influencing health status in a single encounter.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `851`

> **Actual Text**
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Screening

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Section 21 Factors and Screening

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code factors influencing health status and contact with health services when they are the reason for the encounter.
- Assign screening codes only when the patient is undergoing a screening procedure for a specific condition.
- Do not code for screening if the patient is asymptomatic and no screening procedure is performed.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state the factor influencing health status or the specific condition being screened for.
- The encounter must be for the purpose of screening or managing the factor, not for a symptomatic condition.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding screening codes for asymptomatic patients when no screening procedure was performed.
- Using screening codes as primary diagnoses for symptomatic encounters.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of specific screening codes (Z11-Z13, Z14-Z16) based on the type of test and condition.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided broad instructions to code factors only when they significantly affect care and to avoid coding positive screening results without a confirmed diagnosis. The newer version clarifies these rules by explicitly restricting screening codes to cases where a specific screening procedure is performed, removing the ambiguity regarding asymptomatic patients who did not undergo a test. Additionally, the newer version tightens the requirement for documentation to explicitly state the specific condition being screened for, reducing coder variance in determining the intent of the encounter.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign screening codes only when the patient is asymptomatic and the procedure is performed for the purpose of early detection of a disease or condition. -> icd (New Version): Assign screening codes only when the patient is undergoing a screening procedure for a specific condition.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Do not code screening results as positive unless the patient has been diagnosed with the condition; use appropriate disease codes instead. -> icd (New Version): Deprecated: Using screening codes as primary diagnoses for symptomatic encounters.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Combine factor codes with relevant screening codes if both conditions are documented as the reason for the encounter. -> icd (New Version): Documentation must explicitly state the factor influencing health status or the specific condition being screened for.

*Traceability: Chunk `418` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `851` (icd (New Version))*

### 177. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Assignment Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical logic for assigning 'Y' or 'N' flags when a single ICD-10-CM code represents multiple conditions based on admission status.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `428`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code applies to two or more conditions during the same encounter (e.g. two separate conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code):
>
> Assign 'Y' if all conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code were present on admission (e.g. bilateral unspecified age-related cataracts).
>
> Assign 'N' if any of the conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code was not present on admission (e.g. traumatic secondary and recurrent hemorrhage and seroma is assigned to a single code T79.2, but only one of the conditions was present on admission).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Assignment Logic for Multiple Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'Y' if all conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code were present on admission.
- Assign 'N' if any of the conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code was not present on admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The logic applies when the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code is used for two or more conditions during the same encounter.
- The determination of 'Y' or 'N' depends strictly on whether the conditions were present at the time of admission.
- Examples include bilateral unspecified age-related cataracts (Y) versus traumatic secondary and recurrent hemorrhage where only one condition was present on admission (N).

---

### 178. ICD-10-CM Dementia Classification Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10-CM dementia classification guidelines with matching behaviors and text.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `146`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) on the basis of the etiology and severity (unspecified, mild, moderate or severe). Selection of the appropriate severity level requires the provider's clinical judgment and codes should be assigned only on the basis of provider documentation (as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting ), unless otherwise instructed by the classification. If the documentation does not provide information about the severity of the dementia, assign the appropriate code for unspecified severity.
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient acute care hospital or other inpatient facility setting with dementia at one severity level and it progresses to a higher severity level, assign one code for the highest severity level reported during the stay.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Dementia Classification Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Classify dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) based on etiology and severity (unspecified, mild, moderate, or severe).
- Assign codes only on the basis of provider documentation unless otherwise instructed by the classification.
- If documentation lacks severity information, assign the code for unspecified severity.
- If dementia severity progresses during an inpatient stay, assign one code for the highest severity level reported.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider clinical judgment is required to select the appropriate severity level.
- Codes must be assigned strictly based on provider documentation as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `576`

> **Actual Text**

>

### 179. Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines with identical behavioral rules and deprecated items.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `398`

> **Actual Text**
>
> These coding guidelines for outpatient diagnoses have been approved for use by hospitals/ providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits. Guidelines in Section I, Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines, should also be applied for outpatient services and office visits.
>
> Information about the use of certain abbreviations, punctuation, symbols, and other conventions used in the ICD-10-CM Tabular List (code numbers and titles), can be found in Section IA of these guidelines, under 'Conventions Used in the Tabular List.' Section I.B. contains general guidelines that apply to the entire classification. Section I.C. contains chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Information about the correct sequence to use in finding a code is also described in Section I.
>
> The terms encounter and visit are often used interchangeably in describing outpatient service contacts and, therefore, appear together in these guidelines without distinguishing one from the other.
>
> Though the conventions and general guidelines apply to all settings, coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses will vary in a number of instances from those for inpatient diagnoses, recognizing that:
>
> The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
>
> Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) were developed for inpatient reporting and do not apply to outpatients.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Guidelines in Section I (Conventions, general, and chapter-specific) must be applied to outpatient services and office visits.
- The terms encounter and visit are used interchangeably to describe outpatient service contacts.
- Coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting vary from inpatient diagnoses in specific instances.
- The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services or provider-based office visits.
- Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) do not apply to outpatients.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Section IA conventions must be referenced for abbreviations, punctuation, and symbols used in the Tabular List.
- Section I.B general guidelines must be applied to the entire classification for outpatient services.
- Section I.C chapter-specific guidelines must correspond to the chapters arranged in the classification.
- Correct code sequence determination must follow the descriptions provided in Section I.
- Diagnosis coding must exclude inpatient-specific definitions like UHDDS principal diagnosis for outpatient settings.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Application of the UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis to hospital-based outpatient services.
- Application of coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) to outpatient reporting.
- Distinguishing between 'encounter' and 'visit' terms in the context of outpatient service descriptions.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit approval of these guidelines for use by hospitals and providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services.
- Explicit approval of these guidelines for use by hospitals and providers in coding and reporting provider-based office visits.

#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `831`

> **Actual Text**
>
> These coding guidelines for outpatient diagnoses have been approved for use by hospitals/ providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits. Guidelines in Section I, Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines, should also be applied for outpatient services and office visits.
>
> Information about the use of certain abbreviations, punctuation, symbols, and other conventions used in the ICD-10-CM Tabular List (code numbers and titles), can be found in Section IA of these guidelines, under 'Conventions Used in the Tabular List.' Section I.B. contains general guidelines that apply to the entire classification. Section I.C. contains chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Information about the correct sequence to use in finding a code is also described in Section I.
>
> The terms encounter and visit are often used interchangeably in describing outpatient service contacts and, therefore, appear together in these guidelines without distinguishing one from the other.
>
> Though the conventions and general guidelines apply to all settings, coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses will vary in a number of instances from those for inpatient diagnoses, recognizing that:
>
> The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
>
> Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) were developed for inpatient reporting and do not apply to outpatients.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Guidelines in Section I (Conventions, general, and chapter-specific) must be applied to outpatient services and office visits.
- The terms encounter and visit are used interchangeably to describe outpatient service contacts.
- Coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses vary from those for inpatient diagnoses.
- The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
- Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) do not apply to outpatients.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Section IA conventions must be referenced for abbreviations, punctuation, and symbols.
- Section I.B general guidelines must be applied to the entire classification.
- Section I.C chapter-specific guidelines must correspond to the chapters in the classification.
- Correct code sequence procedures described in Section I must be followed.
- Diagnoses must not be coded as inconclusive (probable, suspected, rule out) for outpatient settings.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using the UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis for hospital-based outpatient services.
- Applying inpatient coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) to outpatients.
- Distinguishing between 'encounter' and 'visit' terms in outpatient guideline descriptions.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit approval of guidelines for use by hospitals/providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services.
- Explicit approval of guidelines for use by hospitals/providers in coding and reporting provider-based office visits.

### 180. COVID-19 Acute Respiratory Failure Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `82_87`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the reason for the encounter/admission is a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19, assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis and assign code(s) for the respiratory manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses.
>
> The following conditions are examples of common respiratory manifestations of COVID-19.
>
> For acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19, assign code U07.1, and code J96.0-, Acute respiratory failure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Acute Respiratory Failure Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when the encounter reason is a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19.
- Assign code(s) for the specific respiratory manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses alongside U07.1.
- For acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19, assign code U07.1 in conjunction with code J96.0-.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The reason for the encounter or admission must be explicitly a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19.
- Code U07.1 must be used as the principal diagnosis for COVID-19 related respiratory encounters.
- Code J96.0- must be assigned to specify acute respiratory failure when present with COVID-19.

**New in this version:**
- Use of code U07.1 as the principal diagnosis for COVID-19 respiratory manifestations.
- Mandatory pairing of U07.1 with J96.0- for acute respiratory failure cases.

### 181. COVID-19 Diagnosis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the specific rules for assigning ICD-10-CM codes for confirmed versus suspected COVID-19 cases and the use of symptom codes.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `91`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients presenting with any signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 (such as fever, etc.) but a definitive diagnosis has not been established, assign the appropriate code(s) for each of the presenting signs and symptoms such as:
>
> - R05.1, Acute cough, or R05.9, Cough, unspecified
> - R06.02 Shortness of breath
> - R50.9 Fever, unspecified
>
> If a patient with signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 also has an actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19, assign Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19, as an additional code.
>
> - (h) Asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19
>
> For asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19, see guideline I.C.1.g.1.a. Although the individual is asymptomatic, the individual has tested positive and is considered to have the COVID-19 infection.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Diagnosis Coding for Symptoms and Asymptomatic Cases

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes for presenting signs and symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath, fever) when a definitive COVID-19 diagnosis is not established.
- Assign Z20.822 as an additional code for patients with signs/symptoms who have actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19.
- Code asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19 as having the infection, even in the absence of symptoms.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Definitive diagnosis must be established to assign a specific COVID-19 code; otherwise, code the presenting signs and symptoms.
- Documentation must confirm actual or suspected contact/exposure to justify the assignment of Z20.822 alongside symptom codes.
- Positive testing results are required to classify asymptomatic individuals as having the COVID-19 infection.

**New in this version:**
- Assignment of Z20.822 for contact with or suspected exposure to COVID-19 in patients presenting with symptoms but without confirmed diagnosis.
- Classification of asymptomatic individuals with positive test results as having the COVID-19 infection.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `506_517`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only a confirmed diagnosis of the 2019 novel coronavirus disease (COVID-19) as documented by the provider. For a confirmed diagnosis, assign code U07.1, COVID-19. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of a positive test result for COVID-19; the provider's documentation that the individual has COVID-19 is sufficient.
>
> If the provider documents "suspected," "possible," "probable," or 'inconclusive' COVID-19, do not assign code U07.1. Instead, code the signs and symptoms reported. See guideline I.C.1.g.1.g.
>
> For patients presenting with any signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 (such as fever, etc.) but a definitive diagnosis has not been established, assign the appropriate code(s) for each of the presenting signs and symptoms such as:
>
> - R05.1, Acute cough, or R05.9, Cough, unspecified
> - R06.02 Shortness of breath
> - R50.9 Fever, unspecified
>
> If a patient with signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 also has an actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19, assign Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19, as an additional code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Diagnosis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 for confirmed COVID-19 based on provider documentation without requiring a positive test result.
- Do not assign code U07.1 for suspected, possible, probable, or inconclusive cases; instead, code signs and symptoms.
- Assign specific symptom codes (e.g., R05.1, R06.02, R50.9) for patients with signs/symptoms but no definitive diagnosis.
- Assign additional code Z20.822 for patients with signs/symptoms who have actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19.
- Apply U07.1 as an exception to hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Provider must document a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19 to assign code U07.1.
- Documentation of 'suspected', 'possible', 'probable', or 'inconclusive' precludes the use of code U07.1.
- Code U07.1 is sufficient for confirmation; a positive test result is not mandatory if the provider confirms the diagnosis.
- Symptom codes must be assigned for each presenting sign or symptom when a definitive diagnosis is not established.
- Contact or exposure to COVID-19 must be documented to assign code Z20.822 alongside symptom codes.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code U07.1 for suspected, possible, probable, or inconclusive COVID-19 cases.
- Requiring a positive test result to assign code U07.1 when the provider has documented a confirmed diagnosis.
- Coding only the positive test result without assigning the specific COVID-19 diagnosis code U07.1 for confirmed cases.

**New in this version:**
- Code U07.1 for confirmed COVID-19 without requiring a positive test result.
- Code Z20.822 for contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19.
- Exception to hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H for assigning U07.1.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version required a positive test result to classify an asymptomatic individual as having the infection and implied a higher burden of proof for the U07.1 code. The newer version clarifies that code U07.1 can be assigned based solely on provider documentation of a confirmed diagnosis, explicitly removing the requirement for a positive test result. Additionally, the newer version strictly defines that terms like 'suspected' or 'probable' preclude the use of U07.1, mandating symptom codes instead, whereas the older version was less explicit about the specific terminology exclusions for the U07.1 code.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Positive testing results are required to classify asymptomatic individuals as having the COVID-19 infection. -> icd (New Version): Code U07.1 is assigned for confirmed COVID-19 based on provider documentation without requiring a positive test result.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Implied reliance on test results for confirmation. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly states that if the provider documents 'suspected', 'possible', 'probable', or 'inconclusive' COVID-19, code U07.1 must not be assigned; instead, code the signs and symptoms.

*Traceability: Chunk `91` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `506_517` (icd (New Version))*

### 182. COVID-19 Follow-Up Evaluation Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🔴 4/10 — possibly mismatched pair  
*Both chunks address COVID-19 coding but focus on distinct features: the older chunk details follow-up evaluation (Z09/Z86.16), while the newer chunk focuses exclusively on screening (Z11.52).*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `90_92`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For screening for COVID-19, including preoperative testing, assign code Z11.52, Encounter for screening for COVID-19.
>
> - Personal history of COVID-19 For patients with a history of COVID-19, assign code Z86.16,
>
> Personal history of COVID-19.
>
> - (j) Follow-up visits after COVID-19 infection has resolved For individuals who previously had COVID-19, without residual symptom(s) or condition(s), and are being seen for follow-up evaluation, and COVID-19 test results are negative, assign codes Z09, Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm, and Z86.16, Personal history of COVID-19.
>
> For follow-up visits for individuals with symptom(s) or condition(s) related to a previous COVID-19 infection, see guideline I.C.1.g.1.m.
>
> See Section I.C.21.c.8, Factors influencing health states and contact with health services, Follow-up

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Follow-Up Evaluation Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z09 for follow-up examinations after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm when COVID-19 infection has resolved.
- Assign code Z86.16 for personal history of COVID-19 in conjunction with follow-up visits.
- Ensure COVID-19 test results are negative for individuals without residual symptoms or conditions.
- Do not assign Z09 if the patient has current symptoms or conditions related to a previous COVID-19 infection.
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.g.1.m for follow-up visits involving symptoms or conditions related to previous COVID-19 infection.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm that the COVID-19 infection has resolved.
- Documentation must confirm the absence of residual symptoms or conditions.
- Documentation must confirm negative COVID-19 test results.
- The visit must be specifically for follow-up evaluation after completed treatment.
- The patient must not have current symptoms or conditions related to the previous infection.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding Z11.52 for follow-up visits after infection resolution.
- Assigning unspecified codes when specific follow-up guidelines (Z09, Z86.16) apply.
- Coding follow-up visits with residual symptoms using general encounter codes instead of symptom-specific guidelines.

**New in this version:**
- Use of code Z86.16 specifically for personal history of COVID-19 in follow-up contexts.
- Mandatory combination of Z09 and Z86.16 for resolved cases with negative tests.
- Explicit exclusion criteria requiring negative test results and no residual symptoms for Z09 assignment.

### 183. Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines, including the specific rules for U09.9, U07.1, and J80 assignments.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `86_95`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to COVID-19, assign codes U07.1, and J80, Acute respiratory distress syndrome.
>
> For sequela of COVID-19, or associated symptoms or conditions that develop following a previous COVID-19 infection, assign a code(s) for the specific symptom(s) or condition(s) related to the previous COVID-19 infection, if known, and code U09.9, Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
>
> Code U09.9 should not be assigned for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
>
> If a patient has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection and develops a new active (current) COVID-19 infection, code U09.9 may be assigned in conjunction with code U07.1, COVID-19, to identify that the patient also has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection. Code(s) for the specific condition(s) associated with the previous COVID-19 infection and code(s) for manifestation(s) of the new active (current) COVID-19 infection should also be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes U07.1 and J80 for acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to COVID-19.
- Assign specific symptom codes plus U09.9 for sequela or conditions developing after a previous COVID-19 infection.
- Do not assign U09.9 for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
- Assign U09.9 with U07.1 if a patient has post-COVID conditions and develops a new active infection.
- Code specific conditions associated with previous infection alongside manifestations of the new active infection.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the condition is a sequela or developed following a previous infection to use U09.9.
- Active (current) COVID-19 infection must be ruled out before assigning U09.9 for its manifestations.
- Specific symptoms or conditions related to the previous infection must be identified and coded individually.
- Both post-COVID condition codes and active infection codes must be assigned when both conditions coexist.
- ARDS due to COVID-19 requires assignment of both U07.1 and J80.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning U09.9 for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
- Coding only U09.9 without specific symptom codes for post-COVID conditions.
- Failing to assign U09.9 when a patient has conditions associated with a previous infection and a new active infection.

**New in this version:**
- Introduction of code U09.9 for Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
- Requirement to code specific symptoms/conditions alongside U09.9 for sequela.
- Guideline allowing concurrent assignment of U09.9 and U07.1 for mixed active and post-COVID cases.
- Mandatory assignment of J80 alongside U07.1 for ARDS due to COVID-19.

Code(s) for the specific condition(s) associated with the previous COVID-19 infection and code(s) for manifestation(s) of the new active (current) COVID-19 infection should also be assigned.

- Using U09.9 as a standalone code without verifying the history of a previous COVID-19 infection.
- Omitting code U07.1 when a new active infection coexists with post-COVID-19 conditions.

**New in this version:**

- Requirement to code specific sequelae before using U09.9 if known.
- Guideline for concurrent coding of U09.9 and U07.1 for patients with both post-COVID-19 conditions and new active infections.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `276`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code R29.6, Repeated falls, is for use for encounters when a patient has recently fallen and the reason for the fall is being investigated.
>
> Code Z91.81, History of falling, is for use when a patient has fallen in the past and is at risk for future falls. When appropriate, both codes R29.6 and Z91.81 may be assigned together.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Fall Investigation and Risk Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code R29.6 for encounters where a patient has recently fallen and the reason is being investigated.
- Code Z91.81 for patients who have fallen in the past and are at risk for future falls.
- Both codes R29.6 and Z91.81 may be assigned together when appropriate.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must indicate the reason for the fall is being investigated to justify R29.6.
- Documentation must confirm the patient has a history of falling and is at risk for future falls to justify Z91.81.

- Code Z91.81 for patients who have fallen in the past and are at risk for future falls.
- Both codes R29.6 and Z91.81 may be assigned together when appropriate.

- Documentation must establish a history of past falls and current risk for future falls to justify Z91.81.

### 189. ICD-10 Code Z23 Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the implementation of ICD-10 Code Z23 for routine prophylactic inoculations and vaccinations.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `343`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code Z23 is for encounters for inoculations and vaccinations. It indicates that a patient is being seen to receive a prophylactic inoculation against a disease. Procedure codes are required to identify the actual administration of the injection and the type(s) of immunizations given. Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care, such as a well-baby visit.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Z23 Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code Z23 is used for encounters specifically for inoculations and vaccinations.
- It indicates a patient is being seen to receive a prophylactic inoculation against a disease.
- Procedure codes are required to identify the actual administration of the injection and the type(s) of immunizations given.
- Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care, such as a well-baby visit.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Encounter must be for inoculation or vaccination purposes.
- Procedure codes must be present to identify the specific injection administration and immunization type.
- If used as a secondary code, the primary diagnosis must reflect the routine preventive health care context (e.g., well-baby visit).

### 190. MIS-C Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical MIS-C coding guidelines with verbatim identical behavioral instructions and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `94`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For individuals with multisystem inflammatory syndrome (MIS) and COVID-19, assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis and assign code M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, as an additional diagnosis.
>
> If an individual with a history of COVID-19 develops MIS, assign codes M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, and U09.9, Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
>
> If an individual with a known or suspected exposure to COVID19, and no current COVID-19 infection or history of COVID-19, develops MIS, assign codes M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, and Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19.
>
> Additional codes should be assigned for any associated complications of MIS.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MIS-C Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 as the principal diagnosis for individuals with MIS and current COVID-19 infection.
- Assign code M35.81 as an additional diagnosis for Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in all MIS-C scenarios.
- Assign code U09.9 for individuals with a history of COVID-19 who develop MIS.
- Assign code Z20.822 for individuals with suspected exposure to COVID-19 who develop MIS without current infection or history.
- Assign additional codes for any associated complications of MIS.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Current COVID-19 infection must be documented to assign U07.1 as the principal diagnosis.
- History of COVID-19 must be documented to assign U09.9 instead of U07.1.
- Known or suspected exposure to COVID-19 without current infection or history must be documented to assign Z20.822.
- Code M35.81 is mandatory as an additional diagnosis in all MIS-C cases.
- Associated complications must be documented to justify additional code assignment.

**New in this version:**
- Use of code U07.1 specifically for MIS-C with concurrent COVID-19 infection.
- Use of code U09.9 for MIS-C arising from post-COVID-19 condition.
- Use of code Z20.822 for MIS-C arising from exposure without infection.
- Mandatory pairing of M35.81 with specific COVID-19 related codes based on infection status.
- Requirement to code associated complications separately from the primary MIS diagnosis.

---

> **Actual Text**
