- Patient must not have an established diagnosis of hypertension to use code R03.0.
- Clinical documentation must distinguish between transient hypertension of pregnancy and established hypertension.
- Documentation must specify the presence or absence of significant proteinuria to differentiate between O13.- and O14.-.

### 249. Pregnancy and Puerperium Z-Code Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Obstetric and Perinatal Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Pregnancy and Puerperium Z-Code guidelines with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `366_367`

> **Actual Text**
>
> See Section I.C.15. Pregnancy, Childbirth, and the Puerperium, for further instruction on the use of these codes.
>
> Z codes for pregnancy are for use in those circumstances when none of the problems or complications included in the codes from the Obstetrics chapter exist (a routine prenatal visit or postpartum care). Codes in category Z34, Encounter for supervision of normal pregnancy, are always first listed and are not to be used with any other code from the OB chapter.
>
> Codes in category Z3A, Weeks of gestation, may be assigned to provide additional information about the pregnancy. Category Z3A codes should not be assigned for pregnancies with abortive outcomes (categories O00O08), elective termination of pregnancy (code Z33.2), nor for postpartum conditions, as category Z3A is not applicable to these conditions. The date of the admission should be used to determine weeks of gestation for inpatient admissions that encompass more than one gestational week.
>
> The outcome of delivery, category Z37, should be included on all maternal delivery records. It is always a secondary code.
>
> Codes in category Z37 should not be used on the newborn record.
>
> Z codes for family planning (contraceptive) or procreative management and counseling should be included on an obstetric record either during the pregnancy or the postpartum stage, if applicable.
>
> {"Z codes/categories for obstetrical and reproductive services:": "Z30 Encounter for contraceptive management"}
> {"Z codes/categories for obstetrical and reproductive services:": "Z31 Encounter for procreative management"}
> {"Z codes/categories for obstetrical and reproductive services:": "Z32.2 Encounter for childbirth instruction"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Pregnancy and Puerperium Z-Code Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in category Z34 are always first listed and must not be used with any other code from the OB chapter for routine prenatal or postpartum care.
- Category Z3A codes for weeks of gestation are assigned to provide additional pregnancy information but are excluded for abortive outcomes, elective terminations, and postpartum conditions.
- The outcome of delivery (category Z37) must be included on all maternal delivery records as a secondary code.
- Family planning or procreative management Z codes should be included on obstetric records during pregnancy or the postpartum stage if applicable.
- Category Z3A codes are not applicable to postpartum conditions.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Z34 codes must be the first listed code when no obstetrical problems or complications exist.
- Z37 outcome codes must be present on all maternal delivery records.
- Z37 codes must not be assigned to newborn records.
- Z3A codes must be based on the date of admission for inpatient admissions spanning multiple gestational weeks.
- Z3A codes must not be assigned for pregnancies with abortive outcomes or elective terminations.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using Z34 codes in conjunction with other codes from the OB chapter for routine care.
- Assigning Z3A codes for postpartum conditions.
- Assigning Z3A codes for pregnancies with abortive outcomes.
- Assigning Z3A codes for elective termination of pregnancy.
- Using Z37 outcome codes on newborn records.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to use the date of admission to determine weeks of gestation for multi-week inpatient admissions.
- Mandatory inclusion of family planning or procreative management Z codes on obstetric records when applicable.
- Clarification that Z34 codes are exclusively for circumstances where no OB chapter problems exist.

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `244`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Pregnancy associated cardiomyopathy, code O90.3, is unique in that it may be diagnosed in the third trimester of pregnancy but may continue to progress months after delivery. For this reason, it is referred to as peripartum cardiomyopathy. Code O90.3 is only for use when the cardiomyopathy develops as a result of pregnancy in a patient who did not have pre-existing heart disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Peripartum Cardiomyopathy Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code O90.3 is used for cardiomyopathy diagnosed in the third trimester or progressing months after delivery.
- The condition must develop as a result of pregnancy.
- The patient must not have pre-existing heart disease.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnosis must occur in the third trimester of pregnancy or continue to progress months after delivery.
- Cardiomyopathy must be a result of pregnancy.
- Patient must have no pre-existing heart disease.

### 254. COVID-19 Sequencing Guidelines for Pregnancy
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Obstetric and Perinatal Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the sequencing of O98.5- and U07.1 codes for pregnant patients with COVID-19.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `253`

> **Actual Text**
>
> During pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium, when COVID-19 is the reason for admission/encounter, code O98.5-, Other viral diseases complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, should be sequenced as the principal/first-listed diagnosis, and code U07.1, COVID-19, and the appropriate codes for associated manifestation(s) should be assigned as additional diagnoses. Codes from Chapter 15 always take sequencing priority.
>
> If the reason for admission/encounter is unrelated to COVID-19 but the patient tests positive for COVID-19 during the admission/encounter, the appropriate code for the reason for admission/encounter should be sequenced as the principal/first-listed diagnosis, and codes O98.5- and U07.1, as well as the appropriate codes for associated COVID-19 manifestations, should be assigned as additional diagnoses.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Sequencing Guidelines for Pregnancy

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code O98.5- should be sequenced as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when COVID-19 is the reason for admission/encounter during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.
- Code U07.1 and codes for associated manifestations should be assigned as additional diagnoses alongside O98.5-.
- Codes from Chapter 15 always take sequencing priority over other codes.
- If the reason for admission is unrelated to COVID-19 but the patient tests positive, the reason for admission code is sequenced first.
- Codes O98.5- and U07.1 are assigned as additional diagnoses when COVID-19 is incidental to the primary reason for admission.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient is in the period of pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.

- Documentation must specify associated manifestations of COVID-19 to assign appropriate additional codes.
- Chapter 15 codes must be prioritized if applicable to the clinical scenario.

### 256. Antepartum Complication Trimester Coding Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Obstetric and Perinatal Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature with identical behaviors, requirements, and raw text excerpts.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `217`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In instances when a patient is admitted to a hospital for complications of pregnancy during one trimester and remains in the hospital into a subsequent trimester, the trimester character for the antepartum complication code should be assigned on the basis of the trimester when the complication developed, not the trimester of the discharge. If the condition developed prior to the current admission/encounter or represents a pre-existing condition, the trimester character for the trimester at the time of the admission/encounter should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Antepartum Complication Trimester Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the trimester character based on when the complication developed, not the trimester of discharge.
- If the condition developed prior to the current admission, assign the trimester character for the time of admission/encounter.
- Do not use the discharge trimester if the patient remains hospitalized into a subsequent trimester after the complication onset.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must clearly identify the specific trimester when the complication first developed.
- Documentation must distinguish between new complications and pre-existing conditions to determine the correct assignment point.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning the trimester character based on the date of discharge when the complication onset occurred in a prior trimester.
- Using the discharge trimester as the primary determinant for antepartum complication codes in multi-trimester admissions.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of the trimester character corresponding to the onset of the complication regardless of length of stay.

---

### 257. Principal Diagnosis Assignment for Malignancy
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding principal diagnosis assignment for malignancy, including the Z51 exception and sequencing rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `98_109_114`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the malignancy is chiefly responsible for occasioning the patient admission/encounter and treatment is directed at the primary site, designate the primary malignancy as the principal/first-listed diagnosis.
>
> The only exception to this guideline is if the administration of chemotherapy, immunotherapy or external beam radiation therapy is chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission/encounter. In that case, assign the appropriate Z51.-code as the first-listed or principal diagnosis, and the underlying diagnosis or problem for which the service is being performed as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> Symptoms, signs, and ill-defined conditions listed in Chapter 18 characteristic of, or associated with, an existing primary or secondary site malignancy cannot be used to replace the malignancy as principal or first-listed diagnosis, regardless of the number of admissions or encounters for treatment and care of the neoplasm.
>
> See section I.C.21 . Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Encounter for prophylactic organ removal.
>
> If the reason for the encounter is for treatment of a primary malignancy, assign the malignancy as the principal/first-listed diagnosis. The primary site is to be sequenced first, followed by any metastatic sites.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Principal Diagnosis Assignment for Malignancy

**Key Behaviors:**
- Designate the primary malignancy as the principal diagnosis if it is chiefly responsible for the admission and treatment is directed at the primary site.
- Assign the appropriate Z51.-code as the first-listed diagnosis if chemotherapy, immunotherapy, or external beam radiation therapy is chiefly responsible for the admission.
- Sequence the primary site first, followed by any metastatic sites when treating a primary malignancy.
- Do not use symptoms, signs, or ill-defined conditions associated with an existing malignancy to replace the malignancy as the principal diagnosis.
- Query the provider for clarification if the reason for the encounter is unclear regarding the primary site or treatment focus.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The malignancy must be chiefly responsible for occasioning the patient admission or encounter.
- Treatment must be directed at the primary site for the malignancy to be the principal diagnosis.
- Clinical documentation must clearly identify the primary site and the nature of the treatment provided.
- If prophylactic organ removal is the reason for the encounter, specific section I.C.21 guidelines must be followed.
- Symptoms or signs cannot be used as the principal diagnosis if an existing primary or secondary site malignancy is documented.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using symptoms, signs, or ill-defined conditions to replace the malignancy as the principal diagnosis.
- Assigning the malignancy as the principal diagnosis when chemotherapy or radiation is the chief reason for admission without sequencing Z51.-first.
- Sequencing metastatic sites before the primary site in treatment of a primary malignancy.
- Ignoring the exception for Z51.-codes when administration of systemic therapy is the primary reason for the encounter.
- Failing to sequence the primary site first when multiple sites are involved in the treatment of a primary malignancy.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit requirement to assign Z51.-codes as the first-listed diagnosis when chemotherapy, immunotherapy, or external beam radiation therapy is chiefly responsible for the admission.
- Mandatory sequencing of the primary site first, followed by any metastatic sites, when treating a primary malignancy.
- Prohibition against using symptoms, signs, or ill-defined conditions to replace the malignancy as the principal diagnosis regardless of the number of admissions.
- Reference to specific section I.C.21 for factors influencing health status and contact with health services, including encounter for prophylactic organ removal.
- Requirement to query the provider for clarification if the documentation does not specify the primary site or the chief reason for the encounter.

---

### 258. Primary Malignancy Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the Primary Malignancy Coding Guidelines, specifically focusing on the rules for sequencing primary codes versus Z85 history codes based on treatment status.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `100_120`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Coding and sequencing of complications associated with the malignancies or with the therapy thereof are subject to the following guidelines:
>
> When a primary malignancy has been excised but further treatment, such as an additional surgery for the malignancy, radiation therapy or chemotherapy is directed to that site, the primary malignancy code should be used until treatment is completed.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site, there is no further treatment (of the malignancy) directed to that site, and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy.
>
> Codes from subcategories Z85.0 - Z85.85 should only be assigned for the former site of a primary malignancy, not the site of a secondary malignancy. Code Z85.89 may be assigned for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Primary Malignancy Coding and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use primary malignancy code until treatment is completed if excised but further therapy is directed to that site
- Use category Z85 code if malignancy is excised/eradicated with no further treatment and no evidence of existing malignancy
- Assign Z85.0-Z85.85 codes only for the former site of a primary malignancy, not for secondary malignancy sites
- Assign Z85.89 for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy
- Reference Section I.C.21 for factors influencing health status and contact with health services

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm whether further treatment is directed to the site of the excised malignancy
- Documentation must confirm there is no evidence of existing primary malignancy at the site
- Documentation must distinguish between former sites of primary malignancy versus sites of secondary malignancy
- Clinical records must support the status of the malignancy (active, excised, or eradicated)
- Coding must align with Section I.C.21 guidelines regarding health status factors

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using Z85.0-Z85.85 codes for the site of a secondary malignancy
- Assigning primary malignancy codes after the malignancy is eradicated with no further treatment
- Failing to query documentation regarding the status of excised malignancies
- Misinterpreting Z85.89 as exclusive to primary malignancy history
- Ignoring Section I.C.21 factors when determining health status codes

**New in this version:**
- Explicit distinction between Z85.0-Z85.85 and Z85.89 based on malignancy type (primary vs secondary)
- Mandatory use of Z85 codes when malignancy is eradicated with no further treatment directed to the site
- Requirement to reference Section I.C.21 for health status and contact with health services documentation
- Clarification that Z85 codes indicate the former site of malignancy, not current active sites
- Updated sequencing rules for complications associated with malignancies or their therapy

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `529_534_547`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Coding and sequencing of complications associated with the malignancies or with the therapy thereof are subject to the following guidelines:
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site and there is no further treatment directed to that site and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy. Any mention of extension, invasion, or metastasis to another site is coded as a secondary malignant neoplasm to that site. The secondary site may be the principal or first-listed diagnosis with the Z85 code used as a secondary code.
>
> See section I.C.2.t. Secondary malignant neoplasm of lymphoid tissue .
>
> - e. Admissions/Encounters involving antineoplastic chemotherapy, immunotherapy and radiation therapy
> - 1) Episode of care involves surgical removal of neoplasm
>
> When an episode of care involves the surgical removal of a neoplasm, primary or secondary site, followed by adjunct chemotherapy or radiation treatment during the same episode of care, the code for the neoplasm should be assigned as principal or first-listed diagnosis .
>
> - 2) Patient admission/encounter chiefly for administration of antineoplastic chemotherapy, immunotherapy and radiation therapy
>
> If a patient admission/encounter is chiefly for the administration of chemotherapy, immunotherapy or external beam radiation therapy for the treatment of a neoplasm , assign code Z51.0, Encounter for antineoplastic radiation therapy, or Z51.11, Encounter for antineoplastic chemotherapy, or Z51.12, Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy as the first-listed or principal diagnosis. If the reason for the encounter is more than one type of antineoplastic therapy, code Z51.0 and codes from subcategory Z51.1 may be assigned together, in which case one of these codes would be reported as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> The malignancy for which the therapy is being administered should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> If a patient admission/encounter is for the insertion or implantation of radioactive elements (e.g., brachytherapy) the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. Code Z51.0 should not be assigned.
>
> - 3) Patient admitted for radiation therapy, chemotherapy or immunotherapy and develops complications
>
> When a patient is admitted for the purpose of external beam radiotherapy, immunotherapy or chemotherapy and develops complications such as uncontrolled nausea and vomiting or dehydration, the principal or first-listed diagnosis is Z51.0, Encounter for antineoplastic radiation therapy, or Z51.11, Encounter for antineoplastic chemotherapy, or Z51.12, Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy followed by any codes for the complications.
>
> When a patient is admitted for the purpose of insertion or implantation of radioactive elements (e.g., brachytherapy) and develops complications such as uncontrolled nausea and vomiting or dehydration, the principal or first-listed diagnosis is the appropriate code for the malignancy followed by any codes for the complications.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been excised but further treatment, such as an additional surgery for the malignancy, radiation therapy or chemotherapy is directed to that site, the primary malignancy code should be used until treatment is completed.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site, there is no further treatment (of the malignancy) directed to that site, and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy.
>
> Codes from subcategories Z85.0 - Z85.85 should only be assigned for the former site of a primary malignancy, not the site of a secondary malignancy. Code Z85.89 may be assigned for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Primary Malignancy Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign Z85 Personal history codes when a primary malignancy is excised/eradicated with no further treatment or evidence of existing malignancy at that site.
- Code secondary malignant neoplasms to the affected site as the principal diagnosis with Z85 as a secondary code.
- Assign the neoplasm code as the principal diagnosis when surgical removal is followed by adjunct chemotherapy or radiation during the same episode.
- Sequence Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as the first-listed diagnosis for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy administration.
- Sequence the malignancy code as principal for brachytherapy encounters, explicitly excluding Z51.0 assignment.
- Assign the malignancy code as principal diagnosis when complications (e.g., nausea, vomiting) develop during an encounter chiefly for antineoplastic therapy, followed by codes for the complications.
- Assign the malignancy code as principal diagnosis for brachytherapy encounters when complications develop, followed by codes for the complications.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm no further treatment is directed to the site and no evidence of existing primary malignancy to use Z85.
- Encounter reason must be chiefly for antineoplastic therapy administration to justify Z51.x codes as principal diagnosis.
- Malignancy code must be assigned as secondary diagnosis when therapy administration is the primary reason for the encounter.
- Z85 codes must only be assigned for the former site of a primary malignancy, not the site of a secondary malignancy.
- Z85.89 may be assigned for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
- Documentation must specify that the encounter is chiefly for the administration of chemotherapy, immunotherapy, or external beam radiation therapy to justify Z51.x codes as the principal diagnosis.
- Documentation must confirm that no further treatment is directed to the site of a previously excised malignancy to validate the use of Z85 codes.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using Z51.0 for encounters involving insertion or implantation of radioactive elements (brachytherapy).
- Assigning Z85 codes for the site of a secondary malignancy instead of the former site of a primary malignancy.
- Coding the malignancy as secondary when the encounter is chiefly for therapy administration without specifying the therapy type.
- Using unspecified codes for malignancy sites when specific documentation of primary or secondary status is available.
- Failing to sequence complications as secondary diagnoses when the principal diagnosis is the therapy encounter code.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to sequence Z51.0 and Z51.1 codes together when multiple antineoplastic therapies are administered, with one reported as secondary.
- Clarification that Z85.89 is the specific code for the former site of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
- Requirement to assign the malignancy code as principal for brachytherapy encounters, distinguishing it from external beam radiation therapy.
- Guidance on sequencing complications (e.g., nausea, vomiting) as secondary diagnoses following the principal therapy encounter code.
- Mandate to use primary malignancy codes until treatment is completed if further treatment is directed to the site after excision.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version established the core rule to use primary codes until treatment is completed but lacked specific sequencing instructions for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy. The newer version clarifies this by explicitly mandating that Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 be sequenced as the first-listed diagnosis for such encounters, while assigning the malignancy code as secondary. Additionally, the newer version refines the distinction for brachytherapy, requiring the malignancy code as principal and explicitly excluding Z51.0 assignment, whereas the older version did not address brachytherapy sequencing nuances.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Mandates use of primary malignancy code until treatment is completed if excised but further therapy is directed to that site. -> icd (New Version): Mandates use of primary malignancy code until treatment is completed if excised but further therapy is directed to that site; additionally, explicitly sequences Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as first-listed for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): General rule for coding complications associated with malignancies. -> icd (New Version): Explicitly assigns malignancy code as principal for brachytherapy encounters when complications develop, excluding Z51.0 assignment, and sequences complications as secondary diagnoses following the principal therapy encounter code.

*Traceability: Chunk `100_120` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `529_534_547` (icd (New Version))*

### 259. Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical sequencing rules for neoplasm and anemia encounters with verbatim matching text.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `101_117`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by the appropriate code for the anemia (such as code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease ).
>
> When an encounter is for management of a complication associated with a neoplasm, such as dehydration, and the treatment is only for the complication, the complication is coded first, followed by the appropriate code(s) for the neoplasm.
>
> The exception to this guideline is anemia. When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy and treatment is only for anemia, sequence malignancy code first followed by D63.0.
- When admission is for management of a complication associated with neoplasm (e.g., dehydration) and treatment is only for the complication, sequence complication code first followed by neoplasm code(s).
- Anemia associated with malignancy is treated as an exception to the general complication sequencing rule, requiring malignancy as the principal diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Treatment must be limited to anemia management for the malignancy-to-be-first rule to apply.
- Treatment must be limited to the complication management for the complication-to-be-first rule to apply.
- Clinical documentation must explicitly link the anemia to the neoplastic disease.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Sequencing anemia as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is for management of anemia associated with malignancy.
- Sequencing the neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is for management of a complication associated with the neoplasm.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of code D63.0 (Anemia in neoplastic disease) when coding anemia associated with malignancy.
- Explicit exception rule for anemia overriding standard complication sequencing guidelines.

---

>
> When an encounter is for management of a complication associated with a neoplasm, such as dehydration, and the treatment is only for the complication, the complication is coded first, followed by the appropriate code(s) for the neoplasm.
>
> The exception to this guideline is anemia. When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy and treatment is only for anemia, sequence malignancy as principal diagnosis followed by anemia code D63.0.
- When admission is for management of a complication associated with neoplasm (e.g., dehydration) and treatment is only for the complication, sequence the complication first followed by the neoplasm code.
- Anemia associated with malignancy is treated as an exception to the general complication sequencing rule, requiring the malignancy to be sequenced first.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Treatment must be limited to anemia management for the malignancy-to-be-first rule to apply.
- Treatment must be limited to the complication management for the complication-to-be-first rule to apply.
- Clinical documentation must explicitly link the anemia to the neoplastic disease.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Sequencing the anemia code as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is for anemia associated with malignancy.
- Sequencing the neoplasm code as the principal diagnosis when the encounter is for a complication like dehydration.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory use of code D63.0 for anemia in neoplastic disease when sequencing malignancy first.
- Explicit exception rule for anemia overriding standard complication sequencing guidelines.

---

The older and newer versions contain identical behavioral rules, requirements, and raw text excerpts regarding the sequencing of malignancy and anemia codes. There are no clinical logic changes, guideline reversals, or definition expansions between the two versions provided. The only distinction is the metadata label identifying the source as 'icd (New Version)' versus the specific FY25 guideline date, indicating a static guideline maintained across versions.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Establishes sequencing rules where the malignancy is the principal diagnosis followed by the anemia code when treatment is limited to the anemia. -> icd (New Version): Establishes sequencing rules where malignancy treatment for anemia requires the malignancy as the principal diagnosis followed by the specific anemia code.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt explicitly states the exception for anemia requiring malignancy first. -> icd (New Version): Raw text excerpt explicitly states the exception for anemia requiring malignancy first.

> **Actual Text**

**Feature:** Tumor Coding Clarification Protocol

- Coder must query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding the status of multiple tumors in the same organ.
- Multiple malignant tumors in the same organ may represent different primaries or metastatic disease depending on the site.
- Correct ICD-10-CM codes must be assigned based on the clarified status of each tumor.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must clearly define the status of each tumor to distinguish between primary and metastatic disease.
- Provider query is mandatory when the clinical record does not specify the relationship between multiple tumors in the same organ.

### 263. Extranodal Lymphoma Coding Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `127`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a malignant neoplasm of lymphoid tissue metastasizes beyond the lymph nodes, a code from categories C81-C85 with a final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites' should be assigned rather than a code for the secondary neoplasm of the affected solid organ. For example, for metastasis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma to the lung, brain and left adrenal gland, assign code C83.39 8 , Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of other extranodal and solid organ sites.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Extranodal Lymphoma Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from categories C81-C85 with a final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites' when malignant neoplasm metastasizes beyond lymph nodes.
- Do not assign a code for the secondary neoplasm of the affected solid organ in cases of extranodal metastasis.
- Use specific codes (e.g., C83.39) to identify the specific lymphoma type and extranodal location.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the neoplasm is malignant and of lymphoid tissue.
- Documentation must confirm metastasis has occurred beyond the lymph nodes to extranodal or solid organ sites.
- The specific type of lymphoma must be identified to select the correct category code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning a code for the secondary neoplasm of the affected solid organ when the primary diagnosis is extranodal lymphoma metastasis.

**New in this version:**
- Requirement to use the final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites' for all malignant neoplasms of lymphoid tissue metastasizing beyond lymph nodes.

For example, for metastasis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma to the lung, brain and left adrenal gland, assign code C83.398, Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of other extranodal and solid organ sites.

### 264. Secondary Neoplasm Principal Diagnosis Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical logic for assigning a secondary neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when treatment is directed solely at the metastatic site.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `99`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted because of a primary neoplasm with metastasis and treatment is directed toward the secondary site only, the secondary neoplasm is designated as the principal diagnosis even though the primary malignancy is still present.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Secondary Neoplasm Principal Diagnosis Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Designate the secondary neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when treatment is directed toward the secondary site only.
- Apply this logic even if the primary malignancy remains present in the patient.
- Prioritize the secondary site over the primary site for principal diagnosis selection based on treatment focus.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient was admitted because of the primary neoplasm with metastasis.
- Clinical records must explicitly state that treatment is directed toward the secondary site only.
- The primary malignancy must be documented as still present in the patient.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning the primary neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when treatment is focused solely on the secondary site.
- Ignoring the presence of the primary malignancy when the secondary site is the sole treatment target.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit rule allowing secondary neoplasm to supersede primary neoplasm for principal diagnosis based on treatment direction.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `528`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is admitted because of a primary neoplasm with metastasis and treatment is directed toward the secondary site only, the secondary neoplasm is designated as the principal diagnosis even though the primary malignancy is still present.

**Extracted Profile:**

**Key Behaviors:**
- Designate the secondary neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when treatment is directed toward the secondary site only.
- Apply this logic even if the primary malignancy remains present in the patient.
- Prioritize the secondary site over the primary site for principal diagnosis assignment based on treatment focus.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must confirm the patient was admitted because of the primary neoplasm with metastasis.
- Clinical records must explicitly state that treatment is directed toward the secondary site only.
- The presence of the primary malignancy must be acknowledged but not the focus of the current treatment plan.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning the primary neoplasm as the principal diagnosis when the secondary site is the sole focus of treatment.
- Ignoring the treatment direction when determining the principal diagnosis for metastatic cases.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit rule allowing secondary neoplasm to supersede primary neoplasm for principal diagnosis based on treatment intent.

Consequently, the core rule remains unchanged: if a patient is admitted for treatment of a metastatic site only, the secondary neoplasm is the principal diagnosis regardless of the primary's presence.

- The primary malignancy or appropriate metastatic site is designated as the principal diagnosis even though chemotherapy or radiotherapy is administered.

- The reason for admission or encounter must be for a procedure such as paracentesis or thoracentesis.
- The primary malignancy or appropriate metastatic site must be designated as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.

### 270. Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature 'Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding' with matching behaviors and deprecated items.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `399_413`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In the outpatient setting, the term first-listed diagnosis is used in lieu of principal diagnosis.
>
> In determining the first-listed diagnosis the coding conventions of ICD-10-CM, as well as the general and disease specific guidelines take precedence over the outpatient guidelines.
>
> Diagnoses often are not established at the time of the initial encounter/visit. It may take two or more visits before the diagnosis is confirmed.
>
> The most critical rule involves beginning the search for the correct code assignment through the Alphabetic Index. Never begin searching initially in the Tabular List as this will lead to coding errors.
>
> For patients receiving diagnostic services only during an encounter/visit, sequence first the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter/visit. Codes for other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) may be sequenced as additional diagnoses.
>
> For encounters for routine laboratory/radiology testing in the absence of any signs, symptoms, or associated diagnosis, assign Z01.89, Encounter for other specified special examinations. If routine testing is performed during the same encounter as a test to evaluate a sign, symptom, or diagnosis, it is appropriate to assign both the Z code and the code describing the reason for the non-routine test.
>
> For outpatient encounters for diagnostic tests that have been interpreted by a physician, and the final report is available at the time of coding, code any confirmed or definitive diagnosis(es) documented in the interpretation. Do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practice in the hospital inpatient setting regarding abnormal findings on test results.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- In the outpatient setting, the term first-listed diagnosis is used in lieu of principal diagnosis.
- The coding conventions of ICD-10-CM and general/disease-specific guidelines take precedence over outpatient guidelines.
- The search for the correct code assignment must begin through the Alphabetic Index, never the Tabular List initially.
- For diagnostic services only, sequence the diagnosis chiefly responsible for services provided during the encounter first.
- For interpreted diagnostic tests with available final reports, code confirmed or definitive diagnoses and do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Diagnoses may not be established at the time of the initial encounter and may require two or more visits for confirmation.
- Codes for other diagnoses, such as chronic conditions, may be sequenced as additional diagnoses after the first-listed.
- Routine laboratory/radiology testing without signs, symptoms, or associated diagnosis requires assignment of Z01.89.
- If routine testing occurs during the same encounter as a non-routine test evaluating a sign, symptom, or diagnosis, both the Z code and the reason code must be assigned.
- Final reports for diagnostic tests must be available at the time of coding to assign confirmed or definitive diagnoses.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Beginning the search for code assignment in the Tabular List initially.
- Coding related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses when a confirmed or definitive diagnosis is documented in an interpreted test report.
- Using the term 'principal diagnosis' in the outpatient setting.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to use 'first-listed diagnosis' terminology instead of 'principal diagnosis' for outpatient claims.
- Mandatory sequencing of Z01.89 for routine laboratory/radiology testing in the absence of other clinical indicators.
- Requirement to assign both Z codes and specific reason codes when routine and non-routine testing occur in the same encounter.

---

### 271. Kidney Transplant Complication Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the assignment of T86.1 codes for kidney transplant complications versus uncomplicated CKD.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `314_349`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease (CKD) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. Code T86.1should be assigned for documented complications of a kidney transplant, such as transplant failure or rejection or other transplant complication. Code T86.1- should not be assigned for post kidney transplant patients who have chronic kidney (CKD) unless a transplant complication such as transplant failure or rejection is documented. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.
>
> Conditions that affect the function of the transplanted kidney, other than CKD, should be assigned a code from subcategory T86.1, Complications of transplanted organ, Kidney, and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> For patients with CKD following a kidney transplant, but who do not have a complication such as failure or rejection, see section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease and kidney transplant status .
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ
>
> See section I.C.19. for information on the coding of organ transplant complications.
>
> - Z99
>
> Dependence on enabling machines and devices, not elsewhere classified
>
> Note: Categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 are for use only if there are no complications or malfunctions of the organ or tissue replaced, the amputation site or the equipment on which the patient is dependent.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Kidney Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code T86.1 for documented complications of a kidney transplant such as transplant failure or rejection.
- Do not assign T86.1- for post-transplant patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) unless a specific transplant complication is documented.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding whether the patient has a transplant complication.
- Assign a code from subcategory T86.1 for conditions affecting transplanted kidney function other than CKD, paired with a secondary complication code.
- Use categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 only if there are no complications or malfunctions of the organ or equipment.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state a transplant complication (e.g., failure, rejection) to justify the use of code T86.1.
- Chronic kidney disease (CKD) alone following a transplant does not require code T86.1 without a documented complication.
- A secondary code identifying the specific complication must be assigned alongside the T86.1 code for non-CKD conditions.
- Provider clarification is mandatory when the documentation is ambiguous regarding the presence of a transplant complication.
- Categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 are conditional and require the absence of organ or equipment complications.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code T86.1- solely for post-transplant chronic kidney disease without documented failure or rejection.
- Coding transplant complications without verifying that they are not integral to the disease process or expected outcomes.
- Using Z-dependence categories when complications or malfunctions of the transplanted organ are present.
- Failing to query the provider when the distinction between CKD and transplant complication is unclear in the record.
- Omitting the secondary complication code when assigning a T86.1 code for specific functional conditions.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to query providers for unclear documentation regarding transplant complications.
- Clarification that CKD following transplant requires separate coding unless linked to a documented complication like rejection.
- Specific reference to subcategory T86.1 for non-CKD conditions affecting transplanted kidney function.
- Guidance on sequencing sepsis due to infection in transplanted organs per section I.C.1.d.4.
- Updated note on the restricted use of Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 categories in the presence of organ complications.
- Sequencing of sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ is governed by section I.C.1.d.4.
- Coding of organ transplant complications generally follows the information provided in section I.C.19.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `742`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease (CKD) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. Code T86.1should be assigned for documented complications of a kidney transplant, such as transplant failure or rejection or other transplant complication. Code T86.1- should not be assigned for post kidney transplant patients who have chronic kidney (CKD) unless a transplant complication such as transplant failure or rejection is documented. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.
>
> Conditions that affect the function of the transplanted kidney, other than CKD, should be assigned a code from subcategory T86.1, Complications of transplanted organ, Kidney, and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> For patients with CKD following a kidney transplant, but who do not have a complication such as failure or rejection, see section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease and kidney transplant status .
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Kidney Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code T86.1 should be assigned for documented complications of a kidney transplant, such as transplant failure or rejection.
- Code T86.1- should not be assigned for post kidney transplant patients who have chronic kidney disease (CKD) unless a transplant complication is documented.
- If documentation is unclear regarding a transplant complication, the provider must be queried for clarification.
- Conditions affecting transplanted kidney function other than CKD require a code from subcategory T86.1 and a secondary code identifying the complication.
- Sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ requires specific sequencing per section I.C.1.d.4.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state a transplant complication (e.g., failure, rejection) to justify assigning code T86.1.
- Chronic kidney disease (CKD) alone in a post-transplant patient is insufficient to assign code T86.1 without a documented complication.
- A secondary code must be assigned to identify the specific complication when using subcategory T86.1.
- Provider queries are mandatory when clinical documentation is ambiguous regarding the presence of a transplant complication.
- Sepsis coding in transplanted organs must follow the specific sequencing rules outlined in section I.C.1.d.4.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code T86.1- solely based on the presence of chronic kidney disease (CKD) in a post-transplant patient without documented failure or rejection.
- Coding transplant complications without verifying that the condition is not an integral part of the underlying disease process.
- Failing to query the provider when the distinction between CKD and a transplant complication is unclear in the documentation.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of a secondary code to identify the specific complication when using subcategory T86.1 for conditions other than CKD.
- Explicit reference to section I.C.1.d.4 for the sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organs.
- Clarification that CKD following a transplant without complications should be coded according to section I.C.14 rather than T86.1.
- Reference to section I.C.14 for coding CKD following a kidney transplant when no complication like failure or rejection is present.

---

The primary difference is structural: the newer version explicitly references section I.C.14 for coding uncomplicated CKD, whereas the older version mentions this section only in a raw text excerpt without integrating it into the main behavioral list. No clinical logic or code assignment rules have changed.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions section I.C.14 only in a raw text excerpt regarding CKD coding -> icd (New Version): Explicitly integrates the reference to section I.C.14 into the main behavioral list for coding uncomplicated CKD.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Guidance on sequencing sepsis' as a new item -> icd (New Version): Incorporates sepsis sequencing rules directly into the core behaviors list.

### 272. HIV Medication Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles address the core logic for assigning HIV disease codes (B20) and medication status codes (Z79.899) to patients on antiretroviral therapy.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `60`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS is currently managed on antiretroviral medications, assign code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease. Code Z79.899, Other long term (current) drug therapy, may be assigned as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Medication Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS currently managed on antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code Z79.899 as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Patient must have documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS.
- Patient must be currently managed on antiretroviral medications.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `487_488`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients with documentation of inconclusive HIV serology, may be assigned code R75 human immunodeficiency virus [HIV].
>
> - Inconclusive HIV serology , Inconclusive laboratory evidence of
> - (g) Previously diagnosed HIV-related illness Patients with documentation of a prior diagnosis of an HIV-related illness should be coded to B20. Once an HIV-related illness has developed, code B20 should always be assigned on every subsequent admission/encounter. Patients previously diagnosed with any HIV illness (B20) should never be assigned to R75, Inconclusive laboratory evidence of human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] or Z21, Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] infection status.
> - (h) HIV Infection in Pregnancy, Childbirth and the Puerperium When a patient presents during pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium with documented symptomatic HIV disease or an HIV related illness, assign a code from subcategory O98.7, Human immunodeficiency [HIV] disease complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, followed by code B20 and additional code(s) for any HIV-related illness(es). Codes from Chapter 15 always take sequencing priority.
>
> When a patient presents during pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium with documented asymptomatic HIV infection status or is HIV-positive, assign a code from subcategory O98.7 followed by code Z21.
>
> - (i)
> - Encounters for HIV testing Use additional codes for any
>
> If a patient without signs or symptoms is tested for HIV, assign code Z11.4, Encounter for screening for human immunodeficiency virus [HIV]. associated high-risk behavior , if applicable.
>
> If a patient with signs or symptoms of HIV presents for HIV testing, code the signs and symptoms. An additional counseling code Z71.7, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] counseling, may be assigned if counseling is provided during the encounter for the test. Code Z11.4, Encounter for screening for human immunodeficiency virus [HIV], should not be assigned if HIV signs or symptoms are present.
>
> When a patient presents for follow up regarding their HIV test results and the test result is negative, assign code Z71.7, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] counseling.
>
> If the results are positive, see previous guidelines and assign codes as appropriate.
>
> If a patient with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS is currently managed on antiretroviral medications, assign code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease.
>
> If a patient with documented HIV positive status is currently managed on antiretroviral medication, assign code Z21, Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] infection status, in the absence of any additional documentation of HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS.
>
> Code Z79.899, Other long term (current) drug therapy, may be assigned as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Medication and Status Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS currently managed on antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code Z21 for patients with documented HIV positive status managed on antiretroviral medication only if there is no additional documentation of HIV disease, illness, or AIDS.
- Assign code Z11.4 for encounters where a patient without signs or symptoms is tested for HIV, with additional codes for associated high-risk behavior if applicable.
- Assign code Z71.7 for encounters regarding HIV test results (negative or counseling) and do not assign Z11.4 if HIV signs or symptoms are present.
- Assign code Z79.899 as an additional code to identify the long-term use of antiretroviral medications.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Code B20 must be assigned on every subsequent admission/encounter once an HIV-related illness has developed.
- Patients previously diagnosed with any HIV illness (B20) must never be assigned to R75 or Z21.
- Codes from Chapter 15 (Pregnancy, Childbirth, and the Puerperium) always take sequencing priority when applicable.
- Code Z11.4 should not be assigned if HIV signs or symptoms are present during the encounter.
- Code Z79.899 may be assigned as an additional code to identify long-term use of antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code R75 for patients with documentation of inconclusive HIV serology.
- Assign code B20 for patients with a prior diagnosis of an HIV-related illness on every subsequent admission/encounter.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Assigning code R75 to patients with a prior diagnosis of an HIV-related illness.
- Assigning code Z21 to patients previously diagnosed with any HIV illness (B20).
- Assigning code Z11.4 for encounters where HIV signs or symptoms are present.
- Failing to assign code B20 on subsequent encounters for patients with documented HIV-related illness.
- Omitting code Z79.899 when identifying long-term antiretroviral medication use.
- Assigning code Z21 to patients with documented asymptomatic HIV infection status or HIV-positive status when additional documentation of HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS is present.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit instruction to assign code Z79.899 as an additional code for long-term antiretroviral medication use.
- Clarification that code Z71.7 may be assigned for HIV counseling during testing encounters.
- Requirement to code signs and symptoms separately if a patient presents for HIV testing with symptoms.
- Specific guidance for coding HIV infection in pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium with subcategory O98.7.
- Mandatory sequencing priority for Chapter 15 codes over other HIV-related codes in pregnancy contexts.
- Mandatory sequencing priority for Chapter 15 codes (O98.7) over other HIV-related codes in pregnancy contexts.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Guideline Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided a basic rule to assign B20 and Z79.899 for patients on antiretrovirals. The newer version significantly expands this by explicitly prohibiting the assignment of Z21 (asymptomatic status) or R75 (inconclusive serology) for patients with a prior HIV-related illness diagnosis, ensuring B20 is used exclusively in those cases. It also introduces specific sequencing rules for pregnancy contexts and clarifies the use of Z79.899 as a mandatory additional code for long-term therapy identification.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease managed on antiretroviral medications; Z79.899 may be assigned as an additional code. -> icd (New Version): Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease managed on antiretroviral medications; explicitly prohibit assigning Z21 or R75 if a prior HIV-related illness (B20) exists, and mandate Z79.899 for long-term use.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): No specific sequencing rules for pregnancy contexts. -> icd (New Version): Codes from Chapter 15 (O98.7) always take sequencing priority over other HIV-related codes when a patient presents during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.

*Traceability: Chunk `60` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `487_488` (icd (New Version))*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `340`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes are for use in any healthcare setting. Z codes may be used as either a first-listed (principal diagnosis code in the inpatient setting) or secondary code, depending on the circumstances of the encounter. Certain Z codes may only be used as first-listed or principal diagnosis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z Code Usage Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes are applicable for use in any healthcare setting.
- Z codes may be assigned as a first-listed or principal diagnosis code in the inpatient setting.
- Z codes may be assigned as a secondary code depending on the circumstances of the encounter.
- Certain specific Z codes are restricted to use only as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- Selection of Z code position depends on the clinical circumstances of the encounter.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The code must be relevant to the circumstances of the healthcare encounter.
- Specific Z codes must be verified against restrictions regarding first-listed or principal diagnosis usage.

#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `770_805_806`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes are for use in any healthcare setting. Z codes may be used as either a first-listed (principal diagnosis code in the inpatient setting) or secondary code, depending on the circumstances of the encounter. Certain Z codes may only be used as first-listed or principal diagnosis.
>
> Certain Z codes are so non-specific, or potentially redundant with other codes in the classification, that there can be little justification for their use in the inpatient setting. Their use in the outpatient setting should be limited to those instances when there is no further documentation to permit more precise coding. Otherwise, any sign or symptom or any other reason for visit that is captured in another code should be used.
>
> Nonspecific Z codes/categories:
>
> {"Z02.9": "Z04.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason"}
> {"Z02.9": "Z13.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for screening, unspecified"}
> {"Z02.9": "Z41.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z Code Usage and Specificity Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes may be used as first-listed or secondary codes depending on the encounter circumstances.
- Certain Z codes are restricted to use only as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- Nonspecific Z codes should generally be avoided in the inpatient setting due to lack of justification.
- Outpatient use of nonspecific Z codes is limited to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding.
- Any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code should be used instead of a nonspecific Z code.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must support the use of a Z code as a first-listed diagnosis in the inpatient setting.
- More precise coding must be available before assigning a nonspecific Z code in the outpatient setting.
- If a sign or symptom is captured in another code, that code must be used instead of a Z code.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Using nonspecific Z codes in the inpatient setting when more specific codes are available.
- Assigning nonspecific Z codes in the outpatient setting when further documentation permits precise coding.
- Using Z codes for signs or symptoms that are already captured in other codes.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit restriction of certain Z codes to first-listed or principal diagnosis status only.
- Mandatory avoidance of nonspecific Z codes in favor of more precise codes when documentation allows.
- Specific code mappings for 'Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified' (Z02.9) to more precise codes such as Z13.9, Z04.9, and Z41.9.

### 274. CKD Post-Transplant Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the distinction between residual CKD (N18) and transplant complications (Z94.0) with identical behavioral rules.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `212`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease ( CKD ) because the kidney transplant may not
> fully restore kidney function. Therefore, the presence of CKD alone does not constitute a transplant complication. Assign the appropriate N18 code for the patient's stage of CKD and code Z94.0, Kidney transplant status. If a transplant complication such as failure or rejection or other transplant complication is documented, see section I.C.19.g for information on coding complications of a kidney transplant. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** CKD Post-Transplant Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate N18 code for the patient's stage of CKD and code Z94.0 for kidney transplant status.
- Do not code CKD alone as a transplant complication.
- If a transplant complication such as failure or rejection is documented, follow section I.C.19.g for coding complications.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding the presence of a transplant complication.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Documentation must explicitly state the stage of CKD to assign the correct N18 code.
- Code Z94.0 must be assigned to indicate kidney transplant status.
- Transplant complications must be clearly documented as failure, rejection, or other specific complications to be coded separately from CKD.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding CKD alone as a transplant complication without additional documentation of failure or rejection.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of Z94.0 alongside N18 codes for patients with CKD post-transplant.

---
#### icd (New Version) — Chunk `641`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease ( CKD ) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. Therefore, the presence of CKD alone does not constitute a transplant complication. Assign the appropriate N18 code for the patient's stage of CKD and code Z94.0, Kidney transplant status. If a transplant complication such as failure or rejection or other transplant complication is documented, see section I.C.19.g for information on coding complications of a kidney transplant. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** CKD Post-Transplant Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate N18 code for the patient's stage of CKD and code Z94.0 for kidney transplant status.
- Do not code CKD alone as a transplant complication.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding the presence of a transplant complication.
- Code documented transplant complications (e.g., failure, rejection) according to section I.C.19.g.
- Distinguish between residual CKD and active transplant complications based on clinical documentation.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly state the stage of CKD to assign the correct N18 code.
- Documentation must confirm the presence of a kidney transplant to assign Z94.0.
- Documentation must clearly identify specific transplant complications (e.g., rejection, failure) to trigger complication coding.
- Provider query is mandatory when the distinction between residual CKD and transplant complication is ambiguous.
- Coding of CKD as a complication is prohibited unless specific complication criteria are met.

**Deprecated in this version:**
- Coding CKD alone as a complication of the kidney transplant.
- Assigning transplant complication codes without explicit documentation of failure, rejection, or other specific complications.
- Failing to assign Z94.0 when a kidney transplant status is present.
- Assuming CKD resolves post-transplant without verifying current functional status.
- Coding unspecified complications without querying the provider for clarification.

**New in this version:**
- Mandatory assignment of Z94.0 (Kidney transplant status) alongside N18 codes for residual CKD.
- Explicit prohibition of coding CKD as a complication unless specific transplant failure or rejection is documented.
- Requirement to query providers for clarification when the nature of post-transplant renal issues is unclear.
- Reference to section I.C.19.g for specific coding of documented transplant complications.
- Differentiation between chronic kidney disease (N18) and acute transplant complications based on clinical documentation.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Administrative Update   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The two versions contain verbatim identical text excerpts and behavioral instructions regarding the assignment of N18 and Z94.0 codes. The 'New Version' profile includes additional metadata tags such as 'Deprecated' items and 'New items' that were not present in the 'Older' profile, but the core clinical logic and coding rules remain unchanged. This indicates an administrative update where the guideline content was preserved while the metadata structure was expanded.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Contains the core behavioral rule and raw text excerpt without explicit metadata tags. -> icd (New Version): Includes the same core rule and text but adds structured metadata tags like 'Deprecated' and 'New items' to highlight specific prohibitions and mandates.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Query the provider if documentation is unclear' as a standard behavior. -> icd (New Version): Reiterates the same query requirement but explicitly categorizes it under 'New items' and 'Requirements' to emphasize its mandatory nature in the updated profile.

*Traceability: Chunk `212` (ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `641` (icd (New Version))*

- Map Z28 to Z40 for encounters regarding prophylactic surgery

> **Actual Text**
>

> {"Z28": "Z40", "Immunization not carried out Except: Z28.3 - , Underimmunization status": "Encounter for prophylactic surgery"}
> {"Z28": "Z41", "Immunization not carried out Except: Z28.3 - , Underimmunization status": "Encounter for procedures for purposes other than remedying health state Except: Z41.9, Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified"}
> [... +6 more table rows omitted ...]
>
> {"Z76.3": "Z76.4", "Healthy person accompanying sick person": "Other boarder to healthcare facility"}
> {"Z76.3": "Z76.5", "Healthy person accompanying sick person": "Malingerer [conscious simulation]"}
> {"Z76.3": "Z91.1-", "Healthy person accompanying sick person": "Patient's noncompliance with medical treatment and regimen"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Feature:** Z28 Underimmunization Code Mapping and Related Encounter Mappings

- Map Z28 (Underimmunization status) to Z29 for encounters regarding other prophylactic measures.
- Map Z28 (Underimmunization status) to Z40 for encounters regarding prophylactic surgery.
- Map Z28 (Underimmunization status) to Z41 for encounters regarding procedures for purposes other than remedying health state.
- Map Z76.3 (Healthy person accompanying sick person) to Z76.4 for other boarder to healthcare facility.
- Map Z76.3 (Healthy person accompanying sick person) to Z91.1- for patient's noncompliance with medical treatment and regimen.
- Map Z28 (Underimmunization status) to Z53 for encounters regarding procedures not carried out.
- Map Z76.3 (Healthy person accompanying sick person) to Z76.5 for encounters regarding malingerers.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The encounter must be specifically for underimmunization status to utilize the Z28 code.
- The encounter must be for prophylactic surgery to utilize the Z40 code.
- The encounter must be for procedures not intended to remedy a health state to utilize the Z41 code.
- The patient must be a healthy person accompanying a sick person to utilize the Z76.3 code.
- The patient must be a boarder to a healthcare facility to utilize the Z76.4 code.
- The encounter must not be for Z28.3 (Underimmunization status) to utilize the Z28 code.
- The encounter must not be for Z41.9 (Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified) to utilize the Z41 code.

**New in this version:**
- Explicit mapping of Z28.3 (Underimmunization status) to Z29.
- Explicit mapping of Z28.3 (Underimmunization status) to Z40.
- Explicit mapping of Z28.3 (Underimmunization status) to Z41.
- Explicit mapping of Z76.3 to Z76.4 for boarder status.
- Explicit mapping of Z76.3 to Z91.1- for noncompliance.
- Explicit mapping of Z28 to Z53 for procedures not carried out.
- Explicit mapping of Z76.3 to Z76.5 for malingerers.

---

The older version focused strictly on mapping Z28 to Z29, Z40, and Z41 based on encounter specifics for underimmunization status. The newer version retains these rules but significantly expands the taxonomy by adding explicit mappings for Z76.3 (Healthy person accompanying sick person) to codes Z76.4, Z76.5, and Z91.1-. This change broadens the definition of related encounter mappings beyond just underimmunization to include boarder status and noncompliance scenarios linked to the healthy person accompanying a sick person.

**Key Differences:**
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Maps Z28 to Z29, Z40, and Z41 for underimmunization status -> icd (New Version): Maps Z28 to Z29, Z40, and Z41 for underimmunization status AND adds mappings for Z76.3 to Z76.4, Z76.5, and Z91.1-.
- ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on Z28.3 exclusion for general Z28 mappings -> icd (New Version): Maintains Z28.3 exclusion while adding specific requirements for Z76.3 usage in boarder and noncompliance contexts.

### 276. Z13 Screening Encounter Classification
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical feature 'Z13 Screening Encounter Classification' with verbatim identical behaviors and requirements.*

#### ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `353_354`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Screening is the testing for disease or disease precursors in seemingly well individuals so that early detection and treatment can be provided for those who test positive for the disease (e.g., screening mammogram).
>
> The testing of a person to rule out or confirm a suspected diagnosis because the patient has some sign or symptom is a diagnostic examination, not a screening. In these cases, the sign or symptom is used to explain the reason for the test.
>
> A screening code may be a first-listed code if the reason for the visit is specifically the screening exam. It may also be used as an additional code if the screening is done during an office visit for other health problems. A screening code is not necessary if the screening is inherent to a routine examination, such as a pap smear done during a routine pelvic examination.
>
> Should a condition be discovered during the screening then the code for the condition may be assigned as an additional diagnosis.
>
> The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned. A procedure code is required to confirm that the screening was performed.
>
> The screening Z codes/categories:
>
> {"Z11": "Z12", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasms"}
> {"Z11": "Z13", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for screening for other diseases and disorders Except: Z13.9, Encounter for screening, unspecified"}
> {"Z11": "Z36", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for antenatal screening for mother"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z13 Screening Encounter Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Screening is defined as testing for disease in seemingly well individuals for early detection, distinct from diagnostic examinations for suspected diagnoses.
- A screening code may be a first-listed code if the visit reason is specifically the screening exam.
- A screening code may be used as an additional code if screening is performed during an office visit for other health problems.
- A screening code is not necessary if the screening is inherent to a routine examination, such as a pap smear during a pelvic exam.
- If a condition is discovered during screening, the code for the condition may be assigned as an additional diagnosis.

**Requirements / Properties:**
- The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned.
- A procedure code is required to confirm that the screening was performed.
- The sign or symptom must not be used to explain the reason for the test if it is a diagnostic examination.

---
